#### #404. Sum of Left Leaves

[leetcode.com/problems/sum-of-left-leaves/](https://leetcode.com/problems/sum-of-left-leaves/) · `O(n) / O(h)`

**Q:** Find the sum of all left leaves in a binary tree.

**Example:** `root = [3,9,20,null,null,15,7]` → `24`

**Algorithm:**
1. Base case: return default value when node is null.
2. Recurse into left subtree to get left result.
3. Recurse into right subtree to get right result.
4. Combine left and right results at current node and return.
`O(n) / O(h)`

```java
class Solution {
    public int sumOfLeftLeaves(TreeNode root) {
        if(root == null) {
            return 0;
        }
        if(root.left != null && (root.left.left == null && root.left.right == null)) {
            return root.left.val + sumOfLeftLeaves(root.right);
        } else {
            return sumOfLeftLeaves(root.left) + sumOfLeftLeaves(root.right);
        }
    }
}
```

- Iterative DFS with explicit stack avoids O(h) call stack overhead.
- Morris traversal achieves O(1) space inorder traversal without stack or recursion.

#### #426. Convert Binary Search Tree to Sorted Doubly Linked List

[leetcode.com/problems/convert-binary-search-tree-to-sorted-doubly-linked-list/](https://leetcode.com/problems/convert-binary-search-tree-to-sorted-doubly-linked-list/) · `O(n) / O(h)`

**Q:** Convert a BST to a sorted circular doubly linked list in-place.

**Example:** `root = [4,2,5,1,3]` → `[1,2,3,4,5] (DLL)`

**Algorithm:**
1. Base case: return default value when node is null.
2. Recurse into left subtree to get left result.
3. Recurse into right subtree to get right result.
4. Combine left and right results at current node and return.
`O(n) / O(h)`

```java
class Solution {
    Node first;
    Node previous;
    public Node treeToDoublyList(Node root) {
        if(root == null) {
            return root;
        }
        dfs(root);
        first.left = previous;
        previous.right = first;
        return first;
    }
    public void dfs(Node root) {
        if(root == null) {
            return ;
        }
        dfs(root.left);
        if(previous != null) {
            previous.right = root;
            root.left = previous;
        } else {
            first = root;
        }
        previous = root;
        dfs(root.right);
    }
}
class Solution {
    public Node treeToDoublyList(Node root) {
        if(root == null) {
            return root;
        }
        Node head = null;
        Node previous = null;
        Node current = root;
        Deque<Node> stack = new ArrayDeque<>();
        while(current != null || !stack.isEmpty()) {
            while(current != null) {
                stack.push(current);
                current = current.left;
            }
            current = stack.pop();
            if(previous == null) {
                head = current;
            } else {
                previous.right = current;
                current.left = previous;
            }
            previous = current;
            current = current.right;
        }
        previous.right = head;
        head.left = previous;
        return head;
    }
}
```

- Iterative DFS with explicit stack avoids O(h) call stack overhead.
- Morris traversal achieves O(1) space inorder traversal without stack or recursion.

---

#### #429. N-ary Tree Level Order Traversal

[leetcode.com/problems/nary-tree-level-order-traversal/](https://leetcode.com/problems/nary-tree-level-order-traversal/) · `O(n) / O(h)`

**Example:** `root = [1,[3,5,6],2,4]` → `[[1],[3,2,4],[5,6]]`

**Algorithm:**
1. Init queue with root node.
2. Process level by level: snapshot queue.size() at start of each level.
3. Poll node, process value, enqueue non-null children.
4. Collect/aggregate results per level or across all nodes.
`O(n) / O(h)`

```java
class Solution {
    public List<List<Integer>> levelOrder(Node root) {
        if(root == null) {
            return new ArrayList<>();
        }
        List<List<Integer>> levelOrder = new ArrayList<>();
        Deque<Node> queue = new ArrayDeque<>();
        queue.offer(root);
        for(int level = 1; !queue.isEmpty(); level++) {
            List<Integer> list = new ArrayList<>();
            for(int i = queue.size(); i > 0; i--) {
                Node current = queue.poll();
                list.add(current.val);
                for(Node child : current.children) {
                    queue.offer(child);
                }
            }
            levelOrder.add(list);
        }
        return levelOrder;
    }
}
```

- Iterative DFS with explicit stack avoids O(h) call stack overhead.
- Morris traversal achieves O(1) space inorder traversal without stack or recursion.

#### #437. Path Sum III

[leetcode.com/problems/path-sum-iii/](https://leetcode.com/problems/path-sum-iii/) · `O(n) / O(h)`

**Q:** Count paths in a binary tree that sum to a given value (path goes downward, any node to any node).

**Example:** `root = [10,5,-3,3,2,null,11,3,-2,null,1], targetSum = 8` → `3`

**Algorithm:**
1. Base case: return default value when node is null.
2. Recurse into left subtree to get left result.
3. Recurse into right subtree to get right result.
4. Combine left and right results at current node and return.
`O(n) / O(h)`

```java
class Solution {
    public int pathSum(TreeNode root, int targetSum) {
        if(root == null) {
            return 0;
        }
        Map<Long, Integer> prefixMap = new HashMap<>();
        prefixMap.put(0L, 1);
        return dfs(root, prefixMap, 0, targetSum);
    }
    public int dfs(TreeNode root, Map<Long, Integer> prefixMap, long currentSum, int targetSum) {
        if(root == null) {
            return 0;
        }
        currentSum += root.val;
        int count = prefixMap.getOrDefault(currentSum - targetSum, 0);
        prefixMap.put(currentSum, prefixMap.getOrDefault(currentSum, 0) + 1);
        count += dfs(root.left, prefixMap, currentSum, targetSum);
        count += dfs(root.right, prefixMap, currentSum, targetSum);
        prefixMap.put(currentSum, prefixMap.getOrDefault(currentSum, 0) - 1);
        return count;
    }
}
```

- Return early on first HashMap hit rather than scanning full input.

---

#### #440. K-th Smallest in Lexicographical Order

[leetcode.com/problems/kth-smallest-in-lexicographical-order/](https://leetcode.com/problems/kth-smallest-in-lexicographical-order/) · `O(n) / O(h)`

**Example:** `n = 13, k = 2` → `10`

**Algorithm:**
1. Base case: return default value when node is null.
2. Recurse into left subtree to get left result.
3. Recurse into right subtree to get right result.
4. Combine left and right results at current node and return.
`O(n) / O(h)`

```java
class Solution {
    public int findKthNumber(int n, int k) {
        int current = 1;
        k = k - 1;//0
        while(k > 0){
            int num = getNode(n,current,current+1);
            if(num <= k){//kcur
                current += 1;//current
                k -= num;
            }else{//
                current *= 10;//current
                k -= 1;//
            }
        }
        return current;
    }
    public int getNode(int n, long first, long last){
        int num = 0;
        while(first <= n){
            num += Math.min(n+1,last) - first;//n19519510096
            first *= 10;
            last *= 10;
        }
        return num;
    }
}
class Solution {
    public int findKthNumber(int n, int k) {
        long current = 1;
        while(k>1){
            long count = count(n,current);
            if(k <= count){
                current*=10;
                k-=1;
            }else{
                current+=1;
                k-=count;
            }
        }
        return (int)current;
    }
    public long count(int n,long current){
        long next = current+1,count = 0;
        while(current<=n){
            count += Math.min(n-current+1,next-current);
            current*=10;
            next*=10;
        }
        return count;
    }
}
```

- Iterative DFS with explicit stack avoids O(h) call stack overhead.
- Morris traversal achieves O(1) space inorder traversal without stack or recursion.

---

#### #450. Delete Node in a BST

[leetcode.com/problems/delete-node-in-a-bst/](https://leetcode.com/problems/delete-node-in-a-bst/) · `O(n) / O(h)`

**Q:** Delete a given key from a BST and return the updated root.

**Example:** `root = [5,3,6,2,4,null,7], key = 3` → `[5,4,6,2,null,null,7]`

**Algorithm:**
1. Base case: return default value when node is null.
2. Recurse into left subtree to get left result.
3. Recurse into right subtree to get right result.
4. Combine left and right results at current node and return.
`O(n) / O(h)`

```java
class Solution {
    private TreeNode findPredecessor(TreeNode root) {
        if(root == null || root.left == null) {
            return null;
        }
        root = root.left;
        while(root.right != null) {
            root = root.right;
        }
        return root;
    }
    private TreeNode findSucessor(TreeNode root) {
        if(root == null || root.rigth == null) {
            return null;
        }
        root = root.right;
        while(root.left != null) {
            root = root.left;
        }
        return root;
    }
    public TreeNode deleteNode(TreeNode root, int key) {
        if(root == null) {
            return root;
        }
        root.left = deleteNode(root.left, key);
        root.right = deleteNode(root.right, key);
        if(root.val != key) {
            return root;
        }
        if(root.left == null && root.right == null) {
            return null;
        } else if(root.left != null) {
            TreeNode predecessor = findPredecessor(root);
            root.val = predecessor.val;
            predecessor.val = key;
            root.left = deleteNode(root.left, key);
        } else {
            TreeNode sucessor = findSucessor(root);
            root.val = sucessor.val;
            sucessor.val = key;
            root.right = deleteNode(root.right, key);
        }
        return root;
    }
}
```

- Iterative DFS with explicit stack avoids O(h) call stack overhead.
- Morris traversal achieves O(1) space inorder traversal without stack or recursion.

---

#### #501. Find Mode in Binary Search Tree

[leetcode.com/problems/find-mode-in-binary-search-tree/](https://leetcode.com/problems/find-mode-in-binary-search-tree/) · `O(n) / O(h)`

**Q:** Find the mode(s) in a BST (values that appear most frequently).

**Example:** `root = [1,null,2,2]` → `[2]`

**Algorithm:**
1. Base case: return default value when node is null.
2. Recurse into left subtree to get left result.
3. Recurse into right subtree to get right result.
4. Combine left and right results at current node and return.
`O(n) / O(h)`

```java
class Solution {
    //  base  count
    List<Integer> answer = new ArrayList<>();
    int base, count, maxCount;
    public int[] findMode(TreeNode root) {
        dfs(root);
        int[] mode = new int[answer.size()];
        for (int i = 0; i < answer.size(); ++i) {
            mode[i] = answer.get(i);
        }
        return mode;
    }
    public void dfs(TreeNode o) {
        if (o == null) {
            return;
        }
        dfs(o.left);
        update(o.val);
        dfs(o.right);
    }
    public void update(int x) {
        //
        if (x == base) {
            ++count;
        } else {
            count = 1;
            base = x;
        }
        if (count == maxCount) {
            answer.add(base);
        }
        if (count > maxCount) {
            maxCount = count;
            answer.clear();
            answer.add(base);
        }
    }
}
```

- Iterative DFS with explicit stack avoids O(h) call stack overhead.
- Morris traversal achieves O(1) space inorder traversal without stack or recursion.

---

#### #508. Most Frequent Subtree Sum

[leetcode.com/problems/most-frequent-subtree-sum/](https://leetcode.com/problems/most-frequent-subtree-sum/) · `O(n) / O(h)`

**Example:** `root = [5,2,-3]` → `[2,-3,4]`

**Algorithm:**
1. Base case: return default value when node is null.
2. Recurse into left subtree to get left result.
3. Recurse into right subtree to get right result.
4. Combine left and right results at current node and return.
`O(n) / O(h)`

```java
class Solution {
    Map<Integer, Integer> map = new HashMap<>();
    public int[] findFrequentTreeSum(TreeNode root) {
        dfs(root);
        int max = 0;
        for(int sum : map.keySet()) {
            max = Math.max(max, map.get(sum));
        }
        List<Integer> list = new ArrayList<>();
        for(int sum : map.keySet()) {
            if(map.get(sum) == max) {
                list.add(sum);
            }
        }
        int[] result = new int[list.size()];
        for(int i = 0; i < list.size(); i++) {
            result[i] = list.get(i);
        }
        return result;
    }
    public int dfs(TreeNode root) {
        if(root == null) {
            return 0;
        }
        int leftSum = dfs(root.left);
        int rightSum = dfs(root.right);
        int currentSum = root.val + leftSum + rightSum;
        map.put(currentSum, map.getOrDefault(currentSum, 0) + 1);
        return currentSum;
    }
}
```

- Return early on first HashMap hit rather than scanning full input.

---

#### #515. Find Largest Value in Each Tree Row

[leetcode.com/problems/find-largest-value-in-each-tree-row/](https://leetcode.com/problems/find-largest-value-in-each-tree-row/) · `O(n) / O(h)`

**Example:** `root = [1,3,2,5,3,null,9]` → `[1,3,9]`

**Algorithm:**
1. Init queue with root node.
2. Process level by level: snapshot queue.size() at start of each level.
3. Poll node, process value, enqueue non-null children.
4. Collect/aggregate results per level or across all nodes.
`O(n) / O(h)`

```java
class Solution {
    public List<Integer> largestValues(TreeNode root) {
        List<Integer> list = new ArrayList<>();
        if(root == null) {
            return list;
        }
        Deque<TreeNode> queue = new ArrayDeque<>();
        queue.offer(root);
        for(int depth = 0; !queue.isEmpty(); depth++) {
            int max = Integer.MIN_VALUE;
            for(int i = queue.size(); i > 0; i--) {
                TreeNode current = queue.poll();
                max = Math.max(max, current.val);
                if(current.left != null) {
                    queue.offer(current.left);
                }
                if(current.right != null) {
                    queue.offer(current.right);
                }
            }
            list.add(max);
        }
        return list;
    }
}
```

- Iterative DFS with explicit stack avoids O(h) call stack overhead.
- Morris traversal achieves O(1) space inorder traversal without stack or recursion.

#### #536. Construct Binary Tree from String

[leetcode.com/problems/construct-binary-tree-from-string/](https://leetcode.com/problems/construct-binary-tree-from-string/) · `O(n) / O(h)`

**Q:** Construct a binary tree from its string representation '4(2(3)(1))(6(5))'.

**Algorithm:**
1. Base case: return default value when node is null.
2. Recurse into left subtree to get left result.
3. Recurse into right subtree to get right result.
4. Combine left and right results at current node and return.
`O(n) / O(h)`

```java
// root.val(root.left.val)(root.right.val)Stack
// stacknull Null")"stack
class Solution {
    public TreeNode str2tree(String s) {
        if(s == null || s.length() == 0) {
            return null;
        }
        Deque<TreeNode> stack = new ArrayDeque<>();
        for(int i = 0; i < s.length(); i++) {
            char ch = s.charAt(i);
            if(ch == ')') {
                stack.pop();
            } else if((ch >= '0' && ch <= '9') || ch == '-') {
                int start = i;
                while(i + 1 < s.length() && s.charAt(i+1) >= '0' && s.charAt(i+1) <= '9') {
                    i++;
                }
                int num = Integer.valueOf(s.substring(start, i+1));
                TreeNode current = new TreeNode(num);
                if(!stack.isEmpty()) {
                    TreeNode parent = stack.peek();
                    if(parent.left == null) {
                        parent.left = current;
                    } else {
                        parent.right = current;
                    }
                }
                stack.push(current);
            }
        }
        return stack.isEmpty() ? null : stack.peek();
    }
}
```

- Iterative DFS with explicit stack avoids O(h) call stack overhead.
- Morris traversal achieves O(1) space inorder traversal without stack or recursion.

#### #538. Convert BST to Greater Tree

[leetcode.com/problems/convert-bst-to-greater-tree/](https://leetcode.com/problems/convert-bst-to-greater-tree/) · `O(n) / O(h)`

**Q:** Convert each BST node's value to the sum of all values greater than or equal to it.

**Example:** `root = [4,1,6,0,2,5,7,null,null,null,3,null,null,null,8]` → `[30,36,21,36,35,26,15,null,null,null,33,null,null,null,8]`

**Algorithm:**
1. Base case: return default value when node is null.
2. Recurse into left subtree to get left result.
3. Recurse into right subtree to get right result.
4. Combine left and right results at current node and return.
`O(n) / O(h)`

```java
class Solution {
    //
    TreeNode previous = null;
    public TreeNode convertBST(TreeNode root) {
        dfs(root);
        return root;
    }
    public void dfs(TreeNode current) {
        if(current == null) {
            return ;
        }
        dfs(current.right);
        if(previous != null) {
            current.val += previous.val;
        }
        previous = current;
        dfs(current.left);
    }
}
```

- Iterative DFS with explicit stack avoids O(h) call stack overhead.
- Morris traversal achieves O(1) space inorder traversal without stack or recursion.

---

#### #543. Diameter of Binary Tree

[leetcode.com/problems/diameter-of-binary-tree/](https://leetcode.com/problems/diameter-of-binary-tree/) · `O(n) / O(h)`

**Q:** Find the diameter of a binary tree (longest path between any two nodes).

**Example:** `root = [1,2,3,4,5]` → `3`

**Algorithm:**
1. DFS: return depth of subtree.
2. At each node: diameter candidate = leftDepth + rightDepth.
3. Update global max diameter; return max(leftDepth, rightDepth) + 1.
`O(n) / O(h)`

```java
class Solution {
    int max = 0;
    public int diameterOfBinaryTree(TreeNode root) {
        maxChildDepth(root);
        return max - 1;
    }
    public int maxChildDepth(TreeNode root) {
        if(root == null) {
            return 0;
        }
        int rightDepth = maxChildDepth(root.right);
        int leftDepth = maxChildDepth(root.left);
        max = Math.max(max, rightDepth + leftDepth + 1);
        return 1 + Math.max(leftDepth, rightDepth);
    }
}
```

- Single DFS pass is O(n). Avoid calling depth() for each node separately (O(n^2)).

---

#### #545. Boundary of Binary Tree

[leetcode.com/problems/boundary-of-binary-tree/](https://leetcode.com/problems/boundary-of-binary-tree/) · `O(n) / O(h)`

**Q:** Return the boundary of a binary tree: left boundary + leaves + right boundary (no duplicates).

**Algorithm:**
1. Base case: return default value when node is null.
2. Recurse into left subtree to get left result.
3. Recurse into right subtree to get right result.
4. Combine left and right results at current node and return.
`O(n) / O(h)`

```java
//  root
//
//
//
class Solution {
    public List<Integer> boundaryOfBinaryTree(TreeNode root) {
        List<Integer> result = new LinkedList<>();
        if (root == null) {
            return result;
        }
        if (!isLeaf(root)) {
            result.add(root.val);
        }
        leftTrunk(root.left, result);
        addLeaves(root, result);
        rightTrunk(root.right, result);
        return result;
    }
    private void leftTrunk(TreeNode root, List<Integer> result) {
        if (root == null) {
            return;
        }
        if (!isLeaf(root)) {
            result.add(root.val);
        }
        if (root.left != null) {
            leftTrunk(root.left, result);
        } else {
            leftTrunk(root.right, result);
        }
    }
    private void rightTrunk(TreeNode root, List<Integer> result) {
        if (root == null) {
            return;
        }
        if (root.right != null) {
            rightTrunk(root.right, result);
        } else {
            rightTrunk(root.left, result);
        }
        if (!isLeaf(root)) {
            result.add(root.val);
        }
    }
    private void addLeaves(TreeNode node, List<Integer> result) {
        if (node == null) {
            return;
        }
        if (isLeaf(node)) {
            result.add(node.val);
        } else {
            addLeaves(node.left, result);
            addLeaves(node.right, result);
        }
    }
    private boolean isLeaf(TreeNode node) {
        return node.left == null && node.right == null;
    }
}
```

- Iterative DFS with explicit stack avoids O(h) call stack overhead.
- Morris traversal achieves O(1) space inorder traversal without stack or recursion.

---

#### #559. Maximum Depth of N-ary Tree

[leetcode.com/problems/maximum-depth-of-nary-tree/](https://leetcode.com/problems/maximum-depth-of-nary-tree/) · `O(n) / O(h)`

**Algorithm:**
1. Init queue with root node.
2. Process level by level: snapshot queue.size() at start of each level.
3. Poll node, process value, enqueue non-null children.
4. Collect/aggregate results per level or across all nodes.
`O(n) / O(h)`

```java
class Solution {
    public int maxDepth(Node root) {
        if(root == null) {
            return 0;
        }
        Deque<Node> queue = new ArrayDeque<>();
        queue.offer(root);
        int level;
        for(level = 1; !queue.isEmpty(); level++) {
            for(int i = queue.size(); i > 0; i--) {
                Node current = queue.poll();
                for(Node child : current.children) {
                    queue.offer(child);
                }
            }
        }
        return level;
    }
}
```

- Iterative DFS with explicit stack avoids O(h) call stack overhead.
- Morris traversal achieves O(1) space inorder traversal without stack or recursion.

#### #563. Binary Tree Tilt

[leetcode.com/problems/binary-tree-tilt/](https://leetcode.com/problems/binary-tree-tilt/) · `O(n) / O(h)`

**Q:** Find the total tilt of a binary tree (sum of |leftSum - rightSum| for all nodes).

**Example:** `root = [4,2,9,3,5,null,7]` → `15`

**Algorithm:**
1. Base case: return default value when node is null.
2. Recurse into left subtree to get left result.
3. Recurse into right subtree to get right result.
4. Combine left and right results at current node and return.
`O(n) / O(h)`

```java
class Solution {
    int tilt = 0;
    public int findTilt(TreeNode root) {
        sum(root);
        return tilt;
    }
    private int sum(TreeNode root) {
        if(root == null) {
            return 0;
        }
        int left = sum(root.left);
        int right = sum(root.right);
        tilt += Math.abs(left - right);
        return root.val + left + right;
    }
}
```

- Iterative DFS with explicit stack avoids O(h) call stack overhead.
- Morris traversal achieves O(1) space inorder traversal without stack or recursion.

---

#### #572. Subtree of Another Tree

[leetcode.com/problems/subtree-of-another-tree/](https://leetcode.com/problems/subtree-of-another-tree/) · `O(m*n) / O(h)`

**Q:** Check if tree t is a subtree of tree s.

**Example:** `root = [3,4,5,1,2], subRoot = [4,1,2]` → `true`

**Algorithm:**
1. For each node in s: check if the subtree rooted there equals t.
2. Use isSameTree() helper (O(n*m) total).
3. Optimize: serialize both trees and check substring.
`O(m*n) / O(h)`

```java
class Solution {
    public boolean isSubtree(TreeNode root, TreeNode subRoot) {
        if(root == null && subRoot == null) {
            return true;
        }
        if(root == null || subRoot == null) {
            return false;
        }
        return isSameTree(root, subRoot) || isSubtree(root.left, subRoot) || isSubtree(root.right, subRoot);
    }
    public boolean isSameTree(TreeNode root, TreeNode subRoot) {
        if(root == null && subRoot == null) {
            return true;
        }
        if(root == null || subRoot == null) {
            return false;
        }
        return root.val == subRoot.val && isSameTree(root.left, subRoot.left) && isSameTree(root.right, subRoot.right);
    }
}
// DFS
class Solution {
    List<Integer> sOrder = new ArrayList<>();
    List<Integer> tOrder = new ArrayList<>();
    int maxElement, lNull, rNull;
    public boolean isSubtree(TreeNode s, TreeNode t) {
        maxElement = Integer.MIN_VALUE;
        getMaxElement(s);
        getMaxElement(t);
        lNull = maxElement + 1;
        rNull = maxElement + 2;
        getDfsOrder(s, sOrder);
        getDfsOrder(t, tOrder);
        return kmp();
    }
    public void getMaxElement(TreeNode t) {
        if (t == null) {
            return;
        }
        maxElement = Math.max(maxElement, t.val);
        getMaxElement(t.left);
        getMaxElement(t.right);
    }
    public void getDfsOrder(TreeNode t, List<Integer> tar) {
        if (t == null) {
            return;
        }
        tar.add(t.val);
        if (t.left != null) {
            getDfsOrder(t.left, tar);
        } else {
            tar.add(lNull);
        }
        if (t.right != null) {
            getDfsOrder(t.right, tar);
        } else {
            tar.add(rNull);
        }
    }
    public boolean kmp() {
        int sLen = sOrder.size(), tLen = tOrder.size();
        int[] fail = new int[tOrder.size()];
        Arrays.fill(fail, -1);
        for (int i = 1, j = -1; i < tLen; ++i) {
            while (j != -1 && !(tOrder.get(i).equals(tOrder.get(j + 1)))) {
                j = fail[j];
            }
            if (tOrder.get(i).equals(tOrder.get(j + 1))) {
                ++j;
            }
            fail[i] = j;
        }
        for (int i = 0, j = -1; i < sLen; ++i) {
            while (j != -1 && !(sOrder.get(i).equals(tOrder.get(j + 1)))) {
                j = fail[j];
            }
            if (sOrder.get(i).equals(tOrder.get(j + 1))) {
                ++j;
            }
            if (j == tLen - 1) {
                return true;
            }
        }
        return false;
    }
}
```

- Serialization check: serialize both trees, check if s_serial contains t_serial. O(m+n) time.

---

#### #589. N-ary Tree Preorder Traversal

[leetcode.com/problems/nary-tree-preorder-traversal/](https://leetcode.com/problems/nary-tree-preorder-traversal/) · `O(n) / O(h)`

**Q:** Return the preorder traversal of an N-ary tree.

**Algorithm:**
1. Base case: return default value when node is null.
2. Recurse into left subtree to get left result.
3. Recurse into right subtree to get right result.
4. Combine left and right results at current node and return.
`O(n) / O(h)`

```java
class Solution {
    public List<Integer> preorder(Node root) {
        List<Integer> inorder = new LinkedList<>();
        if(root == null) {
            return inorder;
        }
        Deque<Node> stack = new ArrayDeque<>();
        stack.push(root);
        while(!stack.isEmpty()) {
            root = stack.pop();
            inorder.add(root.val);
            int childrenNums = root.children.size();
            // , binary tree
            for(int i = childrenNums - 1; i >= 0; i--) {
                stack.push(root.children.get(i));
            }
        }
        return inorder;
    }
}
```

- Iterative DFS with explicit stack avoids O(h) call stack overhead.
- Morris traversal achieves O(1) space inorder traversal without stack or recursion.

---

#### #590. N-ary Tree Postorder Traversal

[leetcode.com/problems/nary-tree-postorder-traversal/](https://leetcode.com/problems/nary-tree-postorder-traversal/) · `O(n) / O(h)`

**Q:** Return the postorder traversal of an N-ary tree.

**Example:** `root = [1,null,3,2,4,null,5,6]` → `[5,6,3,2,4,1]`

**Algorithm:**
1. Base case: return default value when node is null.
2. Recurse into left subtree to get left result.
3. Recurse into right subtree to get right result.
4. Combine left and right results at current node and return.
`O(n) / O(h)`

```java
class Solution {
    public List<Integer> postorder(Node root) {
        List<Integer> postOrderRes = new LinkedList<>();
        if(root == null) {
            return postOrderRes;
        }
        Stack<Node> stack = new Stack<>();
        Node preNode = null; //
        stack.add(root);
        while(!stack.isEmpty()) {
            root = stack.peek();
            int childrenNums = root.children.size();
            //
            if(childrenNums == 0 || root.children.get(childrenNums - 1) == preNode) {
                preNode = stack.pop();
                postOrderRes.add(preNode.val);
            } else {
                //
                for(int i = childrenNums - 1; i >= 0; --i ) {
                    stack.add(root.children.get(i));
                }
            }
        }
        return postOrderRes;
    }
}
```

- Iterative DFS with explicit stack avoids O(h) call stack overhead.
- Morris traversal achieves O(1) space inorder traversal without stack or recursion.

---

#### #606. Construct String from Binary Tree

[leetcode.com/problems/construct-string-from-binary-tree/](https://leetcode.com/problems/construct-string-from-binary-tree/) · `O(n) / O(h)`

**Q:** Construct a string from a binary tree using preorder with parentheses.

**Example:** `root = [1,2,3]` → `"1(2)(3)"`

**Algorithm:**
1. Base case: return default value when node is null.
2. Recurse into left subtree to get left result.
3. Recurse into right subtree to get right result.
4. Combine left and right results at current node and return.
`O(n) / O(h)`

```java
public class Solution {
    public String tree2str(TreeNode t) {
        if(t==null)
        return "";
        if(t.left==null && t.right==null)
        return t.val+"";
        if(t.right==null)
        return t.val+"("+tree2str(t.left)+")";
        return t.val+"("+tree2str(t.left)+")("+tree2str(t.right)+")";
    }
}
class Solution {
    public String tree2str(TreeNode root) {
        if(root == null) {
            return  "";
        }
        Deque<TreeNode> stack = new ArrayDeque<>();
        stack.push(root);
        Set<TreeNode> visited = new HashSet<>();
        StringBuilder sb = new StringBuilder();
        while(!stack.isEmpty()) {
            root = stack.peek();
            if(visited.contains(root)) {
                stack.pop();
                sb.append(")");
            } else {
                visited.add(root);
                sb.append("(" + root.val);
                //  ()
                if(root.left == null && root.right != null) {
                    sb.append("()");
                }
                //
                if(root.right != null) {
                    stack.push(root.right);
                }
                if(root.left != null) {
                    stack.push(root.left);
                }
            }
        }
        return sb.substring(1, sb.length() - 1);
    }
}
```

- Iterative DFS with explicit stack avoids O(h) call stack overhead.
- Morris traversal achieves O(1) space inorder traversal without stack or recursion.

#### #617. Merge Two Binary Trees

[leetcode.com/problems/merge-two-binary-trees/](https://leetcode.com/problems/merge-two-binary-trees/) · `O(n) / O(h)`

**Q:** Merge two binary trees by summing overlapping nodes.

**Example:** `root1 = [1,3,2,5], root2 = [2,1,3,null,4,null,7]` → `[3,4,5,5,4,null,7]`

**Algorithm:**
1. Base case: return default value when node is null.
2. Recurse into left subtree to get left result.
3. Recurse into right subtree to get right result.
4. Combine left and right results at current node and return.
`O(n) / O(h)`

```java
class Solution {
    public TreeNode mergeTrees(TreeNode root1, TreeNode root2) {
        if(root1 == null) {
            return root2;
        } else if(root2 == null) {
            return root1;
        } else {
            TreeNode root = new TreeNode(root1.val + root2.val);
            root.left = mergeTrees(root1.left, root2.left);
            root.right = mergeTrees(root1.right, root2.right);
            return root;
        }
    }
}
```

- Iterative DFS with explicit stack avoids O(h) call stack overhead.
- Morris traversal achieves O(1) space inorder traversal without stack or recursion.

---

#### #637. Average of Levels in Binary Tree

[leetcode.com/problems/average-of-levels-in-binary-tree/](https://leetcode.com/problems/average-of-levels-in-binary-tree/) · `O(n) / O(h)`

**Q:** Return the average value of each level of a binary tree.

**Example:** `root = [3,9,20,null,null,15,7]` → `[3.00000,14.50000,11.00000]`

**Algorithm:**
1. Init queue with root node.
2. Process level by level: snapshot queue.size() at start of each level.
3. Poll node, process value, enqueue non-null children.
4. Collect/aggregate results per level or across all nodes.
`O(n) / O(h)`

```java
class Solution {
    public List<Double> averageOfLevels(TreeNode root) {
        List<Double> averages = new ArrayList<>();
        Deque<TreeNode> queue = new ArrayDeque<>();
        queue.offer(root);
        while (!queue.isEmpty()) {
            double sum = 0;
            int size = queue.size();
            for (int i = 0; i < size; i++) {
                TreeNode node = queue.poll();
                sum += node.val;
                TreeNode left = node.left, right = node.right;
                if (left != null) {
                    queue.offer(left);
                }
                if (right != null) {
                    queue.offer(right);
                }
            }
            averages.add(sum / size);
        }
        return averages;
    }
}
```

- Iterative DFS with explicit stack avoids O(h) call stack overhead.
- Morris traversal achieves O(1) space inorder traversal without stack or recursion.

---

#### #652. Find Duplicate Subtrees

[leetcode.com/problems/find-duplicate-subtrees/](https://leetcode.com/problems/find-duplicate-subtrees/) · `O(n) / O(h)`

**Q:** Find all duplicate subtrees in a binary tree; return root of each.

**Example:** `root = [1,2,2,4,null,null,4]` → `[4]`

**Algorithm:**
1. Base case: return default value when node is null.
2. Recurse into left subtree to get left result.
3. Recurse into right subtree to get right result.
4. Combine left and right results at current node and return.
`O(n) / O(h)`

```java
class Solution {
    Map<String, Integer> map = new HashMap<>();
    List<TreeNode> nodes = new ArrayList<>();
    public List<TreeNode> findDuplicateSubtrees(TreeNode root) {
        if(root == null) {
            return nodes;
        }
        dfs(root);
        return nodes;
    }
    private String dfs(TreeNode root) {
        if(root == null) return "#";
        String left = dfs(root.left);
        String right = dfs(root.right);
        String preorderSerialization = root.val + " " + left + " " + right;
        Integer i = map.getOrDefault(preorderSerialization, 0);
        if(i == 1) {
            nodes.add(root);
        }
        map.put(preorderSerialization, i + 1);
        return preorderSerialization;
    }
}
}
```

- Return early on first HashMap hit rather than scanning full input.

---

#### #653. Two Sum IV - Input is a BST

[leetcode.com/problems/two-sum-iv-input-is-a-bst/](https://leetcode.com/problems/two-sum-iv-input-is-a-bst/) · `O(n) / O(h)`

**Q:** Find two numbers in a BST that sum to a given target.

**Example:** `root = [5,3,6,2,4,null,7], k = 9` → `true`

**Algorithm:**
1. Base case: return default value when node is null.
2. Recurse into left subtree to get left result.
3. Recurse into right subtree to get right result.
4. Combine left and right results at current node and return.
`O(n) / O(h)`

```java
class Solution {
    public boolean findTarget(TreeNode root, int k) {
        if(root == null) {
            return false;
        }
        Deque<TreeNode> stack = new ArrayDeque<>();
        TreeNode current = root;
        Set<Integer> set = new HashSet<>();
        while(current != null || !stack.isEmpty()) {
            while(current != null) {
                stack.push(current);
                current = current.left;
            }
            current = stack.pop();
            if(set.contains(k - current.val)) {
                return true;
            }
            set.add(current.val);
            current = current.right;
        }
        return false;
    }
}
```

- Iterative DFS with explicit stack avoids O(h) call stack overhead.
- Morris traversal achieves O(1) space inorder traversal without stack or recursion.

#### #654. Maximum Binary Tree

[leetcode.com/problems/maximum-binary-tree/](https://leetcode.com/problems/maximum-binary-tree/) · `O(n) / O(h)`

**Q:** Build the maximum binary tree: root is array max, left from left subarray, right from right.

**Example:** `nums = [3,2,1,6,0,5]` → `[6,3,5,null,2,0,null,null,1]`

**Algorithm:**
1. Base case: return default value when node is null.
2. Recurse into left subtree to get left result.
3. Recurse into right subtree to get right result.
4. Combine left and right results at current node and return.
`O(n) / O(h)`

```java
public class Solution {
    public TreeNode constructMaximumBinaryTree(int[] nums) {
        return buildTree(nums, 0, nums.length);
    }
    public TreeNode buildTree(int[] nums, int l, int r) {
        if (l == r)
        return null;
        int max_i = max(nums, l, r);
        TreeNode root = new TreeNode(nums[max_i]);
        root.left = buildTree(nums, l, max_i);
        root.right = buildTree(nums, max_i + 1, r);
        return root;
    }
    public int max(int[] nums, int l, int r) {
        int max_i = l;
        for (int i = l; i < r; i++) {
            if (nums[max_i] < nums[i])
            max_i = i;
        }
        return max_i;
    }
}
```

- Iterative DFS with explicit stack avoids O(h) call stack overhead.
- Morris traversal achieves O(1) space inorder traversal without stack or recursion.

---

#### #998. Maximum Binary Tree II

[leetcode.com/problems/maximum-binary-tree-ii/](https://leetcode.com/problems/maximum-binary-tree-ii/) · `O(n) / O(h)`

**Example:** `root = [4,2,4,3,1], val = 5, key = 4` → `[5,4,null,2,null,3,1]`

**Algorithm:**
1. Base case: return default value when node is null.
2. Recurse into left subtree to get left result.
3. Recurse into right subtree to get right result.
4. Combine left and right results at current node and return.
`O(n) / O(h)`

```java
class Solution {
    // Aval
    //  root
    //  root  val
    public TreeNode insertIntoMaxTree(TreeNode root, int val) {
        if(root == null || root.val < val) {
            TreeNode temp = new TreeNode(val);
            temp.left = root;
            return temp;
        }
        root.right = insertIntoMaxTree(root.right, val);
        return root;
    }
}
class pair{
    int first;
    int second;
    pair(int f, int s){first=f;second=s;}
}
class Solution {
    static node num[] = new node[4005];
    class node{
        int id;
        int left, right;
        int max;
        pair build(int[] a, int l, int r, int c){
            int m;
            num[c].left = l;
            num[c].right = r;
            if(l==r){
                num[c].id = l;
                num[c].max=a[l];
                return new pair(l,a[l]);
            }
            m = l+r>>1;
            pair r1 = build(a, l,m,c*2);
            pair r2 = build(a, m+1,r,c*2+1);
            if(r1.second>r2.second){
                num[c].id=r1.first;
                num[c].max=r1.second;
                return r1;
            }else{
                num[c].id=r2.first;
                num[c].max=r2.second;
                return r2;
            }
        }
        pair queryMax(int[] a, int l, int r, int c){
            int m;
            if(l==num[c].left&&r==num[c].right)
            return new pair(num[c].id,num[c].max);
            m = (num[c].left+num[c].right)>>1;
            if(r<=m)
            return queryMax(a, l,r,c*2);
            else if(l>m)
            return queryMax(a, l,r,c*2+1);
            else{
                pair r1 = queryMax(a, l,m,c*2);
                pair r2 = queryMax(a, m+1,r,c*2+1);
                return r1.second>r2.second?r1:r2;
            }
        }
    }
    void fun(TreeNode root, int[] nums, int l, int r){
        if(l<=r){
            //[l,r]
            int max = Integer.MIN_VALUE, maxIdx = 0, size = r-l+1;
            //1.
            /*
            for(int i=l;i<=r;i++){
                if(nums[i] > max){
                    max = nums[i];
                    maxIdx = i;
                }
            }
            root.val = max;
            */
            //2.
            pair getP = new node().queryMax(nums,l, r, 1);
            max = getP.second;
            maxIdx = getP.first;
            root.val = max;
            if(maxIdx -1 >=l){
                root.left = new TreeNode(0);
                fun(root.left, nums, l, maxIdx-1);
            }
            if(maxIdx+1<=r){
                root.right = new TreeNode(0);
                fun(root.right, nums, maxIdx+1, r);
            }
        }
    }
    TreeNode fun1(int[] nums){
        Stack<TreeNode> sv = new Stack();
        for(int i=0;i<nums.length;i++){
            int num = nums[i];
            TreeNode temp = null, prePeek = null;
            while(!sv.empty() && num > sv.peek().val)
            temp = sv.pop();
            //
            if(sv.size() != 0)
            prePeek = sv.peek();
            sv.push(new TreeNode(num));
            //
            if(prePeek != null){
                prePeek.right = sv.peek();
            }
            //
            if(temp != null){
                sv.peek().left = temp;
            }
            if(i==nums.length-1){
                while(sv.size()>1){
                    sv.pop();
                }
                return sv.peek();
            }
        }
        return null;
    }
    public TreeNode constructMaximumBinaryTree(int[] nums) {
        return fun1(nums); //
        /*
        TreeNode root = null;
        int size = nums.length;
        if(nums.length == 0) return root;
        else{
            //
            for(int i=0;i<nums.length*4;i++){
                num[i] = new node();
            }
            node p = new node();
            p.build(nums, 0, nums.length-1,1);
            root = new TreeNode(0);
            fun(root, nums, 0, size-1); //
        }
        return root;
        */
    }
}
```

- Iterative DFS with explicit stack avoids O(h) call stack overhead.
- Morris traversal achieves O(1) space inorder traversal without stack or recursion.

#### #662. Maximum Width of Binary Tree

[leetcode.com/problems/maximum-width-of-binary-tree/](https://leetcode.com/problems/maximum-width-of-binary-tree/) · `O(n) / O(h)`

**Example:** `root = [1,3,2,5,3,null,9]` → `4`

**Algorithm:**
1. Init left=0, frequency map/counter, result.
2. Expand right: add s[right] to window state.
3. While window invalid: remove s[left] and advance left.
4. Update result with current window size/value.
`O(n) / O(h)`

```java
class Solution {
    public int widthOfBinaryTree(TreeNode root) {
        Deque<TreeNode> queue = new ArrayDeque<>();
        Deque<Integer> positionQueue = new ArrayDeque<>();
        queue.offer(root);
        positionQueue.offer(0);
        int max = 0;
        for(int depth = 1; !queue.isEmpty(); depth++) {
            int left = -1, right = -1;
            for(int size = queue.size(); size > 0; size--) {
                TreeNode current = queue.poll();
                int currentPosition = positionQueue.poll();
                if(left == -1) {
                    left = currentPosition;
                }
                right = currentPosition;
                if(current.left != null) {
                    queue.offer(current.left);
                    positionQueue.offer(currentPosition * 2);
                }
                if(current.right != null) {
                    queue.offer(current.right);
                    positionQueue.offer(currentPosition * 2 + 1);
                }
            }
            max = Math.max(max, right - left + 1);
        }
        return max;
    }
}
class Solution {
    int result;
    Map<Integer, Integer> left;
    //  position  left[depth]
    //  pos - left[depth] + 1
    public int widthOfBinaryTree(TreeNode root) {
        result = 0;
        left = new HashMap();
        dfs(root, 0, 0);
        return result;
    }
    public void dfs(TreeNode root, int depth, int pos) {
        if (root == null) return;
        left.computeIfAbsent(depth, x-> pos);
        result = Math.max(result, pos - left.get(depth) + 1);
        dfs(root.left, depth + 1, 2 * pos);
        dfs(root.right, depth + 1, 2 * pos + 1);
    }
}
```

- Iterative DFS with explicit stack avoids O(h) call stack overhead.
- Morris traversal achieves O(1) space inorder traversal without stack or recursion.

---

#### #671. Second Minimum Node In a Binary Tree

[leetcode.com/problems/second-minimum-node-in-a-binary-tree/](https://leetcode.com/problems/second-minimum-node-in-a-binary-tree/) · `O(n) / O(h)`

**Example:** `root = [2,2,5,null,null,5,7]` → `10`

**Algorithm:**
1. Base case: return default value when node is null.
2. Recurse into left subtree to get left result.
3. Recurse into right subtree to get right result.
4. Combine left and right results at current node and return.
`O(n) / O(h)`

```java
class Solution {
    //
    int result;
    int rootvalue;
    public int findSecondMinimumValue(TreeNode root) {
        result = -1;
        rootvalue = root.val;
        dfs(root);
        return result;
    }
    public void dfs(TreeNode node) {
        if (node == null) {
            return;
        }
        if (result != -1 && node.val >= result) {
            return;
        }
        if (node.val > rootvalue) {
            result = node.val;
        }
        dfs(node.left);
        dfs(node.right);
    }
}
class Solution {
    //
    public int findSecondMinimumValue(TreeNode root) {
        if(root.left == null && root.right == null) {
            return -1;
        }
        //  root.val
        //  root.val
        int left = root.left.val;
        if(root.left.val == root.val) {
            left = findSecondMinimumValue(root.left);
        }
        int right = root.right.val;
        if(root.right.val == root.val) {
            right = findSecondMinimumValue(root.right);
        }
        if(left == -1) {
            return right;
        }
        if(right == -1) {
            return left;
        }
        //
        return Math.min(left, right);
    }
}
```

- Iterative DFS with explicit stack avoids O(h) call stack overhead.
- Morris traversal achieves O(1) space inorder traversal without stack or recursion.

#### #700. Search in a Binary Search Tree

[leetcode.com/problems/search-in-a-binary-search-tree/](https://leetcode.com/problems/search-in-a-binary-search-tree/) · `O(h) / O(h)`

**Q:** Search for a value in a BST.

**Example:** `root = [4,2,7,1,3], val = 2` → `2`

**Algorithm:**
1. If root null or root.val==target: return root.
2. If target < root.val: search left; else search right.
`O(h) / O(h)`

```java
class Solution {
    public TreeNode searchBST(TreeNode root, int val) {
        if(root == null) {
            return null;
        }
        if(root.val == val) {
            return root;
        } else if(root.val > val) {
            return searchBST(root.left, val);
        } else {
            return searchBST(root.right, val);
        }
    }
}
```

- Iterative BST search avoids O(h) call stack.

---

#### #701. Insert into a Binary Search Tree

[leetcode.com/problems/insert-into-a-binary-search-tree/](https://leetcode.com/problems/insert-into-a-binary-search-tree/) · `O(n) / O(h)`

**Q:** Insert a value into a BST and return the updated root.

**Example:** `root = [4,2,7,1,3], val = 5` → `[4,2,7,1,3,5]`

**Algorithm:**
1. Base case: return default value when node is null.
2. Recurse into left subtree to get left result.
3. Recurse into right subtree to get right result.
4. Combine left and right results at current node and return.
`O(n) / O(h)`

```java
class Solution {
    public TreeNode insertIntoBST(TreeNode root, int val) {
        if (root == null) {
            return new TreeNode(val);
        }
        if (val < root.val) {
            root.left = insertIntoBST(root.left, val);
        } else if (val > root.val) {
            root.right = insertIntoBST(root.right, val);
        }
        return root;
    }
}
```

- Iterative DFS with explicit stack avoids O(h) call stack overhead.
- Morris traversal achieves O(1) space inorder traversal without stack or recursion.

---

#### #742. Closest Leaf in a Binary Tree

[leetcode.com/problems/closest-leaf-in-a-binary-tree/](https://leetcode.com/problems/closest-leaf-in-a-binary-tree/) · `O(n) / O(h)`

**Q:** Find the node in a binary tree closest to the target that is a leaf.

**Algorithm:**
1. Init queue with root node.
2. Process level by level: snapshot queue.size() at start of each level.
3. Poll node, process value, enqueue non-null children.
4. Collect/aggregate results per level or across all nodes.
`O(n) / O(h)`

```java
class Solution {
    public int findClosestLeaf(TreeNode root, int k) {
        Map<TreeNode, List<TreeNode>> graph = new HashMap();
        //
        dfs(graph, root, null);
        Deque<TreeNode> queue = new ArrayDeque<>();
        Set<TreeNode> seen = new HashSet();
        for (TreeNode node: graph.keySet()) {
            if (node != null && node.val == k) {
                queue.offer(node);
                seen.add(node);
            }
        }
        // System.out.println(queue.peek().val);
        while (!queue.isEmpty()) {
            TreeNode current = queue.poll();
            if(current == null) {
                continue;
            }
            if (graph.get(current).size() <= 1) {
                return current.val; // leaf node
            }
            for (TreeNode neighbor: graph.get(current)) {
                if (neighbor != null && !seen.contains(neighbor)) {
                    seen.add(neighbor);
                    queue.offer(neighbor);
                }
            }
        }
        return Integer.MAX_VALUE;
    }
    public void dfs(Map<TreeNode, List<TreeNode>> graph, TreeNode current, TreeNode parent) {
        if(current == null) {
            return ;
        }
        graph.computeIfAbsent(current, list -> new ArrayList<>()).add(parent);
        graph.computeIfAbsent(parent, list -> new ArrayList<>()).add(current);
        dfs(graph, current.left, current);
        dfs(graph, current.right, current);
    }
}
```

- Iterative DFS with explicit stack avoids O(h) call stack overhead.
- Morris traversal achieves O(1) space inorder traversal without stack or recursion.

---

#### #776. Split BST

[leetcode.com/problems/split-bst/](https://leetcode.com/problems/split-bst/) · `O(n) / O(h)`

**Q:** Split a BST into two trees: one with values ≤ V and one with values > V.

**Example:** `root = [1,2,3,null,null,4,5,null,null,6,7], k = 2` → `[[6],[7],[4,5],[2,3],[1]]... (splits)`

**Algorithm:**
1. Base case: return default value when node is null.
2. Recurse into left subtree to get left result.
3. Recurse into right subtree to get right result.
4. Combine left and right results at current node and return.
`O(n) / O(h)`

```java
class Solution {
    public TreeNode[] splitBST(TreeNode root, int V) {
        if (root == null)
        return new TreeNode[]{null, null};
        else if (root.val <= V) {
            TreeNode[] bns = splitBST(root.right, V);
            root.right = bns[0];
            bns[0] = root;
            return bns;
        } else {
            TreeNode[] bns = splitBST(root.left, V);
            root.left = bns[1];
            bns[1] = root;
            return bns;
        }
    }
}
```

- Iterative DFS with explicit stack avoids O(h) call stack overhead.
- Morris traversal achieves O(1) space inorder traversal without stack or recursion.

---

#### #814. Binary Tree Pruning

[leetcode.com/problems/binary-tree-pruning/](https://leetcode.com/problems/binary-tree-pruning/) · `O(n) / O(h)`

**Q:** Prune a binary tree by removing subtrees containing no 1s.

**Example:** `root = [1,null,0,0,1]` → `[1,null,0,null,1]`

**Algorithm:**
1. Base case: return default value when node is null.
2. Recurse into left subtree to get left result.
3. Recurse into right subtree to get right result.
4. Combine left and right results at current node and return.
`O(n) / O(h)`

```java
class Solution {
    public TreeNode pruneTree(TreeNode root) {
        return dfs(root) ? root : null;
    }
    public boolean dfs(TreeNode root) {
        if(root == null) {
            return false;
        }
        boolean left = dfs(root.left);
        boolean right = dfs(root.right);
        if(!left) {
            root.left = null;
        }
        if(!right) {
            root.right = null;
        }
        return left || right || root.val == 1;
    }
}
```

- Iterative DFS with explicit stack avoids O(h) call stack overhead.
- Morris traversal achieves O(1) space inorder traversal without stack or recursion.

---

#### #863. All Nodes Distance K in Binary Tree

[leetcode.com/problems/all-nodes-distance-k-in-binary-tree/](https://leetcode.com/problems/all-nodes-distance-k-in-binary-tree/) · `O(n) / O(h)`

**Q:** Find all nodes at distance k from a target node in a binary tree.

**Example:** `root = [3,5,1,6,2,0,8,null,null,7,4], target = 5, k = 2` → `[7,4,1]`

**Algorithm:**
1. Init queue with root node.
2. Process level by level: snapshot queue.size() at start of each level.
3. Poll node, process value, enqueue non-null children.
4. Collect/aggregate results per level or across all nodes.
`O(n) / O(h)`

```java
class Solution {
    // hashMap
    private Map<Integer, TreeNode> map = new HashMap<>();
    private List<Integer> result = new ArrayList<>();
    private boolean[] visited = new boolean[500];
    public List<Integer> distanceK(TreeNode root, TreeNode target, int k) {
        // 1.
        findParent(root);
        Deque<TreeNode> queue = new LinkedList<>();
        queue.offer(target);
        int depth = 0;
        // 2.
        while (!queue.isEmpty()) {
            for (int qSize = queue.size(); qSize > 0; qSize--){
                TreeNode node = queue.poll();
                visited[node.val] = true;
                if (depth == k) // Kres
                result.add(node.val);
                if (node.left != null && !visited[node.left.val])
                queue.offer(node.left);
                if (node.right != null && !visited[node.right.val])
                queue.offer(node.right);
                //
                if (map.get(node.val) != null && !visited[map.get(node.val).val])
                queue.offer(map.get(node.val));
            }
            depth++;
        }
        return result;
    }
    private void findParent(TreeNode root) {
        if (root.left != null) {
            map.put(root.left.val, root);
            findParent(root.left);
        }
        if (root.right != null) {
            map.put(root.right.val, root);
            findParent(root.right);
        }
    }
}
class Solution {
    public List<Integer> distanceK(TreeNode root, TreeNode target, int k) {
        Map<TreeNode, TreeNode> map = generateParentMap(root);
        Set<TreeNode> visited = new HashSet<>();
        Deque<TreeNode> queue = new ArrayDeque<>();
        queue.offer(target);
        visited.add(target);
        List<Integer> result = new ArrayList<>();
        for(int depth = 0; !queue.isEmpty() && depth <= k; depth++) {
            for(int size = queue.size(); size > 0; size--) {
                TreeNode current = queue.poll();
                if(depth == k) {
                    result.add(current.val);
                    continue;
                }
                if(current.left != null && !visited.contains(current.left)) {
                    queue.offer(current.left);
                    visited.add(current.left);
                }
                if(current.right != null && !visited.contains(current.right)) {
                    queue.offer(current.right);
                    visited.add(current.right);
                }
                if(map.get(current) != null && !visited.contains(map.get(current))) {
                    queue.offer(map.get(current));
                    visited.add(map.get(current));
                }
            }
        }
        return result;
    }
    public Map<TreeNode, TreeNode> generateParentMap(TreeNode root) {
        Map<TreeNode, TreeNode> map = new HashMap<>();
        dfs(root, map);
        return map;
    }
    private void dfs(TreeNode root, Map<TreeNode, TreeNode> map) {
        if(root.left != null) {
            map.put(root.left, root);
            dfs(root.left, map);
        }
        if(root.right != null) {
            map.put(root.right, root);
            dfs(root.right, map);
        }
    }
}
```

- Iterative DFS with explicit stack avoids O(h) call stack overhead.
- Morris traversal achieves O(1) space inorder traversal without stack or recursion.

---

#### #865. Smallest Subtree with all the Deepest Nodes

[leetcode.com/problems/smallest-subtree-with-all-the-deepest-nodes/](https://leetcode.com/problems/smallest-subtree-with-all-the-deepest-nodes/) · `O(n) / O(h)`

**Q:** Find the smallest subtree containing all the deepest leaves.

**Example:** `root = [3,5,1,6,2,0,8,null,null,7,4]` → `[2,7,4]`

**Algorithm:**
1. Base case: return default value when node is null.
2. Recurse into left subtree to get left result.
3. Recurse into right subtree to get right result.
4. Combine left and right results at current node and return.
`O(n) / O(h)`

```java
class Solution {
    class Element {
        TreeNode node; //
        int distance; //
        Element(TreeNode _node, int _distance) {
            node = _node;
            distance = _distance;
        }
    }
    // DFS
    public TreeNode subtreeWithAllDeepest(TreeNode root) {
        return dfs(root).node;
    }
    public Element dfs(TreeNode root) {
        if(root == null ) {
            return new Element(null, 0);
        }
        Element left = dfs(root.left);
        Element right = dfs(root.right);
        if(left.distance > right.distance) {
            return new Element(left.node, left.distance + 1);
        } else if (left.distance < right.distance) {
            return new Element(right.node, right.distance + 1);
        } else {
            return new Element(root, left.distance + 1);
        }
    }
}
```

- Iterative DFS with explicit stack avoids O(h) call stack overhead.
- Morris traversal achieves O(1) space inorder traversal without stack or recursion.

---

#### #897. Increasing Order Search Tree

[leetcode.com/problems/increasing-order-search-tree/](https://leetcode.com/problems/increasing-order-search-tree/) · `O(n) / O(h)`

**Q:** Transform a BST into an increasing-order search tree (right-skewed).

**Example:** `root = [5,3,6,2,4,null,8,1,null,null,null,null,null,7,9]` → `[1,2,3,4,5,6,7,8,9]`

**Algorithm:**
1. Base case: return default value when node is null.
2. Recurse into left subtree to get left result.
3. Recurse into right subtree to get right result.
4. Combine left and right results at current node and return.
`O(n) / O(h)`

```java
class Solution {
    private TreeNode ancestor = null;
    private TreeNode head = null;
    public TreeNode increasingBST(TreeNode root) {
        dfs(root);
        ancestor.left = null;
        ancestor.right = null;
        return head;
    }
    private void dfs(TreeNode root) {
        if(root == null) {
            return ;
        }
        dfs(root.left);
        if(ancestor != null) {
            ancestor.left = null;
            ancestor.right = root;
        }
        if(head == null) {
            head = root;
        }
        ancestor = root;
        dfs(root.right);
    }
}
```

- Iterative DFS with explicit stack avoids O(h) call stack overhead.
- Morris traversal achieves O(1) space inorder traversal without stack or recursion.

---

#### #919. Complete Binary Tree Inserter

[leetcode.com/problems/complete-binary-tree-inserter/](https://leetcode.com/problems/complete-binary-tree-inserter/) · `O(n) / O(h)`

**Q:** Design a complete binary tree inserter with O(1) insert.

**Example:** `root = [1,2,3,4], val = 5` → `[1,2,3,4,5]`

**Algorithm:**
1. Base case: return default value when node is null.
2. Recurse into left subtree to get left result.
3. Recurse into right subtree to get right result.
4. Combine left and right results at current node and return.
`O(n) / O(h)`

```java
class CBTInserter {
    int size;
    TreeNode[] A;
    private void traverse(TreeNode root,int k){
        if(root==null) {
            return;
        }
        A[k] = root;
        if(k > size){
            size = k;
        }
        traverse(root.left, 2 * k);
        traverse(root.right, 2 * k + 1);
    }
    public CBTInserter(TreeNode root) {
        A = new TreeNode[11004];
        size = 0;
        traverse(root,1);
    }
    public int insert(int v) {
        size++;
        A[size]=new TreeNode(v);
        if(size % 2 == 0){
            A[size / 2].left = A[size];
        }
        else{
            A[size / 2].right = A[size];
        }
        return A[size / 2].val;
    }
    public TreeNode get_root() {
        return A[1];
    }
}
```

- Iterative DFS with explicit stack avoids O(h) call stack overhead.
- Morris traversal achieves O(1) space inorder traversal without stack or recursion.

---

#### #938. Range Sum of BST

[leetcode.com/problems/range-sum-of-bst/](https://leetcode.com/problems/range-sum-of-bst/) · `O(log n) / O(1)`

**Q:** Return the sum of all BST node values within the range [low, high].

**Example:** `root = [10,5,15,3,7,null,18], low = 7, high = 15` → `32`

**Algorithm:**
1. Set left=0, right=n-1 (or on answer space).
2. Compute mid = left + (right-left)/2 to avoid overflow.
3. If nums[mid] satisfies condition: lock bound (shrink toward answer).
4. Else: eliminate that half.
5. Return left (or right) after loop.
`O(log n) / O(1)`

```java
class Solution {
    public int rangeSumBST(TreeNode root, int low, int high) {
        int sum = 0;
        Queue<TreeNode> queue = new LinkedList<>();
        // Deque<TreeNode> queue = new ArrayDeque<>();
        queue.offer(root);
        while(!queue.isEmpty()) {
            TreeNode current = queue.poll();
            if(current == null) {
                continue;
            }
            if(current.val > high) {
                queue.offer(current.left);
            } else if(current.val < low) {
                queue.offer(current.right);
            } else {
                sum += current.val;
                queue.offer(current.left);
                queue.offer(current.right);
            }
        }
        return sum;
    }
}
class Solution {
    public int rangeSumBST(TreeNode root, int low, int high) {
        if(root == null) {
            return 0;
        }
        if(root.val < low) {
            return rangeSumBST(root.right, low, high);
        } else if(root.val > high) {
            return rangeSumBST(root.left, low, high);
        } else {
            return root.val + rangeSumBST(root.right, low, high) + rangeSumBST(root.left, low, high);
        }
    }
}
```

- Iterative DFS with explicit stack avoids O(h) call stack overhead.
- Morris traversal achieves O(1) space inorder traversal without stack or recursion.

#### #951. Flip Equivalent Binary Trees

[leetcode.com/problems/flip-equivalent-binary-trees/](https://leetcode.com/problems/flip-equivalent-binary-trees/) · `O(n) / O(h)`

**Q:** Check if two binary trees are flip-equivalent (can make them identical by flipping children).

**Example:** `root1 = [1,2,3,4,5,6], root2 = [1,3,2,null,6,4,5]` → `true`

**Algorithm:**
1. Base case: return default value when node is null.
2. Recurse into left subtree to get left result.
3. Recurse into right subtree to get right result.
4. Combine left and right results at current node and return.
`O(n) / O(h)`

```java
class Solution {
    public boolean flipEquiv(TreeNode root1, TreeNode root2) {
        if(root1 == null && root2 == null) {
            return true;
        }
        if(root1 == null || root2 == null) {
            return false;
        }
        if(root1.val != root2.val) {
            return false;
        }
        boolean option1 = flipEquiv(root1.left, root2.left) && flipEquiv(root1.right, root2.right);
        boolean option2 = flipEquiv(root1.left, root2.right) && flipEquiv(root1.right, root2.left);
        return option1 || option2;
    }
}
```

- Iterative DFS with explicit stack avoids O(h) call stack overhead.
- Morris traversal achieves O(1) space inorder traversal without stack or recursion.

#### #958. Check Completeness of a Binary Tree

[leetcode.com/problems/check-completeness-of-a-binary-tree/](https://leetcode.com/problems/check-completeness-of-a-binary-tree/) · `O(n) / O(h)`

**Q:** Check if a binary tree is a complete binary tree.

**Example:** `root = [1,2,3,4,5,6]` → `true`

**Algorithm:**
1. Base case: return default value when node is null.
2. Recurse into left subtree to get left result.
3. Recurse into right subtree to get right result.
4. Combine left and right results at current node and return.
`O(n) / O(h)`

```java
class Solution {
    public boolean isCompleteTree(TreeNode root) {
        List<ANode> nodes = new ArrayList();
        nodes.add(new ANode(root, 1));
        int i = 0;
        while (i < nodes.size()) {
            ANode anode = nodes.get(i++);
            if (anode.node != null) {
                nodes.add(new ANode(anode.node.left, anode.code * 2));
                nodes.add(new ANode(anode.node.right, anode.code * 2 + 1));
            }
        }
        return nodes.get(i-1).code == nodes.size();
    }
}
class ANode {  // Annotated Node
    TreeNode node;
    int code;
    ANode(TreeNode node, int code) {
        this.node = node;
        this.code = code;
    }
}
```

- Iterative DFS with explicit stack avoids O(h) call stack overhead.
- Morris traversal achieves O(1) space inorder traversal without stack or recursion.

#### #987. Vertical Order Traversal of a Binary Tree

[leetcode.com/problems/vertical-order-traversal-of-a-binary-tree/](https://leetcode.com/problems/vertical-order-traversal-of-a-binary-tree/) · `O(n log n) / O(1)`

**Q:** Return node values grouped by vertical position (column), then by row, then by value.

**Example:** `root = [1,2,3,4,5,6,7]` → `[[4],[2],[1,5,6],[3],[7]]`

**Algorithm:**
1. Base case: return default value when node is null.
2. Recurse into left subtree to get left result.
3. Recurse into right subtree to get right result.
4. Combine left and right results at current node and return.
`O(n log n) / O(1)`

```java
class Solution {
    //  col row value
    Map<TreeNode, int[]> map = new HashMap<>(); // col, row, val
    public List<List<Integer>> verticalTraversal(TreeNode root) {
        map.put(root, new int[]{0, 0, root.val});
        dfs(root);
        List<int[]> list = new ArrayList<>(map.values());
        Collections.sort(list, (a, b)->{
            if (a[0] != b[0]) return a[0] - b[0];
            if (a[1] != b[1]) return a[1] - b[1];
            return a[2] - b[2];
        });
        int n = list.size();
        List<List<Integer>> result = new ArrayList<>();
        for (int i = 0; i < n; ) {
            int j = i;
            List<Integer> temp = new ArrayList<>();
            while (j < n && list.get(j)[0] == list.get(i)[0]) temp.add(list.get(j++)[2]);
            result.add(temp);
            i = j;
        }
        return result;
    }
    void dfs(TreeNode root) {
        if (root == null) return ;
        int[] info = map.get(root);
        int col = info[0], row = info[1], val = info[2];
        if (root.left != null) {
            map.put(root.left, new int[]{col - 1, row + 1, root.left.val});
            dfs(root.left);
        }
        if (root.right != null) {
            map.put(root.right, new int[]{col + 1, row + 1, root.right.val});
            dfs(root.right);
        }
    }
}
class Solution {
    Map<Integer, Map<Integer, List<Integer>>> col2row2nodes = new HashMap<>();
    public List<List<Integer>> verticalTraversal(TreeNode root) {
        List<List<Integer>> result = new ArrayList<>();
        dfs(root, 0, 0);
        List<Integer> colList = new ArrayList<>(col2row2nodes.keySet());
        Collections.sort(colList);
        for(int col: colList){
            Map<Integer, List<Integer>> row2nodes = col2row2nodes.get(col);
            List<Integer> rowList = new ArrayList<>(row2nodes.keySet()), current = new ArrayList<>();
            Collections.sort(rowList);
            for(int row: rowList){
                List<Integer> nodes = row2nodes.get(row);
                Collections.sort(nodes);
                current.addAll(nodes);
            }
            result.add(current);
        }
        return result;
    }
    public void dfs(TreeNode root, int col, int row){
        if(root==null)
        return;
        Map<Integer, List<Integer>> row2nodes = col2row2nodes.getOrDefault(col, new HashMap<>());
        List<Integer> nodes = row2nodes.getOrDefault(row, new ArrayList<>());
        nodes.add(root.val);
        row2nodes.put(row, nodes);
        col2row2nodes.put(col, row2nodes);
        dfs(root.left, col-1, row+1);
        dfs(root.right, col+1, row+1);
    }
}
```

- Return early on first HashMap hit rather than scanning full input.

---

#### #988. Smallest String Starting From Leaf

[leetcode.com/problems/smallest-string-starting-from-leaf/](https://leetcode.com/problems/smallest-string-starting-from-leaf/) · `O(n) / O(h)`

**Q:** Find the lexicographically smallest string from root-to-leaf path.

**Example:** `root = [1,2,3,null,null,4,null,null,6], k = 2` → `"bacd"`

**Algorithm:**
1. Base case: return default value when node is null.
2. Recurse into left subtree to get left result.
3. Recurse into right subtree to get right result.
4. Combine left and right results at current node and return.
`O(n) / O(h)`

```java
class Solution {
    String result = "z";
    public String smallestFromLeaf(TreeNode root) {
        dfs(root, new StringBuilder());
        return result;
    }
    public void dfs(TreeNode node, StringBuilder builder) {
        if (node == null) {
            return;
        }
        builder.append((char) ('a' + node.val));
        if (node.left == null && node.right == null) {
            String current = builder.reverse().toString();
            System.out.println(current);
            builder.reverse();
            if (current.compareTo(result) < 0) {
                result = current;
                System.out.println(result);
            }
        }
        dfs(node.left, builder);
        dfs(node.right, builder);
        builder.deleteCharAt(builder.length() - 1);
    }
}
```

- Iterative DFS with explicit stack avoids O(h) call stack overhead.
- Morris traversal achieves O(1) space inorder traversal without stack or recursion.

#### #993. Cousins in Binary Tree

[leetcode.com/problems/cousins-in-binary-tree/](https://leetcode.com/problems/cousins-in-binary-tree/) · `O(n) / O(h)`

**Q:** Check if two nodes in a binary tree are cousins (same depth, different parents).

**Example:** `root = [1,2,3,4], x = 4, y = 3` → `false`

**Algorithm:**
1. Init queue with root node.
2. Process level by level: snapshot queue.size() at start of each level.
3. Poll node, process value, enqueue non-null children.
4. Collect/aggregate results per level or across all nodes.
`O(n) / O(h)`

```java
class Solution {
    public boolean isCousins(TreeNode root, int x, int y) {
        int xDepth = -1;
        TreeNode xParent = null;
        int yDepth = -1;
        TreeNode yParent = null;
        Deque<TreeNode> queue = new ArrayDeque<>();
        queue.offer(root);
        for(int level = 1; !queue.isEmpty(); level++) {
            for(int i = queue.size(); i > 0; i--) {
                TreeNode current = queue.poll();
                if(current.left != null) {
                    queue.offer(current.left);
                    if(current.left.val == x) {
                        xDepth = level + 1;
                        xParent = current;
                    }
                    if(current.left.val == y) {
                        yDepth = level + 1;
                        yParent = current;
                    }
                }
                if(current.right != null) {
                    queue.offer(current.right);
                    if(current.right.val == x) {
                        xDepth = level + 1;
                        xParent = current;
                    }
                    if(current.right.val == y) {
                        yDepth = level + 1;
                        yParent = current;
                    }
                }
            }
        }
        if(xDepth == -1 || yDepth == -1) {
            return false;
        }
        System.out.println(xDepth + " " + yDepth);
        return xParent != yParent && xDepth == yDepth;
    }
}
```

- Iterative DFS with explicit stack avoids O(h) call stack overhead.
- Morris traversal achieves O(1) space inorder traversal without stack or recursion.

#### #1008. Construct Binary Search Tree from Preorder Traversal

[leetcode.com/problems/construct-binary-search-tree-from-preorder-traversal/](https://leetcode.com/problems/construct-binary-search-tree-from-preorder-traversal/) · `O(log n) / O(1)`

**Q:** Construct a BST from its preorder traversal.

**Algorithm:**
1. Set left=0, right=n-1 (or on answer space).
2. Compute mid = left + (right-left)/2 to avoid overflow.
3. If nums[mid] satisfies condition: lock bound (shrink toward answer).
4. Else: eliminate that half.
5. Return left (or right) after loop.
`O(log n) / O(1)`

```java
class Solution {
    public TreeNode bstFromPreorder(int[] preorder) {
        if(preorder == null || preorder.length == 0) {
            return null;
        }
        return bstFromPreorder(preorder, 0, preorder.length - 1);
    }
    public TreeNode bstFromPreorder(int[] preorder, int start, int end) {
        if(start > end) {
            return null;
        }
        TreeNode root = new TreeNode(preorder[start]);
        if(start == end) {
            return root;
        }
        int left = start + 1;
        int right = end;
        int index = end;
        while(left <= right) {
            int mid = left + (right - left) / 2;
            if(preorder[mid] < root.val) {
                index = mid;
                left = mid + 1;
            } else {
                right = mid -1;
            }
        }
        System.out.println(root.val);
        root.left = bstFromPreorder(preorder, start + 1, index);
        root.right = bstFromPreorder(preorder, index + 1, end);
        return root;
    }
}
public class Solution {
    public TreeNode bstFromPreorder(int[] preorder) {
        int len = preorder.length;
        if (len == 0) {
            return null;
        }
        return dfs(preorder, 0, len - 1);
    }
    private TreeNode dfs(int[] preorder, int left, int right) {
        if (left > right) {
            return null;
        }
        TreeNode root = new TreeNode(preorder[left]);
        if (left == right) {
            return root;
        }
        //  [left..right]  preorder[left]
        //  left  left + 1
        //  2  [left, right]  1  2  right
        int l = left;
        int r = right;
        while (l < r) {
            int mid = l + (r - l + 1) / 2;
            if (preorder[mid] < preorder[left]) {
                //  [mid, r]
                l = mid;
            } else {
                //  [l, mid - 1]
                r = mid - 1;
            }
        }
        TreeNode leftTree = dfs(preorder, left + 1, l);
        TreeNode rightTree = dfs(preorder, l + 1, right);
        root.left = leftTree;
        root.right = rightTree;
        return root;
    }
}
public class Solution {
    private int index = 0;
    private int[] preorder;
    private int len;
    /**
    *
    *
    * @param preorder
    * @return
    */
    public TreeNode bstFromPreorder(int[] preorder) {
        this.preorder = preorder;
        this.len = preorder.length;
        return dfs(Integer.MIN_VALUE, Integer.MAX_VALUE);
    }
    /**
    *
    *
    * @param lowerBound
    * @param upperBound
    * @return
    */
    private TreeNode dfs(int lowerBound, int upperBound) {
        //
        if (index == len) {
            return null;
        }
        int current = preorder[index];
        if (current < lowerBound || current > upperBound) {
            return null;
        }
        index++;
        TreeNode root = new TreeNode(current);
        root.left = dfs(lowerBound, current);
        root.right = dfs(current, upperBound);
        return root;
    }
}
public class Solution {
    public TreeNode bstFromPreorder(int[] preorder) {
        int len = preorder.length;
        if (len == 0) {
            return null;
        }
        TreeNode root = new TreeNode(preorder[0]);
        Deque<TreeNode> stack = new ArrayDeque<>();
        stack.push(root);
        for (int i = 1; i < len; i++) {
            //
            TreeNode parent = stack.peek();
            TreeNode current = new TreeNode(preorder[i]);
            // <>
            while (!stack.isEmpty() && stack.peek().val < current.val) {
                parent = stack.pop();
            }
            if (parent.val < current.val) {
                parent.right = current;
            } else {
                parent.left = current;
            }
            stack.push(current);
        }
        return root;
    }
}
```

- Iterative DFS with explicit stack avoids O(h) call stack overhead.
- Morris traversal achieves O(1) space inorder traversal without stack or recursion.

---

#### #1026. Maximum Difference Between Node and Ancestor

[leetcode.com/problems/maximum-difference-between-node-and-ancestor/](https://leetcode.com/problems/maximum-difference-between-node-and-ancestor/) · `O(n) / O(h)`

**Q:** Find the maximum difference between a node and its ancestor in a binary tree.

**Algorithm:**
1. Base case: return default value when node is null.
2. Recurse into left subtree to get left result.
3. Recurse into right subtree to get right result.
4. Combine left and right results at current node and return.
`O(n) / O(h)`

```java
class Solution {
    public int maxAncestorDiff(TreeNode root) {
        if (root == null) {
            return 0;
        }
        return dfs(root)[2];
    }
    private int[] dfs(TreeNode root) {// [,]
        int val = root.val;
        int min = val;
        int max = val;
        int result = 0;
        if (root.left != null) {
            int[] info = dfs(root.left);
            min = Math.min(min, info[0]);
            max = Math.max(max, info[1]);
            result = Math.max(info[2], Math.max(Math.abs(val - info[0]), Math.abs(val - info[1])));
        }
        if (root.right != null) {
            int[] info = dfs(root.right);
            min = Math.min(min, info[0]);
            max = Math.max(max, info[1]);
            // ans
            result = Math.max(Math.max(result, info[2]), Math.max(Math.abs(val - info[0]), Math.abs(val - info[1])));
        }
        return new int[] { min, max, result };
    }
}
```

- Iterative DFS with explicit stack avoids O(h) call stack overhead.
- Morris traversal achieves O(1) space inorder traversal without stack or recursion.
