#### #1038. Binary Search Tree to Greater Sum Tree

[leetcode.com/problems/binary-search-tree-to-greater-sum-tree/](https://leetcode.com/problems/binary-search-tree-to-greater-sum-tree/) · `O(n) / O(h)`

**Q:** Convert each BST node to the sum of all nodes with a greater or equal value.

**Algorithm:**
1. Base case: return default value when node is null.
2. Recurse into left subtree to get left result.
3. Recurse into right subtree to get right result.
4. Combine left and right results at current node and return.
`O(n) / O(h)`

```java
class Solution {
    //
    TreeNode previous = null;
    public TreeNode bstToGst(TreeNode root) {
        dfs(root);
        return root;
    }
    public void dfs(TreeNode current) {
        if(current == null) {
            return ;
        }
        dfs(current.right);
        if(previous != null) {
            current.val += previous.val;
        }
        previous = current;
        dfs(current.left);
    }
}
```

- Iterative DFS with explicit stack avoids O(h) call stack overhead.
- Morris traversal achieves O(1) space inorder traversal without stack or recursion.

#### #1104. Path In Zigzag Labelled Binary Tree

[leetcode.com/problems/path-in-zigzag-labelled-binary-tree/](https://leetcode.com/problems/path-in-zigzag-labelled-binary-tree/) · `O(n) / O(h)`

**Q:** Find the parent node in a zigzag-labeled infinite binary tree.

**Algorithm:**
1. Base case: return default value when node is null.
2. Recurse into left subtree to get left result.
3. Recurse into right subtree to get right result.
4. Combine left and right results at current node and return.
`O(n) / O(h)`

```java
class Solution {
    //  1
    //  i  2^(i-1)
    // i: 2 * 1,  2 * i + 1
    // i, : i / 2
    public List<Integer> pathInZigZagTree(int label) {
        List<Integer> list = getInitPath(label);
        boolean flag = false;
        for(int l = list.size() - 1; l >= 0; l--){
            if(flag){
                int num = reverse(l, list.get(l));
                list.set(l, num);
            }
            flag = !flag;
        }
        return list;
    }
    //
    List<Integer> getInitPath(int label){
        LinkedList<Integer> list = new LinkedList<>();
        while(label > 0){
            list.addFirst(label);
            label = label/2;
        }
        return list;
    }
    // num
    int reverse(int level, int num){
        return (1 << level) + (1 << level + 1) - 1 - num;
    }
}
```

- Iterative DFS with explicit stack avoids O(h) call stack overhead.
- Morris traversal achieves O(1) space inorder traversal without stack or recursion.

#### #1110. Delete Nodes And Return Forest

[leetcode.com/problems/delete-nodes-and-return-forest/](https://leetcode.com/problems/delete-nodes-and-return-forest/) · `O(n) / O(h)`

**Q:** Delete given nodes from a binary tree and return the roots of the remaining forest.

**Algorithm:**
1. Base case: return default value when node is null.
2. Recurse into left subtree to get left result.
3. Recurse into right subtree to get right result.
4. Combine left and right results at current node and return.
`O(n) / O(h)`

```java
class Solution {
    List<TreeNode> forest = new ArrayList<>();
    Set<Integer> set = new HashSet<>();
    public List<TreeNode> delNodes(TreeNode root, int[] to_delete) {
        if(root == null) {
            return new ArrayList<>();
        }
        for(int val : to_delete) {
            set.add(val);
        }
        dfs(root, true);
        return forest;
    }
    public TreeNode dfs(TreeNode root, boolean isRoot) {
        if(root == null) {
            return root;
        }
        boolean deleted = set.contains(root.val);
        if(!deleted && isRoot) {
            forest.add(root);
        }
        root.left = dfs(root.left, deleted);
        root.right = dfs(root.right, deleted);
        return deleted ? null : root;
    }
}
```

- Iterative DFS with explicit stack avoids O(h) call stack overhead.
- Morris traversal achieves O(1) space inorder traversal without stack or recursion.

---

#### #1123. Lowest Common Ancestor of Deepest Leaves

[leetcode.com/problems/lowest-common-ancestor-of-deepest-leaves/](https://leetcode.com/problems/lowest-common-ancestor-of-deepest-leaves/) · `O(n) / O(h)`

**Q:** Find the LCA of the deepest leaves in a binary tree.

**Algorithm:**
1. Base case: return default value when node is null.
2. Recurse into left subtree to get left result.
3. Recurse into right subtree to get right result.
4. Combine left and right results at current node and return.
`O(n) / O(h)`

```java
class Solution {
    int maxDepth = 0;
    TreeNode ancestor = null;
    public TreeNode lcaDeepestLeaves(TreeNode root) {
        dfs(root);
        return ancestor;
    }
    public int dfs(TreeNode root) {
        if(root == null) {
            return 0;
        }
        int left = dfs(root.left);
        int right = dfs(root.right);
        int depth = 1 + Math.max(left, right);
        if(depth > maxDepth) {
            maxDepth = depth;
        }
        if(left == right && depth == maxDepth) {
            ancestor = root;
        }
        // System.out.println(root.val + " " + depth);
        return depth;
    }
}
```

- Iterative DFS with explicit stack avoids O(h) call stack overhead.
- Morris traversal achieves O(1) space inorder traversal without stack or recursion.

---

#### #1161. Maximum Level Sum of a Binary Tree

[leetcode.com/problems/maximum-level-sum-of-a-binary-tree/](https://leetcode.com/problems/maximum-level-sum-of-a-binary-tree/) · `O(n) / O(h)`

**Q:** Find the level of a binary tree with the maximum sum of node values.

**Algorithm:**
1. Init queue with root node.
2. Process level by level: snapshot queue.size() at start of each level.
3. Poll node, process value, enqueue non-null children.
4. Collect/aggregate results per level or across all nodes.
`O(n) / O(h)`

```java
class Solution {
    public int maxLevelSum(TreeNode root) {
        if(root == null) {
            return 0;
        }
        Deque<TreeNode> queue = new ArrayDeque<>();
        queue.offer(root);
        int minLevel = -1;
        int maxSum = Integer.MIN_VALUE;
        for(int level = 1; !queue.isEmpty(); level++) {
            int sum = 0;
            for(int i = queue.size(); i > 0; i--) {
                TreeNode current = queue.poll();
                sum += current.val;
                if(current.left != null) {
                    queue.offer(current.left);
                }
                if(current.right != null) {
                    queue.offer(current.right);
                }
            }
            // System.out.println(sum + " " + level);
            if(sum > maxSum) {
                minLevel = level;
                maxSum = sum;
            }
        }
        return minLevel;
    }
}
```

- Iterative DFS with explicit stack avoids O(h) call stack overhead.
- Morris traversal achieves O(1) space inorder traversal without stack or recursion.

---

#### #1214. Two Sum BSTs

[leetcode.com/problems/two-sum-bsts/](https://leetcode.com/problems/two-sum-bsts/) · `O(n) / O(h)`

**Algorithm:**
1. Base case: return default value when node is null.
2. Recurse into left subtree to get left result.
3. Recurse into right subtree to get right result.
4. Combine left and right results at current node and return.
`O(n) / O(h)`

```java
class Solution {
    public boolean twoSumBSTs(TreeNode root1, TreeNode root2, int target) {
        if(root1 == null || root2 == null) {
            return false;
        }
        Set<Integer> set = generateSet(root1);
        TreeNode current = root2;
        Deque<TreeNode> stack = new ArrayDeque<>();
        while(current != null || !stack.isEmpty()) {
            while(current != null) {
                stack.push(current);
                current = current.left;
            }
            current = stack.pop();
            if(set.contains(target - current.val)) {
                return true;
            }
            current = current.right;
        }
        return false;
    }
    public Set<Integer> generateSet(TreeNode root) {
        TreeNode current = root;
        Set<Integer> set = new HashSet<>();
        Deque<TreeNode> stack = new ArrayDeque<>();
        while(current != null || !stack.isEmpty()) {
            while(current != null) {
                stack.push(current);
                current = current.left;
            }
            current = stack.pop();
            set.add(current.val);
            current = current.right;
        }
        return set;
    }
}
class Solution {
    public boolean twoSumBSTs(TreeNode root1, TreeNode root2, int target) {
        if(root1 == null || root2 == null) {
            return false;
        }
        TreeNode current1 = root1;
        TreeNode current2 = root2;
        Deque<TreeNode> stack1 = new ArrayDeque<>();
        Deque<TreeNode> stack2 = new ArrayDeque<>();
        while((current1 != null || !stack1.isEmpty()) && (current2 != null || !stack2.isEmpty())) {
            while(current1 != null) {
                stack1.push(current1);
                current1 = current1.left;
            }
            while(current2 != null) {
                stack2.push(current2);
                current2 = current2.right;
            }
            int sum = stack1.peek().val + stack2.peek().val;
            if(sum == target) {
                return true;
            } else if(sum > target) {
                current2 = stack2.pop();
                current2 = current2.left;
            } else {
                current1 = stack1.pop();
                current1 = current1.right;
            }
        }
        return false;
    }
}
```

- Iterative DFS with explicit stack avoids O(h) call stack overhead.
- Morris traversal achieves O(1) space inorder traversal without stack or recursion.

#### #1305. All Elements in Two Binary Search Trees

[leetcode.com/problems/all-elements-in-two-binary-search-trees/](https://leetcode.com/problems/all-elements-in-two-binary-search-trees/) · `O(n) / O(h)`

**Q:** Return all elements from two BSTs in sorted order.

**Algorithm:**
1. Base case: return default value when node is null.
2. Recurse into left subtree to get left result.
3. Recurse into right subtree to get right result.
4. Combine left and right results at current node and return.
`O(n) / O(h)`

```java
class Solution {
    public List<Integer> getAllElements(TreeNode root1, TreeNode root2) {
        List<Integer> list1 = new ArrayList<>();
        List<Integer> list2 = new ArrayList<>();
        dfs(root1, list1);
        dfs(root2, list2);
        List<Integer> result = merge(list1, list2);
        return result;
    }
    public void dfs(TreeNode root, List<Integer> list) {
        if(root == null) {
            return ;
        }
        dfs(root.left, list);
        list.add(root.val);
        dfs(root.right, list);
    }
    public List<Integer> merge(List<Integer> list1, List<Integer> list2) {
        List<Integer> result = new ArrayList<>();
        int i = 0, j = 0;
        while(i < list1.size() || j < list2.size()) {
            if(i < list1.size() && j < list2.size()) {
                if(list1.get(i) <= list2.get(j)) {
                    result.add(list1.get(i));
                    i++;
                } else {
                    result.add(list2.get(j));
                    j++;
                }
            } else if(i < list1.size()) {
                result.add(list1.get(i));
                i++;
            } else {
                result.add(list2.get(j));
                j++;
            }
        }
        return result;
    }
}
```

- Iterative DFS with explicit stack avoids O(h) call stack overhead.
- Morris traversal achieves O(1) space inorder traversal without stack or recursion.

#### #1361. Validate Binary Tree Nodes

[leetcode.com/problems/validate-binary-tree-nodes/](https://leetcode.com/problems/validate-binary-tree-nodes/) · `O(n) / O(h)`

**Algorithm:**
1. Base case: return default value when node is null.
2. Recurse into left subtree to get left result.
3. Recurse into right subtree to get right result.
4. Combine left and right results at current node and return.
`O(n) / O(h)`

```java
class Solution {
    boolean flag = false;
    int count = 0;
    public boolean validateBinaryTreeNodes(int n, int[] leftChild, int[] rightChild) {
        int[] inDegree = new int[n];
        for(int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
            if(leftChild[i] != -1) {
                inDegree[leftChild[i]]++;
            }
            if(rightChild[i] != -1) {
                inDegree[rightChild[i]]++;
            }
        }
        int root = -1;
        for(int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
            if(inDegree[i] == 0) {
                if(root == -1) {
                    root = i;
                } else {
                    return false;
                }
            }
        }
        if(root == -1) {
            return false;
        }
        boolean[] visited = new boolean[n];
        dfs(root, leftChild, rightChild, visited);
        return flag ? false : count == n;
    }
    public void dfs(int current, int[] leftChild, int[] rightChild, boolean[] visited) {
        if(visited[current]) {
            flag = true;
        }
        if(flag) {
            return ;
        }
        visited[current] = true;
        count++;
        if(leftChild[current] != -1) {
            dfs(leftChild[current], leftChild, rightChild, visited);
        }
        if(rightChild[current] != -1) {
            dfs(rightChild[current], leftChild, rightChild, visited);
        }
    }
}
```

- Iterative DFS with explicit stack avoids O(h) call stack overhead.
- Morris traversal achieves O(1) space inorder traversal without stack or recursion.

#### #1382. Balance a Binary Search Tree

[leetcode.com/problems/balance-a-binary-search-tree/](https://leetcode.com/problems/balance-a-binary-search-tree/) · `O(log n) / O(1)`

**Q:** Convert a BST to a balanced BST.

**Algorithm:**
1. Set left=0, right=n-1 (or on answer space).
2. Compute mid = left + (right-left)/2 to avoid overflow.
3. If nums[mid] satisfies condition: lock bound (shrink toward answer).
4. Else: eliminate that half.
5. Return left (or right) after loop.
`O(log n) / O(1)`

```java
class Solution {
    public TreeNode balanceBST(TreeNode root) {
        List<Integer> inorderSequence = new ArrayList<>();
        dfs(root, inorderSequence);
        return buildTree(inorderSequence, 0, inorderSequence.size() - 1);
    }
    public void dfs(TreeNode root, List<Integer> inorderSequence) {
        if(root == null) {
            return ;
        }
        dfs(root.left, inorderSequence);
        inorderSequence.add(root.val);
        dfs(root.right, inorderSequence);
    }
    public TreeNode buildTree(List<Integer> inorderSequence, int left, int right) {
        if(left > right) {
            return null;
        }
        int mid = left + (right - left) / 2;
        TreeNode root = new TreeNode(inorderSequence.get(mid));
        root.left = buildTree(inorderSequence, left, mid - 1);
        root.right = buildTree(inorderSequence, mid + 1, right);
        return root;
    }
}
class Solution {
    int i = 0;
    public TreeNode balanceBST(TreeNode root) {
        List<Integer> inorderSequence = new ArrayList<>();
        dfs(root, inorderSequence);
        return buildTree(inorderSequence, 0, inorderSequence.size() - 1);
    }
    public void dfs(TreeNode root, List<Integer> inorderSequence) {
        if(root == null) {
            return ;
        }
        dfs(root.left, inorderSequence);
        inorderSequence.add(root.val);
        dfs(root.right, inorderSequence);
    }
    public TreeNode buildTree(List<Integer> inorderSequence, int start, int end) {
        // range: [start, end]
        if(start > end) {
            return null;
        }
        int mid = start + (end - start) / 2;
        TreeNode root = new TreeNode();
        root.left = buildTree(inorderSequence, start, mid - 1);
        root.val = inorderSequence.get(i++);
        root.right = buildTree(inorderSequence, mid + 1, end);
        return root;
    }
}
```

- Iterative DFS with explicit stack avoids O(h) call stack overhead.
- Morris traversal achieves O(1) space inorder traversal without stack or recursion.

#### #1522. Diameter of N-Ary Tree

[leetcode.com/problems/diameter-of-nary-tree/](https://leetcode.com/problems/diameter-of-nary-tree/) · `O(n) / O(h)`

**Q:** Find the diameter of an N-ary tree.

**Algorithm:**
1. Base case: return default value when node is null.
2. Recurse into left subtree to get left result.
3. Recurse into right subtree to get right result.
4. Combine left and right results at current node and return.
`O(n) / O(h)`

```java
class Solution {
    private int longestPath = -1;
    public int diameter(Node root) {
        maxDepth(root);
        return longestPath;
    }
    private int maxDepth(Node root) {
        if(root == null) {
            return 0;
        }
        int largest = -1;
        int secondLargset = -1;
        for(Node neighbor : root.children) {
            int depth = maxDepth(neighbor);
            if(depth > largest) {
                secondLargset = largest;
                largest = depth;
            } else if(depth > secondLargset) {
                secondLargset = depth;
            }
        }
        longestPath = Math.max(longestPath, largest + secondLargset + 2);
        return largest + 1;
    }
}
```

- Iterative DFS with explicit stack avoids O(h) call stack overhead.
- Morris traversal achieves O(1) space inorder traversal without stack or recursion.

### Dynamic Programming (81 problems)

#### #5. Longest Palindromic Substring

[leetcode.com/problems/longest-palindromic-substring/](https://leetcode.com/problems/longest-palindromic-substring/) · `O(n^2) / O(n^2)`

**Q:** Find the longest palindromic substring within a given string.

**Example:** `s = "babad"` → `"bab"`

**Algorithm:**
1. DP: build isPalin[i][j] = (s[i]==s[j]) && isPalin[i+1][j-1].
2. Iterate length from 1 to n; track longest palindrome found.
3. Alt: Expand-from-center O(n^2) time O(1) space.
`O(n^2) / O(n^2)`

```java
class Solution {
    public String longestPalindrome(String s) {
        if(s == null || s.length() == 0) {
            return "";
        }
        int n = s.length();
        boolean[][] f = new boolean[n][n];
        for (int i = 0; i < n; ++i) {
            Arrays.fill(f[i], true);
        }
        for (int i = n - 1; i >= 0; i--) {
            for (int j = i + 1; j < n; j++) {
                f[i][j] = (s.charAt(i) == s.charAt(j)) && f[i + 1][j - 1];
            }
        }
        int maxLen = 0;
        String result = "";
        for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
            for (int j = i; j < n; j++) {
                if(f[i][j] && j - i + 1 > maxLen) {
                    maxLen = j - i + 1;
                    result = s.substring(i, j+1);
                }
            }
        }
        return result;
    }
}
```

- DP solution uses O(n^2) space; expand-from-center achieves O(1) space with same O(n^2) time.
- Manacher's algorithm achieves O(n) time and O(n) space — optimal for this problem.

---

#### #131. Palindrome Partitioning

[leetcode.com/problems/palindrome-partitioning/](https://leetcode.com/problems/palindrome-partitioning/) · `O(n) / O(n)`

**Q:** Partition a string into all possible subsets where every substring is a palindrome.

**Example:** `s = "aab"` → `[["a","a","b"],["aa","b"]]`

**Algorithm:**
1. Define dp[i] = answer for subproblem of size i.
2. Set base case dp[0] (and dp[1] if needed).
3. Fill dp left to right with recurrence: dp[i] = f(dp[i-1], dp[i-2], ...).
4. Return dp[n] or max/min over dp array.
`O(n) / O(n)`

```java
class Solution {
    boolean[][] f;
    List<List<String>> ret = new ArrayList<>();
    List<String> result = new ArrayList<>();
    int n;
    public List<List<String>> partition(String s) {
        n = s.length();
        f = new boolean[n][n];
        for (int i = 0; i < n; ++i) {
            Arrays.fill(f[i], true);
        }
        for (int i = n - 1; i >= 0; i--) {
            for (int j = i + 1; j < n; j++) {
                f[i][j] = (s.charAt(i) == s.charAt(j)) && f[i + 1][j - 1];
            }
        }
        dfs(s, 0);
        return ret;
    }
    public void dfs(String s, int i) {
        if (i == n) {
            ret.add(new ArrayList<>(result));
            return;
        }
        for (int j = i; j < n; ++j) {
            if (f[i][j]) {
                result.add(s.substring(i, j + 1));
                dfs(s, j + 1);
                result.remove(result.size() - 1);
            }
        }
    }
}
```

- Use 1D rolling array if dp[i] only depends on previous row — reduces space from O(mn) to O(n).

---

#### #132. Palindrome Partitioning II

[leetcode.com/problems/palindrome-partitioning-ii/](https://leetcode.com/problems/palindrome-partitioning-ii/) · `O(log n) / O(1)`

**Q:** Find the minimum number of cuts to partition a string so every part is a palindrome.

**Example:** `s = "aab"` → `1`

**Algorithm:**
1. Set left=0, right=n-1 (or on answer space).
2. Compute mid = left + (right-left)/2 to avoid overflow.
3. If nums[mid] satisfies condition: lock bound (shrink toward answer).
4. Else: eliminate that half.
5. Return left (or right) after loop.
`O(log n) / O(1)`

```java
//
//
// dp
class Solution {
    public int minCut(String s) {
        int n = s.length();
        boolean[][] g = new boolean[n][n];
        for (int i = 0; i < n; ++i) {
            Arrays.fill(g[i], true);
        }
        for (int i = n - 1; i >= 0; --i) {
            for (int j = i + 1; j < n; ++j) {
                g[i][j] = s.charAt(i) == s.charAt(j) && g[i + 1][j - 1];
            }
        }
        int[] f = new int[n];
        Arrays.fill(f, Integer.MAX_VALUE);
        for (int i = 0; i < n; ++i) {
            if (g[0][i]) {
                f[i] = 0;
            } else {
                for (int j = 0; j < i; ++j) {
                    if (g[j + 1][i]) {
                        f[i] = Math.min(f[i], f[j] + 1);
                    }
                }
            }
        }
        return f[n - 1];
    }
}
01.06. Compress String LCCI
class Solution {
    public String compressString(String S) {
        if(S == null || S.length() == 0) {
            return S;
        }
        char[] arr = S.toCharArray();
        StringBuilder builder = new StringBuilder();
        int i = 0;
        while(i < arr.length) {
            char ch = arr[i];
            int count = 0;
            while(i < arr.length && arr[i] == ch) {
                i++;
                count++;
            }
            builder.append(ch).append(count);
        }
        return builder.length() >= S.length() ? S : builder.toString();
    }
}
10.03. Search Rotate Array LCCI
class Solution {
    public int search(int[] nums, int target) {
        int left = 0;
        int right = nums.length - 1;
        if (right == -1)
        return -1;
        while (left < right) {                                         // left==right
            int mid = left + (right - left) / 2;
            if (nums[left] < nums[mid]) {                              //
                if (nums[left] <= target && target <= nums[mid]) {     // mid
                    right = mid;
                } else {                                               // mid+1
                    left = mid + 1;
                }
            } else if (nums[left] > nums[mid]) {                       //
                if (nums[left] <= target || target <= nums[mid]) {     // mid
                    right = mid;
                } else {                                               // mid+1
                    left = mid + 1;
                }
            } else if (nums[left] == nums[mid]) {                      //
                if (nums[left] != target) {                            //
                    left++;
                } else {                                               //
                    right = left;                                      // left
                }
            }
        }
        return (nums[left] == target) ? left : -1;                     // left-1
    }
}
```

- Use 1D rolling array if dp[i] only depends on previous row — reduces space from O(mn) to O(n).

---

#### #516. Longest Palindromic Subsequence

[leetcode.com/problems/longest-palindromic-subsequence/](https://leetcode.com/problems/longest-palindromic-subsequence/) · `O(mn) / O(mn)`

**Q:** Find the length of the longest palindromic subsequence in a string.

**Example:** `s = "bbbab"` → `4`

**Algorithm:**
1. Define dp[i][j] = solution for subproblem on s1[0..i-1], s2[0..j-1].
2. Set base cases: dp[0][j] and dp[i][0].
3. Fill row by row: if chars match → dp[i][j]=dp[i-1][j-1]+1; else take max/min of neighbors.
4. Return dp[m][n].
`O(mn) / O(mn)`

```java
class Solution {
    public int longestPalindromeSubseq(String s) {
        int n = s.length();
        int[][] dp = new int[n][n];
        // dp[i][j]: [i,j]
        for (int i = n - 1; i >= 0; i--) {
            dp[i][i] = 1;
            for (int j = i + 1; j < n; j++) {
                // (i, j-1) (i, j)
                // (i+1, j-1) (i+1, j)
                if (s.charAt(i) == s.charAt(j)) {
                    dp[i][j] = dp[i + 1][j - 1] + 2;
                } else {
                    dp[i][j] = Math.max(dp[i + 1][j], dp[i][j - 1]);
                }
            }
        }
        return dp[0][n - 1];
    }
}
class Solution {
    public int longestPalindromeSubseq(String s) {
        if(s == null || s.length() == 0) {
            return 0;
        }
        int n = s.length();
        int[][] dp = new int[n][n];
        for(int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
            dp[i][i] = 1;
        }
        char[] arr = s.toCharArray();
        for(int len = 2; len <= n; len++) {
            for(int i = 0; i + len -1 < n; i++) {
                // j - i + 1 = len, j = len + i - 1
                int j = i + len - 1;
                if(arr[i] == arr[j]) {
                    dp[i][j] = dp[i+1][j-1] + 2;
                } else {
                    dp[i][j] = Math.max(dp[i][j-1], dp[i+1][j]);
                }
            }
        }
        return dp[0][n-1];
    }
}
```

- Use 1D rolling array if dp[i] only depends on previous row — reduces space from O(mn) to O(n).

---

#### #647. Palindromic Substrings

[leetcode.com/problems/palindromic-substrings/](https://leetcode.com/problems/palindromic-substrings/) · `O(n^2) / O(1)`

**Q:** Count all substrings of a string that are palindromes.

**Example:** `s = "abc"` → `3`

**Algorithm:**
1. For each center (odd and even length), expand while chars match.
2. Count every palindrome found during expansion.
`O(n^2) / O(1)`

```java
class Solution {
    public int countSubstrings(String s) {
        if(s == null || s.length() == 0) {
            return 0;
        }
        int n = s.length();
        boolean[][] f = new boolean[n][n];
        for (int i = 0; i < n; ++i) {
            Arrays.fill(f[i], true);
        }
        for (int i = n - 1; i >= 0; i--) {
            for (int j = i + 1; j < n; j++) {
                f[i][j] = (s.charAt(i) == s.charAt(j)) && f[i + 1][j - 1];
            }
        }
        int count = 0;
        for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
            for (int j = i; j < n; j++) {
                if(f[i][j]) {
                    count++;
                }
            }
        }
        return count;
    }
}
```

- Manacher's algorithm achieves O(n) — optimal but complex. Expand-from-center O(n^2) is standard.
