#### #171. Excel Sheet Column Number

[leetcode.com/problems/excel-sheet-column-number/](https://leetcode.com/problems/excel-sheet-column-number/) · `O(n) / O(n)`

**Q:** Convert an Excel column title (A→1, Z→26, AA→27) to its integer number.

**Example:** `columnTitle = "A"` → `1`

**Algorithm:**
1. Identify string operation: search, transform, validate, or parse.
2. Use appropriate data structure: char array, HashMap, stack, or two pointers.
3. Process characters with relevant conditions.
4. Build or return result.
`O(n) / O(n)`

```java
class Solution {
    public int titleToNumber(String columnTitle) {
        int result = 0;
        for(char ch : columnTitle.toCharArray()) {
            result = result * 26 + (int) (ch - 'A' + 1);
        }
        return result;
    }
}
```

- Use char[] instead of String concatenation in loops — avoids O(n²) copies.
- int[26] char frequency array is faster than HashMap for lowercase-only strings.

#### #179. Largest Number

[leetcode.com/problems/largest-number/](https://leetcode.com/problems/largest-number/) · `O(n log n) / O(1)`

**Q:** Arrange numbers so their concatenation forms the largest possible number.

**Example:** `nums = [3,30,34,5,9]` → `"9534330"`

**Algorithm:**
1. Use StringBuilder for efficient string building.
2. Process characters left to right; apply transformation rules.
3. Append to builder; handle edge cases (spaces, special chars).
4. Return builder.toString().
`O(n log n) / O(1)`

```java
class Solution {
    public String largestNumber(int[] nums) {
        int n = nums.length;
        //  Comparator  lambda
        Integer[] numsArr = new Integer[n];
        for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
            numsArr[i] = nums[i];
        }
        Arrays.sort(numsArr, (x, y) -> {
            long sx = 10, sy = 10;
            while (sx <= x) {
                sx *= 10;
            }
            while (sy <= y) {
                sy *= 10;
            }
            // sy * x + y, sx * y + x
            //
            return (int) (-sy * x - y + sx * y + x);
        });
        if (numsArr[0] == 0) {
            return "0";
        }
        StringBuilder builder = new StringBuilder();
        for (int num : numsArr) {
            builder.append(num);
        }
        return builder.toString();
    }
}
Offer 45.
class Solution {
    public String minNumber(int[] nums) {
        int n = nums.length;
        //  Comparator  lambda
        Integer[] numsArr = new Integer[n];
        for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
            numsArr[i] = nums[i];
        }
        Arrays.sort(numsArr, (x, y) -> {
            long sx = 10, sy = 10;
            while (sx <= x) {
                sx *= 10;
            }
            while (sy <= y) {
                sy *= 10;
            }
            // sy * x + y, sx * y + x
            //
            return (int) (-sy * x - y + sx * y + x);
        });
        StringBuilder builder = new StringBuilder();
        for (int i = numsArr.length - 1; i >= 0; i--) {
            builder.append(numsArr[i]);
        }
        return builder.toString();
    }
}
```

- After sorting, use binary search (O(log n)) instead of linear scan for lookups.

#### #205. Isomorphic Strings

[leetcode.com/problems/isomorphic-strings/](https://leetcode.com/problems/isomorphic-strings/) · `O(n) / O(n)`

**Q:** Check if two strings follow the same character-substitution pattern (isomorphic).

**Example:** `s = "egg", t = "add"` → `true`

**Algorithm:**
1. Use sliding window with HashMap to track character frequencies.
2. Expand right; when window invalid shrink from left.
3. Track result (max window, min window, count of valid windows).
`O(n) / O(n)`

```java
class Solution {
    public boolean isIsomorphic(String s, String t) {
        Map<Character, Character> s2t = new HashMap<>();
        Map<Character, Character> t2s = new HashMap<>();
        int len = s.length();
        for (int i = 0; i < len; ++i) {
            char x = s.charAt(i), y = t.charAt(i);
            // No two characters may map to the same character
            if ((s2t.containsKey(x) && s2t.get(x) != y) || (t2s.containsKey(y) && t2s.get(y) != x)) {
                return false;
            }
            s2t.put(x, y);
            t2s.put(y, x);
        }
        return true;
    }
}
```

- Return early on first HashMap hit rather than scanning full input.

#### #228. Summary Ranges

[leetcode.com/problems/summary-ranges/](https://leetcode.com/problems/summary-ranges/) · `O(n) / O(n)`

**Example:** `nums = [0,1,2,4,5,7]` → `["0->2","4->5","7"]`

**Algorithm:**
1. Identify string operation: search, transform, validate, or parse.
2. Use appropriate data structure: char array, HashMap, stack, or two pointers.
3. Process characters with relevant conditions.
4. Build or return result.
`O(n) / O(n)`

```java
class Solution {
    public List<String> summaryRanges(int[] nums) {
        if(nums == null || nums.length == 0) {
            return new ArrayList<>();
        }
        List<String> result = new ArrayList<>();
        int i = 0;
        while(i < nums.length) {
            int j = i;
            while(j + 1 < nums.length && nums[j+1] == nums[j] + 1) {
                j++;
            }
            if(j == i) {
                result.add(nums[i] + "");
            } else {
                result.add(nums[i] + "->" + nums[j]);
            }
            i = j + 1;
        }
        return result;
    }
}
```

- Use char[] instead of String concatenation in loops — avoids O(n²) copies.
- int[26] char frequency array is faster than HashMap for lowercase-only strings.

#### #246. Strobogrammatic Number

[leetcode.com/problems/strobogrammatic-number/](https://leetcode.com/problems/strobogrammatic-number/) · `O(n) / O(n)`

**Algorithm:**
1. Identify string operation: search, transform, validate, or parse.
2. Use appropriate data structure: char array, HashMap, stack, or two pointers.
3. Process characters with relevant conditions.
4. Build or return result.
`O(n) / O(n)`

```java
class Solution {
    public boolean isStrobogrammatic(String num) {
        if(num == null) {
            return false;
        }
        if(num.length() == 1) {
            return num.charAt(0) == '1' || num.charAt(0) == '8' || num.charAt(0) == '0';
        }
        int i = 0, j = num.length() - 1;
        char[] arr = num.toCharArray();
        for(i = 0; i <= j; i++, j--) {
            if(arr[i] == '8' && arr[j] == '8') {
                continue;
            }
            if(arr[i] == '6' && arr[j] == '9') {
                continue;
            }
            if(arr[i] == '9' && arr[j] == '6') {
                continue;
            }
            if(arr[i] == '1' && arr[j] == '1') {
                continue;
            }
            if(arr[i] == '0' && arr[j] == '0') {
                continue;
            }
            return false;
        }
        return true;
    }
}
```

- Use char[] instead of String concatenation in loops — avoids O(n²) copies.
- int[26] char frequency array is faster than HashMap for lowercase-only strings.

---

#### #249. Group Shifted Strings

[leetcode.com/problems/group-shifted-strings/](https://leetcode.com/problems/group-shifted-strings/) · `O(n) / O(n)`

**Q:** Group strings that follow the same shift sequence (each char shifted by the same amount).

**Algorithm:**
1. Use sliding window with HashMap to track character frequencies.
2. Expand right; when window invalid shrink from left.
3. Track result (max window, min window, count of valid windows).
`O(n) / O(n)`

```java
class Solution {
    public List<List<String>> groupStrings(String[] strings) {
        Map<String, List<String>> map = new HashMap<>();
        for(String s : strings) {
            String key = generateKey(s);
            map.computeIfAbsent(key, list -> new ArrayList<>()).add(s);
        }
        return new ArrayList<>(map.values());
    }
    private String generateKey(String s) {
        char head = s.charAt(0);
        StringBuilder builder = new StringBuilder();
        for(char ch :s.toCharArray()) {
            int distance = (ch - head + 26) % 26;
            builder.append("#" + distance);
        }
        return builder.toString();
    }
}
```

- Use char[] instead of String concatenation in loops — avoids O(n²) copies.
- int[26] char frequency array is faster than HashMap for lowercase-only strings.

#### #266. Palindrome Permutation

[leetcode.com/problems/palindrome-permutation/](https://leetcode.com/problems/palindrome-permutation/) · `O(n) / O(n)`

**Q:** Check if a string can be rearranged into a palindrome (at most one odd-count char).

**Algorithm:**
1. Use int[26] frequency array for O(1) char counting (lowercase letters).
2. Scan string, increment/decrement counts.
3. Check condition on frequency array (all zeros, single non-zero, etc.).
`O(n) / O(n)`

```java
class Solution {
    public boolean canPermutePalindrome(String s) {
        int[] count =  new int[26];
        for(char ch : s.toCharArray()) {
            count[ch - 'a']++;
        }
        int result = 0;
        for(int i = 0; i < 26; i++) {
            result += count[i] % 2;
        }
        return result <= 1;
    }
}
```

- Use char[] instead of String concatenation in loops — avoids O(n²) copies.
- int[26] char frequency array is faster than HashMap for lowercase-only strings.

#### #271. Encode and Decode Strings

[leetcode.com/problems/encode-and-decode-strings/](https://leetcode.com/problems/encode-and-decode-strings/) · `O(n) / O(n)`

**Q:** Encode a list of strings to a single string and decode it back.

**Algorithm:**
1. Use StringBuilder for efficient string building.
2. Process characters left to right; apply transformation rules.
3. Append to builder; handle edge cases (spaces, special chars).
4. Return builder.toString().
`O(n) / O(n)`

```java
public class Codec {
    //  ASCII
    String regex = Character.toString((char)258);
    public String encode(List<String> strs) {
        if (strs.size() == 0)  {
            return regex;
        }
        String d = regex;
        StringBuilder builder = new StringBuilder();
        for(String s: strs) {
            builder.append(s + regex);
        }
        builder.deleteCharAt(builder.length() - 1);
        return builder.toString();
    }
    // Decodes a single string to a list of strings.
    public List<String> decode(String s) {
        if (s.equals(regex)) {
            return new ArrayList();
        }
        return Arrays.asList(s.split(regex, -1));
    }
}
```

- Use char[] instead of String concatenation in loops — avoids O(n²) copies.
- int[26] char frequency array is faster than HashMap for lowercase-only strings.

#### #273. Integer to English Words

[leetcode.com/problems/integer-to-english-words/](https://leetcode.com/problems/integer-to-english-words/) · `O(n) / O(n)`

**Q:** Convert a non-negative integer to its English words representation.

**Example:** `num = 123` → `"One Hundred Twenty Three"`

**Algorithm:**
1. Use StringBuilder for efficient string building.
2. Process characters left to right; apply transformation rules.
3. Append to builder; handle edge cases (spaces, special chars).
4. Return builder.toString().
`O(n) / O(n)`

```java
class Solution {
    String[] singles = {"", "One", "Two", "Three", "Four", "Five", "Six", "Seven", "Eight", "Nine"};
    String[] teens = {"Ten", "Eleven", "Twelve", "Thirteen", "Fourteen", "Fifteen", "Sixteen", "Seventeen", "Eighteen", "Nineteen"};
    String[] tens = {"", "Ten", "Twenty", "Thirty", "Forty", "Fifty", "Sixty", "Seventy", "Eighty", "Ninety"};
    String[] thousands = {"", "Thousand", "Million", "Billion"};
    public String numberToWords(int num) {
        if (num == 0) {
            return "Zero";
        }
        StringBuffer sb = new StringBuffer();
        for (int i = 3, unit = 1000000000; i >= 0; i--, unit /= 1000) {
            int curNum = num / unit;
            if (curNum != 0) {
                num -= curNum * unit;
                StringBuffer current = new StringBuffer();
                recursion(current, curNum);
                current.append(thousands[i]).append(" ");
                sb.append(current);
            }
        }
        return sb.toString().trim();
    }
    public void recursion(StringBuffer current, int num) {
        if (num == 0) {
            return;
        } else if (num < 10) {
            current.append(singles[num]).append(" ");
        } else if (num < 20) {
            current.append(teens[num - 10]).append(" ");
        } else if (num < 100) {
            current.append(tens[num / 10]).append(" ");
            recursion(current, num % 10);
        } else {
            current.append(singles[num / 100]).append(" Hundred ");
            recursion(current, num % 100);
        }
    }
}
```

- Use char[] instead of String concatenation in loops — avoids O(n²) copies.
- int[26] char frequency array is faster than HashMap for lowercase-only strings.

---

#### #290. Word Pattern

[leetcode.com/problems/word-pattern/](https://leetcode.com/problems/word-pattern/) · `O(n) / O(n)`

**Q:** Check if a string s follows a given pattern (bijective character mapping).

**Example:** `pattern = "abba", s = "dog cat cat dog"` → `true`

**Algorithm:**
1. Use sliding window with HashMap to track character frequencies.
2. Expand right; when window invalid shrink from left.
3. Track result (max window, min window, count of valid windows).
`O(n) / O(n)`

```java
class Solution {
    public boolean wordPattern(String pattern, String s) {
        String[] arr = s.split(" ");
        if(arr.length != pattern.length()) {
            return false;
        }
        Map<Character, String> charToString = new HashMap<>();
        Map<String, Character> stringToChar = new HashMap<>();
        for(int i = 0; i < arr.length; i++) {
            //  a bijection between a letter in pattern and a non-empty word in s.
            if(charToString.containsKey(pattern.charAt(i)) && !charToString.get(pattern.charAt(i)).equals(arr[i])) {
                return false;
            }
            if(stringToChar.containsKey(arr[i]) && stringToChar.get(arr[i]) != pattern.charAt(i)) {
                return false;
            }
            charToString.put(pattern.charAt(i), arr[i]);
            stringToChar.put(arr[i], pattern.charAt(i));
        }
        return true;
    }
}
```

- Return early on first HashMap hit rather than scanning full input.

---

#### #299. Bulls and Cows

[leetcode.com/problems/bulls-and-cows/](https://leetcode.com/problems/bulls-and-cows/) · `O(n) / O(n)`

**Q:** Count bulls (right digit, right place) and cows (right digit, wrong place) in a number guessing game.

**Example:** `secret = "1807", guess = "7810"` → `"1A3B"`

**Algorithm:**
1. Use sliding window with HashMap to track character frequencies.
2. Expand right; when window invalid shrink from left.
3. Track result (max window, min window, count of valid windows).
`O(n) / O(n)`

```java
class Solution {
    public String getHint(String secret, String guess) {
        int bulls = 0;
        // to store the occurrence of a char that is not in the bulls
        Map<Character, Integer> secretMap = new HashMap<>();
        Map<Character, Integer> guessMap = new HashMap<>();
        for(int i = 0; i < secret.length(); i++) {
            char secretChar = secret.charAt(i);
            char guessChar = guess.charAt(i);
            if(secretChar == guessChar) {
                bulls++;
            } else {
                secretMap.put(secretChar, secretMap.getOrDefault(secretChar, 0) + 1);
                guessMap.put(guessChar, guessMap.getOrDefault(guessChar, 0) + 1);
            }
        }
        int cows = 0;
        for(char ch : secretMap.keySet()) {
            if(guessMap.keySet().contains(ch)) {
                cows += Math.min(secretMap.get(ch), guessMap.get(ch));
            }
        }
        return bulls + "A" + cows + "B";
    }
}
```

- Return early on first HashMap hit rather than scanning full input.

#### #344. Reverse String

[leetcode.com/problems/reverse-string/](https://leetcode.com/problems/reverse-string/) · `O(n) / O(1)`

**Q:** Reverse a character array in-place.

**Example:** `s = ["h","e","l","l","o"]` → `["o","l","l","e","h"]`

**Algorithm:**
1. Two pointers l=0, r=n-1.
2. While l<r: swap s[l] and s[r]; l++; r--.
`O(n) / O(1)`

```java
class Solution {
    public void reverseString(char[] arr) {
        for(int i = 0, j = arr.length - 1; i < j; i++, j--) {
            swap(arr, i, j);
        }
    }
    private void swap(char[] arr, int i, int j) {
        char temp = arr[i];
        arr[i] = arr[j];
        arr[j] = temp;
    }
}
```

- Single-pass two-pointer swapping is O(n) O(1) — optimal.

---

#### #541. Reverse String II

[leetcode.com/problems/reverse-string-ii/](https://leetcode.com/problems/reverse-string-ii/) · `O(n) / O(n)`

**Q:** Reverse the first k characters of every 2k-character block in a string.

**Example:** `s = "abcdefg", k = 2` → `"bacdfeg"`

**Algorithm:**
1. Identify string operation: search, transform, validate, or parse.
2. Use appropriate data structure: char array, HashMap, stack, or two pointers.
3. Process characters with relevant conditions.
4. Build or return result.
`O(n) / O(n)`

```java
class Solution {
    public String reverseStr(String s, int k) {
        if(s == null || s.length() == 0) {
            return s;
        }
        char[] arr = s.toCharArray();
        int i = 0;
        while(i < arr.length) {
            // j - i + 1 = k
            // j = i + k - 1
            // j = i + 2k - 1
            int j = Math.min(i + k - 1, arr.length - 1);
            reverse(arr, i, j);
            i = i + 2 * k;
        }
        return new String(arr);
    }
    private void swap(char[] arr, int i, int j) {
        char temp = arr[i];
        arr[i] = arr[j];
        arr[j] = temp;
    }
    private void reverse(char[] arr, int i, int j) {
        for(; i < j; i++, j--) {
            swap(arr, i, j);
        }
    }
}
```

- Use char[] instead of String concatenation in loops — avoids O(n²) copies.
- int[26] char frequency array is faster than HashMap for lowercase-only strings.

---

#### #345. Reverse Vowels of a String

[leetcode.com/problems/reverse-vowels-of-a-string/](https://leetcode.com/problems/reverse-vowels-of-a-string/) · `O(n) / O(n)`

**Q:** Reverse only the vowels of a string.

**Example:** `s = "hello"` → `"holle"`

**Algorithm:**
1. Identify pointer strategy: opposite ends or same direction.
2. Move pointers based on comparison with target condition.
3. Track result/count when condition satisfied.
4. Handle duplicates by skipping equal adjacent elements.
`O(n) / O(n)`

```java
class Solution {
    public String reverseVowels(String s) {
        if(s == null || s.length() == 0) {
            return s;
        }
        char[] arr = s.toCharArray();
        int left = 0, right = arr.length - 1;
        while(left < right) {
            while(left < right && !isVowels(arr[left])) {
                left++;
            }
            while(left < right && !isVowels(arr[right])) {
                right--;
            }
            swap(arr, left, right);
            left++;
            right--;
        }
        return new String(arr);
    }
    public boolean isVowels(char ch) {
        return ch == 'a' || ch == 'e' || ch == 'i' || ch == 'o' || ch == 'u' || ch == 'A' || ch == 'E' || ch == 'I' || ch == 'O' || ch == 'U';
    }
    private void swap(char[] arr, int i, int j) {
        char temp = arr[i];
        arr[i] = arr[j];
        arr[j] = temp;
    }
}
```

- Use char[] instead of String concatenation in loops — avoids O(n²) copies.
- int[26] char frequency array is faster than HashMap for lowercase-only strings.

#### #383. Ransom Note

[leetcode.com/problems/ransom-note/](https://leetcode.com/problems/ransom-note/) · `O(n) / O(n)`

**Q:** Determine if a ransom note can be constructed from a magazine's letters.

**Example:** `ransomNote = "a", magazine = "b"` → `false`

**Algorithm:**
1. Use int[26] frequency array for O(1) char counting (lowercase letters).
2. Scan string, increment/decrement counts.
3. Check condition on frequency array (all zeros, single non-zero, etc.).
`O(n) / O(n)`

```java
class Solution {
    public boolean canConstruct(String ransomNote, String magazine) {
        int[] count1 = generateCount(ransomNote);
        int[] count2 = generateCount(magazine);
        for(int i = 0; i < 26; i++) {
            if(count1[i] > count2[i]) {
                return false;
            }
        }
        return true;
    }
    //
    public int[] generateCount(String word) {
        int[] count = new int[26];
        for(char ch : word.toCharArray()) {
            count[ch - 'a']++;
        }
        return count;
    }
}
```

- Use char[] instead of String concatenation in loops — avoids O(n²) copies.
- int[26] char frequency array is faster than HashMap for lowercase-only strings.

#### #387. First Unique Character in a String

[leetcode.com/problems/first-unique-character-in-a-string/](https://leetcode.com/problems/first-unique-character-in-a-string/) · `O(n) / O(n)`

**Q:** Find the first non-repeating character in a string. Return its index or -1.

**Example:** `s = "leetcode"` → `0`

**Algorithm:**
1. Use int[26] frequency array for O(1) char counting (lowercase letters).
2. Scan string, increment/decrement counts.
3. Check condition on frequency array (all zeros, single non-zero, etc.).
`O(n) / O(n)`

```java
class Solution {
    public int firstUniqChar(String s) {
        int[] count = new int[26];
        for(char ch : s.toCharArray()) {
            count[ch - 'a']++;
        }
        for(int i = 0; i < s.length(); i++) {
            if(count[s.charAt(i) - 'a'] == 1) {
                return i;
            }
        }
        return -1;
    }
}
```

- Use char[] instead of String concatenation in loops — avoids O(n²) copies.
- int[26] char frequency array is faster than HashMap for lowercase-only strings.

#### #388. Longest Absolute File Path

[leetcode.com/problems/longest-absolute-file-path/](https://leetcode.com/problems/longest-absolute-file-path/) · `O(n) / O(n)`

**Q:** Find the length of the longest absolute file path in a file system string.

**Algorithm:**
1. Identify string operation: search, transform, validate, or parse.
2. Use appropriate data structure: char array, HashMap, stack, or two pointers.
3. Process characters with relevant conditions.
4. Build or return result.
`O(n) / O(n)`

```java
class Solution {
    // split(‘\n’)
    // ’\t’/0split(‘\n’)\t...
    // "."
    public int lengthLongestPath(String input) {
        if (input.length() == 0) {
            return 0;
        }
        int result = 0;
        int[] sum = new int[input.length() + 1];    //100
        for (String s : input.split("\n")) {
            int level = s.lastIndexOf('\t') + 2;    // \t
            int len = s.length() - (level - 1);     //
            if (s.contains(".")) {
                result = Math.max(result, sum[level - 1] + len);
            } else {
                sum[level] = sum[level - 1] + len + 1;  //+1/
            }
        }
        return result;
    }
}
```

- Use char[] instead of String concatenation in loops — avoids O(n²) copies.
- int[26] char frequency array is faster than HashMap for lowercase-only strings.

#### #392. Is Subsequence

[leetcode.com/problems/is-subsequence/](https://leetcode.com/problems/is-subsequence/) · `O(mn) / O(mn)`

**Q:** Check if string s is a subsequence of string t.

**Example:** `s = "abc", t = "ahbgdc"` → `true`

**Algorithm:**
1. Define dp on string indices (e.g. dp[i][j] = property of s[i..j]).
2. Base case: single chars or empty strings.
3. Fill diagonally or by length; use char equality for transitions.
4. Return dp answer.
`O(mn) / O(mn)`

```java
class Solution {
    public boolean isSubsequence(String s, String t) {
        int n = s.length(), m = t.length();
        int i = 0, j = 0;
        while (i < n && j < m) {
            if (s.charAt(i) == t.charAt(j)) {
                i++;
            }
            j++;
        }
        return i == n;
    }
}
class Solution {
    public boolean isSubsequence(String s, String t) {
        int[][] dp = new int[s.length() + 1][t.length() + 1];
        // dp[i][j]: s[0, i-1] t[0, j-1]
        for (int i = 1; i < s.length() + 1; i++) {
            for (int j = 1; j < t.length() + 1; j++) {
                if (s.charAt(i-1) == t.charAt(j-1)){
                    dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j-1] + 1;
                }else {
                    dp[i][j] = dp[i][j-1];
                }
            }
        }
        return dp[s.length()][t.length()] == s.length();
    }
}
```

- Use 1D rolling array if dp[i] only depends on previous row — reduces space from O(mn) to O(n).

#### #408. Valid Word Abbreviation

[leetcode.com/problems/valid-word-abbreviation/](https://leetcode.com/problems/valid-word-abbreviation/) · `O(n) / O(n)`

**Q:** Check if an abbreviation matches a word (digits represent skipped characters).

**Example:** `s = "internationalization", abbr = "i12iz4n"` → `true`

**Algorithm:**
1. Identify string operation: search, transform, validate, or parse.
2. Use appropriate data structure: char array, HashMap, stack, or two pointers.
3. Process characters with relevant conditions.
4. Build or return result.
`O(n) / O(n)`

```java
class Solution {
    public boolean validWordAbbreviation(String word, String abbr) {
        int num = 0;
        int next = 0;
        for(char ch : abbr.toCharArray()) {
            if(ch >= '0' && ch <= '9') {
                if(num == 0 && ch == '0') {
                    return false;
                }
                num = num * 10 + (ch - '0');
                continue;
            }
            next = next + num;
            if(next >= word.length() || word.charAt(next) != ch) {
                return false;
            }
            next++;
            num = 0;
        }
        return next + num == word.length();
    }
}
class Solution {
    public boolean validWordAbbreviation(String word, String abbr) {
        int i = 0, j = 0;
        while(i < abbr.length() && j < word.length()) {
            if(Character.isDigit(abbr.charAt(i))) {
                if(abbr.charAt(i) == '0') {
                    return false;
                }
                int k = i;
                while(k + 1 < abbr.length() && Character.isDigit(abbr.charAt(k+1))) {
                    k++;
                }
                int num = Integer.valueOf(abbr.substring(i, k + 1));
                System.out.println(num);
                i = k + 1;
                j = j + num;
            } else {
                if(abbr.charAt(i) != word.charAt(j)) {
                    System.out.println(i + " " + j);
                    System.out.println(abbr.charAt(i) + " " + word.charAt(j));
                    return false;
                }
                i++;
                j++;
            }
        }
        return i == abbr.length() && j == word.length();
    }
}
```

- Use char[] instead of String concatenation in loops — avoids O(n²) copies.
- int[26] char frequency array is faster than HashMap for lowercase-only strings.

#### #409. Longest Palindrome

[leetcode.com/problems/longest-palindrome/](https://leetcode.com/problems/longest-palindrome/) · `O(n) / O(n)`

**Example:** `s = "abccccdd"` → `7`

**Algorithm:**
1. Identify string operation: search, transform, validate, or parse.
2. Use appropriate data structure: char array, HashMap, stack, or two pointers.
3. Process characters with relevant conditions.
4. Build or return result.
`O(n) / O(n)`

```java
class Solution {
    //
    public int longestPalindrome(String s) {
        int[] count = new int[128];
        for (int i = 0; i < s.length(); ++i) {
            char c = s.charAt(i);
            count[c]++;
        }
        int max = 0;
        for (int v: count) {
            max += v / 2 * 2;
            if (v % 2 == 1 && max % 2 == 0) {
                max++;
            }
        }
        return max;
    }
}
```

- Use char[] instead of String concatenation in loops — avoids O(n²) copies.
- int[26] char frequency array is faster than HashMap for lowercase-only strings.

#### #415. Add Strings

[leetcode.com/problems/add-strings/](https://leetcode.com/problems/add-strings/) · `O(n) / O(n)`

**Q:** Add two non-negative integers given as strings. Return the sum as a string.

**Example:** `num1 = "11", num2 = "123"` → `"134"`

**Algorithm:**
1. Use StringBuilder for efficient string building.
2. Process characters left to right; apply transformation rules.
3. Append to builder; handle edge cases (spaces, special chars).
4. Return builder.toString().
`O(n) / O(n)`

```java
class Solution {
    public String addStrings(String num1, String num2) {
        StringBuilder sb = new StringBuilder();
        int i = num1.length() - 1, j = num2.length() - 1, carry = 0;
        while(i >= 0 || j >= 0 || carry != 0) {
            int digit = 0;
            if(i >= 0) {
                digit += num1.charAt(i) - '0';
                i--;
            }
            if(j >= 0) {
                digit += num2.charAt(j) - '0';
                j--;
            }
            digit += carry;
            carry = digit / 10;
            digit = digit % 10;
            sb.append(digit);
        }
        return sb.reverse().toString();
    }
}
```

- Use char[] instead of String concatenation in loops — avoids O(n²) copies.
- int[26] char frequency array is faster than HashMap for lowercase-only strings.

#### #422. Valid Word Square

[leetcode.com/problems/valid-word-square/](https://leetcode.com/problems/valid-word-square/) · `O(n) / O(n)`

**Example:** `words = ["abcd","bnrt","crmy","dtye"]` → `true`

**Algorithm:**
1. Use sliding window with HashMap to track character frequencies.
2. Expand right; when window invalid shrink from left.
3. Track result (max window, min window, count of valid windows).
`O(n) / O(n)`

```java
class Solution {
    public boolean validWordSquare(List<String> words) {
        int n = words.size();
        for(int i = 0; i< n; i++) {
            for(int j = 0; j < words.get(i).length(); j++) {
                if(words.get(i).length() > n) {
                    return false;
                }
                if(words.get(j).length() <= i) {
                    return false;
                }
                if(words.get(i).charAt(j) != words.get(j).charAt(i)) {
                    return false;
                }
            }
        }
        return true;
    }
}
```

- Use char[] instead of String concatenation in loops — avoids O(n²) copies.
- int[26] char frequency array is faster than HashMap for lowercase-only strings.

#### #423. Reconstruct Original Digits from English

[leetcode.com/problems/reconstruct-original-digits-from-english/](https://leetcode.com/problems/reconstruct-original-digits-from-english/) · `O(n) / O(n)`

**Q:** Given a scrambled string of digit-word letters, decode original digits in order.

**Example:** `s = "owoztneoer"` → `"two"`

**Algorithm:**
1. Use sliding window with HashMap to track character frequencies.
2. Expand right; when window invalid shrink from left.
3. Track result (max window, min window, count of valid windows).
`O(n) / O(n)`

```java
class Solution {
    public String originalDigits(String s) {
        Map<Character, Integer> c = new HashMap<>();
        for (int i = 0; i < s.length(); ++i) {
            char ch = s.charAt(i);
            c.put(ch, c.getOrDefault(ch, 0) + 1);
        }
        int[] count = new int[10];
        count[0] = c.getOrDefault('z', 0);
        count[2] = c.getOrDefault('w', 0);
        count[4] = c.getOrDefault('u', 0);
        count[6] = c.getOrDefault('x', 0);
        count[8] = c.getOrDefault('g', 0);
        count[3] = c.getOrDefault('h', 0) - count[8];
        count[5] = c.getOrDefault('f', 0) - count[4];
        count[7] = c.getOrDefault('s', 0) - count[6];
        count[1] = c.getOrDefault('o', 0) - count[0] - count[2] - count[4];
        count[9] = c.getOrDefault('i', 0) - count[5] - count[6] - count[8];
        StringBuffer result = new StringBuffer();
        for (int i = 0; i < 10; ++i) {
            for (int j = 0; j < count[i]; ++j) {
                result.append((char) (i + '0'));
            }
        }
        return result.toString();
    }
}
```

- Return early on first HashMap hit rather than scanning full input.

class Solution {

            if ((i == 0 || s.charAt(i - 1) == ' ') && s.charAt(i) != ' ') {
                segmentCount++;

        return segmentCount;

#### #443. String Compression

[leetcode.com/problems/string-compression/](https://leetcode.com/problems/string-compression/) · `O(n) / O(n)`

**Q:** Compress an array of chars in-place: 'aabcc' → 'a2bc2'. Return new length.

**Example:** `chars = ["a","a","b","b","c","c","c"]` → `6`

**Algorithm:**
1. Identify string operation: search, transform, validate, or parse.
2. Use appropriate data structure: char array, HashMap, stack, or two pointers.
3. Process characters with relevant conditions.
4. Build or return result.
`O(n) / O(n)`

```java
class Solution {
    public int compress(char[] chars) {
        if(chars == null || chars.length == 0) {
            return 0;
        }
        int i = 0, j = 0;
        // [0, i)
        while(j < chars.length) {
            int start = j;
            while(j + 1 < chars.length && chars[j+1] == chars[j]) {
                j++;
            }
            int len = j - start + 1;
            if(len == 1) {
                chars[i] = chars[j];
                i = i + 1;
            } else {
                chars[i] = chars[j];
                i++;
                while (len != 0) {
                    chars[i] = (char)(len % 10 + '0');
                    len /= 10;
                    i++;
                }
            }
            j = j + 1;
        }
        return i;
    }
}
```

- Use char[] instead of String concatenation in loops — avoids O(n²) copies.
- int[26] char frequency array is faster than HashMap for lowercase-only strings.

#### #459. Repeated Substring Pattern

[leetcode.com/problems/repeated-substring-pattern/](https://leetcode.com/problems/repeated-substring-pattern/) · `O(n) / O(n)`

**Example:** `s = "abcabcabcabc"` → `true`

**Algorithm:**
1. Identify string operation: search, transform, validate, or parse.
2. Use appropriate data structure: char array, HashMap, stack, or two pointers.
3. Process characters with relevant conditions.
4. Build or return result.
`O(n) / O(n)`

```java
class Solution {
    //  s  s  s
    public boolean repeatedSubstringPattern(String s) {
        return (s + s).indexOf(s, 1) != s.length();
    }
}
class Solution {
    public boolean repeatedSubstringPattern(String s) {
        int n = s.length();
        for (int i = 1; i * 2 <= n; ++i) {
            if (n % i == 0) {
                boolean match = true;
                for (int j = i; j < n; ++j) {
                    if (s.charAt(j) != s.charAt(j - i)) {
                        match = false;
                        break;
                    }
                }
                if (match) {
                    return true;
                }
            }
        }
        return false;
    }
}
```

- Use char[] instead of String concatenation in loops — avoids O(n²) copies.
- int[26] char frequency array is faster than HashMap for lowercase-only strings.

#### #468. Validate IP Address

[leetcode.com/problems/validate-ip-address/](https://leetcode.com/problems/validate-ip-address/) · `O(n) / O(n)`

**Q:** Validate whether a string is a valid IPv4 or IPv6 address.

**Example:** `queryIP = "172.16.254.1"` → `"IPv4"`

**Algorithm:**
1. Identify string operation: search, transform, validate, or parse.
2. Use appropriate data structure: char array, HashMap, stack, or two pointers.
3. Process characters with relevant conditions.
4. Build or return result.
`O(n) / O(n)`

```java
class Solution {
    public String validateIPv4(String IP) {
        String[] nums = IP.split("\\.", -1);
        for (String x : nums) {
            if (x.length() == 0 || x.length() > 3) return "Neither";
            if (x.charAt(0) == '0' && x.length() != 1) return "Neither";
            for (char ch : x.toCharArray()) {
                if (! Character.isDigit(ch)) return "Neither";
            }
            if (Integer.parseInt(x) > 255) return "Neither";
        }
        return "IPv4";
    }
    public String validateIPv6(String IP) {
        String[] nums = IP.split(":", -1);
        String hexdigits = "0123456789abcdefABCDEF";
        for (String x : nums) {
            if (x.length() == 0 || x.length() > 4) return "Neither";
            for (Character ch : x.toCharArray()) {
                if (hexdigits.indexOf(ch) == -1) return "Neither";
            }
        }
        return "IPv6";
    }
    public String validIPAddress(String IP) {
        if (IP.chars().filter(ch -> ch == '.').count() == 3) {
            return validateIPv4(IP);
        }
        else if (IP.chars().filter(ch -> ch == ':').count() == 7) {
            return validateIPv6(IP);
        }
        else return "Neither";
    }
}
```

- Use char[] instead of String concatenation in loops — avoids O(n²) copies.
- int[26] char frequency array is faster than HashMap for lowercase-only strings.

---

#### #556. Next Greater Element III

[leetcode.com/problems/next-greater-element-iii/](https://leetcode.com/problems/next-greater-element-iii/) · `O(n) / O(n)`

**Q:** Find the next greater element by rearranging the digits of n. Return -1 if not possible or overflow.

**Example:** `n = 12` → `21`

**Algorithm:**
1. Identify string operation: search, transform, validate, or parse.
2. Use appropriate data structure: char array, HashMap, stack, or two pointers.
3. Process characters with relevant conditions.
4. Build or return result.
`O(n) / O(n)`

```java
class Solution {
    public int nextGreaterElement(int n) {
        char[] arr = String.valueOf(n).toCharArray();
        n = arr.length;
        int i = n - 2;
        while(i >= 0 && arr[i] >= arr[i+1]) {
            i--;
        }
        if(i < 0) {
            return -1;
        }
        int j = n - 1;
        while(j >= 0 && arr[i] >= arr[j]) {
            j--;
        }
        swap(arr, i, j);
        reverse(arr, i + 1, n - 1);
        try {
            return Integer.parseInt(String.valueOf(arr));
        } catch (Exception e) {
            return -1;
        }
    }
    public void swap(char[] arr, int i, int j) {
        char temp = arr[i];
        arr[i] = arr[j];
        arr[j] = temp;
    }
    public void reverse(char[] arr, int i, int j) {
        for(; i < j; i++, j--) {
            swap(arr, i, j);
        }
    }
}
```

- Use char[] instead of String concatenation in loops — avoids O(n²) copies.
- int[26] char frequency array is faster than HashMap for lowercase-only strings.

#### #500. Keyboard Row

[leetcode.com/problems/keyboard-row/](https://leetcode.com/problems/keyboard-row/) · `O(n) / O(n)`

**Q:** Find all words that can be typed on a single row of a QWERTY keyboard.

**Example:** `kb = ["qwertyuiop","asdfghjkl","zxcvbnm"], words = ["Hello","Alaska","Dad","Peace"]` → `["Alaska","Dad"]`

**Algorithm:**
1. Use sliding window with HashMap to track character frequencies.
2. Expand right; when window invalid shrink from left.
3. Track result (max window, min window, count of valid windows).
`O(n) / O(n)`

```java
class Solution {
    public String[] findWords(String[] words) {
        List<String> list = new ArrayList<>();
        //
        String rowIdx = "12210111011122000010020202";
        for (String word : words) {
            boolean isValid = true;
            char index = rowIdx.charAt(Character.toLowerCase(word.charAt(0)) - 'a');
            for (int i = 1; i < word.length(); ++i) {
                if (rowIdx.charAt(Character.toLowerCase(word.charAt(i)) - 'a') != index) {
                    isValid = false;
                    break;
                }
            }
            if (isValid) {
                list.add(word);
            }
        }
        String[] result = new String[list.size()];
        for (int i = 0; i < list.size(); ++i) {
            result[i] = list.get(i);
        }
        return result;
    }
}
```

- Use char[] instead of String concatenation in loops — avoids O(n²) copies.
- int[26] char frequency array is faster than HashMap for lowercase-only strings.

#### #520. Detect Capital

[leetcode.com/problems/detect-capital/](https://leetcode.com/problems/detect-capital/) · `O(n) / O(n)`

**Q:** Check if a word is capitalized correctly (all caps, all lower, or first letter only).

**Example:** `word = "Hello"` → `false`

**Algorithm:**
1. Identify string operation: search, transform, validate, or parse.
2. Use appropriate data structure: char array, HashMap, stack, or two pointers.
3. Process characters with relevant conditions.
4. Build or return result.
`O(n) / O(n)`

```java
class Solution {
    public boolean detectCapitalUse(String word) {
        //  1  2
        if (word.length() >= 2 && Character.isLowerCase(word.charAt(0)) && Character.isUpperCase(word.charAt(1))) {
            return false;
        }
        //  1  2
        for (int i = 2; i < word.length(); i++) {
            if (Character.isLowerCase(word.charAt(i)) ^ Character.isLowerCase(word.charAt(1))) {
                return false;
            }
        }
        return true;
    }
}
```

- Use char[] instead of String concatenation in loops — avoids O(n²) copies.
- int[26] char frequency array is faster than HashMap for lowercase-only strings.

#### #524. Longest Word in Dictionary through Deleting

[leetcode.com/problems/longest-word-in-dictionary-through-deleting/](https://leetcode.com/problems/longest-word-in-dictionary-through-deleting/) · `O(n) / O(n)`

**Q:** Find the longest word in a dictionary that is a subsequence of string s.

**Algorithm:**
1. Identify string operation: search, transform, validate, or parse.
2. Use appropriate data structure: char array, HashMap, stack, or two pointers.
3. Process characters with relevant conditions.
4. Build or return result.
`O(n) / O(n)`

```java
class Solution {
    public String findLongestWord(String s, List<String> dictionary) {
        String result = "";
        int maxLen = 0;
        for(String word : dictionary) {
            if(match(s, word)) {
                if(word.length() > result.length()) {
                    result = word;
                    maxLen = word.length();
                }
                if(word.length() == result.length() && word.compareTo(result) < 0) {
                    result = word;
                }
            }
        }
        return result;
    }
    //  i == word.length()
    public boolean match(String s, String word) {
        int i = 0;
        for(char ch : s.toCharArray()) {
            if(i < word.length() && ch == word.charAt(i)) {
                i++;
            }
        }
        return i == word.length();
    }
}
```

- Use char[] instead of String concatenation in loops — avoids O(n²) copies.
- int[26] char frequency array is faster than HashMap for lowercase-only strings.

#### #551. Student Attendance Record I

[leetcode.com/problems/student-attendance-record-i/](https://leetcode.com/problems/student-attendance-record-i/) · `O(n) / O(n)`

**Q:** Check if a student's attendance record is eligible for an award (no late > 1, no absence ≥ 3 consecutive).

**Example:** `s = "0110101"` → `true`

**Algorithm:**
1. Identify string operation: search, transform, validate, or parse.
2. Use appropriate data structure: char array, HashMap, stack, or two pointers.
3. Process characters with relevant conditions.
4. Build or return result.
`O(n) / O(n)`

```java
class Solution {
    public boolean checkRecord(String s) {
        if(s == null || s.length() == 0) {
            return false;
        }
        int countAbsent = 0;
        for(int i = 0; i < s.length(); i++) {
            char ch = s.charAt(i);
            if(ch == 'A') {
                countAbsent++;
            }
            if(ch == 'L' && i + 2 < s.length() && s.charAt(i+1) == 'L' && s.charAt(i+2) == 'L') {
                return false;
            }
        }
        return countAbsent < 2;
    }
}
```

- Use char[] instead of String concatenation in loops — avoids O(n²) copies.
- int[26] char frequency array is faster than HashMap for lowercase-only strings.

#### #592. Fraction Addition and Subtraction

[leetcode.com/problems/fraction-addition-and-subtraction/](https://leetcode.com/problems/fraction-addition-and-subtraction/) · `O(n) / O(n)`

**Q:** Add or subtract fractions, returning the result in lowest terms.

**Algorithm:**
1. Use sliding window with HashMap to track character frequencies.
2. Expand right; when window invalid shrink from left.
3. Track result (max window, min window, count of valid windows).
`O(n) / O(n)`

```java
public class Solution {
    //  LCM GCD
    public String fractionAddition(String expression) {
        List < Character > sign = new ArrayList < > ();
        for (int i = 1; i < expression.length(); i++) {
            if (expression.charAt(i) == '+' || expression.charAt(i) == '-')
            sign.add(expression.charAt(i));
        }
        List < Integer > num = new ArrayList < > ();
        List < Integer > den = new ArrayList < > ();
        for (String sub: expression.split("\\+")) {
            for (String subsub: sub.split("-")) {
                if (subsub.length() > 0) {
                    String[] fraction = subsub.split("/");
                    num.add(Integer.parseInt(fraction[0]));
                    den.add(Integer.parseInt(fraction[1]));
                }
            }
        }
        if (expression.charAt(0) == '-')
        num.set(0, -num.get(0));
        int lcm = 1;
        for (int x: den) {
            lcm = lcm_(lcm, x);
        }
        int result = lcm / den.get(0) * num.get(0);
        for (int i = 1; i < num.size(); i++) {
            if (sign.get(i - 1) == '+')
            result += lcm / den.get(i) * num.get(i);
            else
            result -= lcm / den.get(i) * num.get(i);
        }
        int g = gcd(Math.abs(result), Math.abs(lcm));
        return (result / g) + "/" + (lcm / g);
    }
    public int lcm_(int a, int b) {
        return a * b / gcd(a, b);
    }
    public int gcd(int a, int b) {
        while (b != 0) {
            int t = b;
            b = a % b;
            a = t;
        }
        return a;
    }
}
public class Solution {
    public String fractionAddition(String expression) {
        List < Character > sign = new ArrayList < > ();
        if (expression.charAt(0) != '-')
        sign.add('+');
        for (int i = 0; i < expression.length(); i++) {
            if (expression.charAt(i) == '+' || expression.charAt(i) == '-')
            sign.add(expression.charAt(i));
        }
        int prev_num = 0, prev_den = 1, i = 0;
        for (String sub: expression.split("(\\+)|(-)")) {
            if (sub.length() > 0) {
                String[] fraction = sub.split("/");
                int num = (Integer.parseInt(fraction[0]));
                int den = (Integer.parseInt(fraction[1]));
                int g = Math.abs(gcd(den, prev_den));
                if (sign.get(i++) == '+')
                prev_num = prev_num * den / g + num * prev_den / g;
                else
                prev_num = prev_num * den / g - num * prev_den / g;
                prev_den = den * prev_den / g;
                g = Math.abs(gcd(prev_den, prev_num));
                prev_num /= g;
                prev_den /= g;
            }
        }
        return prev_num + "/" + prev_den;
    }
    public int gcd(int a, int b) {
        while (b != 0) {
            int t = b;
            b = a % b;
            a = t;
        }
        return a;
    }
}
```

- Use char[] instead of String concatenation in loops — avoids O(n²) copies.
- int[26] char frequency array is faster than HashMap for lowercase-only strings.

#### #616. Add Bold Tag in String

[leetcode.com/problems/add-bold-tag-in-string/](https://leetcode.com/problems/add-bold-tag-in-string/) · `O(n log n) / O(1)`

**Algorithm:**
1. Use sliding window with HashMap to track character frequencies.
2. Expand right; when window invalid shrink from left.
3. Track result (max window, min window, count of valid windows).
`O(n log n) / O(1)`

```java
class Solution {
    public String addBoldTag(String s, String[] dict) {
        List<int[]> ranges = new ArrayList<>();
        getRanges(s, dict, ranges);
        //
        if (ranges.isEmpty()) return s;
        //
        LinkedList<int[]> merge = getMerge(ranges);
        //
        StringBuilder sb = new StringBuilder();
        int start = 0;
        for (int i = 0; i < merge.size(); i++) {
            int[] r = merge.get(i);
            sb.append(s, start, r[0]).append("<b>").append(s, r[0], r[1] + 1).append("</b>");
            start = r[1] + 1;
        }
        if (start < s.length()) {
            sb.append(s.substring(start));
        }
        return sb.toString();
    }
    private LinkedList<int[]> getMerge(List<int[]> ranges) {
        LinkedList<int[]> merge = new LinkedList<>();
        merge.add(ranges.get(0));
        for (int i = 1; i < ranges.size(); i++) {
            int[] last = merge.getLast();
            int[] range = ranges.get(i);
            if (range[0] <= last[1] + 1) {
                merge.removeLast();
                merge.add(new int[]{Math.min(last[0], range[0]), Math.max(last[1], range[1])});
            } else merge.add(range);
        }
        return merge;
    }
    private void getRanges(String s, String[] dict, List<int[]> ranges) {
        for (String dic : dict) {
            if (s.contains(dic)) {
                helper(s, dic, 0, ranges);
            }
        }
        Collections.sort(ranges, (o1, o2) -> {
            if (o1[0] == o2[0]) return o1[1] - o2[1];
            else return o1[0] - o2[0];
        });
    }
    private void helper(String s, String dic, int i, List<int[]> ranges) {
        if (i < s.length() && s.substring(i).contains(dic)) {
            int start = s.indexOf(dic, i);
            int end = start + dic.length() - 1;
            ranges.add(new int[]{start, end});
            helper(s, dic, start + 1, ranges);
        }
    }
}
```

- Use char[] instead of String concatenation in loops — avoids O(n²) copies.
- int[26] char frequency array is faster than HashMap for lowercase-only strings.

#### #657. Robot Return to Origin

[leetcode.com/problems/robot-return-to-origin/](https://leetcode.com/problems/robot-return-to-origin/) · `O(n) / O(n)`

**Q:** Check if a robot following instruction string (UDLR) returns to origin.

**Example:** `directions = "UD"` → `true`

**Algorithm:**
1. Identify string operation: search, transform, validate, or parse.
2. Use appropriate data structure: char array, HashMap, stack, or two pointers.
3. Process characters with relevant conditions.
4. Build or return result.
`O(n) / O(n)`

```java
class Solution {
    public boolean judgeCircle(String moves) {
        int x = 0, y = 0;
        int length = moves.length();
        for (int i = 0; i < length; i++) {
            char move = moves.charAt(i);
            if (move == 'U') {
                y--;
            } else if (move == 'D') {
                y++;
            } else if (move == 'L') {
                x--;
            } else if (move == 'R') {
                x++;
            }
        }
        return x == 0 && y == 0;
    }
}
```

- Use char[] instead of String concatenation in loops — avoids O(n²) copies.
- int[26] char frequency array is faster than HashMap for lowercase-only strings.

#### #670. Maximum Swap

[leetcode.com/problems/maximum-swap/](https://leetcode.com/problems/maximum-swap/) · `O(n) / O(n)`

**Q:** Swap two digits in a number to get the maximum possible value.

**Example:** `num = 2736` → `7236`

**Algorithm:**
1. Identify string operation: search, transform, validate, or parse.
2. Use appropriate data structure: char array, HashMap, stack, or two pointers.
3. Process characters with relevant conditions.
4. Build or return result.
`O(n) / O(n)`

```java
// array, map
//
Solution {
    public int maximumSwap(int num) {
        char[] arr = String.valueOf(num).toCharArray();
        int[] lastIndex = new int[10];
        for(int i = 0; i < arr.length; i++) {
            lastIndex[arr[i] - '0'] = i;
        }
        for(int i = 0; i < arr.length; i++) {
            for(int j = 9; j > arr[i] - '0'; j--) {
                if(lastIndex[j] > i) {
                    swap(arr, i, lastIndex[j]);
                    return Integer.parseInt(new String(arr));
                }
            }
        }
        return Integer.parseInt(new String(arr));
    }
    public void swap(char[] arr, int i, int j) {
        char temp = arr[i];
        arr[i] = arr[j];
        arr[j] = temp;
    }
}
```

- Use char[] instead of String concatenation in loops — avoids O(n²) copies.
- int[26] char frequency array is faster than HashMap for lowercase-only strings.

#### #681. Next Closest Time

[leetcode.com/problems/next-closest-time/](https://leetcode.com/problems/next-closest-time/) · `O(n) / O(n)`

**Q:** Find the next closest time using only the digits from a given time string.

**Algorithm:**
1. Identify string operation: search, transform, validate, or parse.
2. Use appropriate data structure: char array, HashMap, stack, or two pointers.
3. Process characters with relevant conditions.
4. Build or return result.
`O(n) / O(n)`

```java
class Solution {
    //
    // set
    public String nextClosestTime(String time) {
        String[] arr = time.split(":");
        Set<Character> s = new HashSet();
        s.add(arr[0].charAt(0));
        s.add(arr[0].charAt(1));
        s.add(arr[1].charAt(0));
        s.add(arr[1].charAt(1));
        int hour = Integer.valueOf(arr[0]);
        int minute = Integer.valueOf(arr[1]);
        int minutes = hour*60+minute;
        for(int i=minutes+1;i<=minutes+24*60;i++){
            String h = ""+(i%(24*60))/60;
            if(h.length()==1)h="0"+h;
            String m = ""+(i%(24*60))%60;
            if(m.length()==1)m="0"+m;
            if(s.contains(m.charAt(0))&&
            s.contains(m.charAt(1))&&
            s.contains(h.charAt(0))&&
            s.contains(h.charAt(1))){
                return h+":"+m;
            }
        }
        return "";
    }
}
```

- Use char[] instead of String concatenation in loops — avoids O(n²) copies.
- int[26] char frequency array is faster than HashMap for lowercase-only strings.

#### #686. Repeated String Match

[leetcode.com/problems/repeated-string-match/](https://leetcode.com/problems/repeated-string-match/) · `O(n) / O(n)`

**Q:** Find the minimum number of times string a must repeat so b is a substring.

**Example:** `s = "abcd", goal = "cdabcdab"` → `3`

**Algorithm:**
1. Use StringBuilder for efficient string building.
2. Process characters left to right; apply transformation rules.
3. Append to builder; handle edge cases (spaces, special chars).
4. Return builder.toString().
`O(n) / O(n)`

```java
class Solution {
    public int repeatedStringMatch(String a, String b) {
        StringBuilder sb = new StringBuilder();
        int result = 0;
        while (sb.length() < b.length() && ++result > 0) sb.append(a);
        sb.append(a);
        int index = strHash(sb.toString(), b);
        if (index == -1) return -1;
        return index + b.length() > a.length() * result ? result + 1 : result;
    }
    int strHash(String ss, String b) {
        int P = 131;
        int n = ss.length(), m = b.length();
        String str = ss + b;
        int len = str.length();
        int[] h = new int[len + 10], p = new int[len + 10];
        p[0] = 1;
        for (int i = 0; i < len; i++) {
            p[i + 1] = p[i] * P;
            h[i + 1] = h[i] * P + str.charAt(i);
        }
        int r = len, l = r - m + 1;
        int target = h[r] - h[l - 1] * p[r - l + 1]; // b
        for (int i = 1; i <= n; i++) {
            int j = i + m - 1;
            int current = h[j] - h[i - 1] * p[j - i + 1]; //
            if (current == target) return i - 1;
        }
        return -1;
    }
}
```

- Use char[] instead of String concatenation in loops — avoids O(n²) copies.
- int[26] char frequency array is faster than HashMap for lowercase-only strings.

#### #709. To Lower Case

[leetcode.com/problems/to-lower-case/](https://leetcode.com/problems/to-lower-case/) · `O(n) / O(n)`

**Q:** Convert a string to lowercase.

**Example:** `s = "Hello"` → `"hello"`

**Algorithm:**
1. Identify string operation: search, transform, validate, or parse.
2. Use appropriate data structure: char array, HashMap, stack, or two pointers.
3. Process characters with relevant conditions.
4. Build or return result.
`O(n) / O(n)`

```java
class Solution {
    public String toLowerCase(String s) {
        //  A - Z [65,90]
        //  a - z [97,122]
        char[] arr = s.toCharArray();
        for (int i = 0; i < s.length(); ++i) {
            if(arr[i] >= 65 && arr[i] <= 90) {
                arr[i] |= 32;
            }
        }
        return new String(arr);
    }
}
class Solution {
    public String toLowerCase(String s) {
        //  A - Z [65,90]
        //  a - z [97,122]
        char[] arr = s.toCharArray();
        for (int i = 0; i < s.length(); ++i) {
            if(arr[i] >= 65 && arr[i] <= 90) {
                arr[i] += 32;
            }
        }
        return new String(arr);
    }
}
```

- Use char[] instead of String concatenation in loops — avoids O(n²) copies.
- int[26] char frequency array is faster than HashMap for lowercase-only strings.

#### #726. Number of Atoms

[leetcode.com/problems/number-of-atoms/](https://leetcode.com/problems/number-of-atoms/) · `O(n) / O(n)`

**Q:** Parse a chemical formula string and return atom counts in sorted order.

**Example:** `formula = "H2O"` → `"H2O"`

**Algorithm:**
1. Use sliding window with HashMap to track character frequencies.
2. Expand right; when window invalid shrink from left.
3. Track result (max window, min window, count of valid windows).
`O(n) / O(n)`

```java
class Solution {
    class Node {
        String s; int v;
        Node (String _s, int _v) {
            s = _s; v = _v;
        }
    }
    public String countOfAtoms(String s) {
        int n = s.length();
        char[] cs = s.toCharArray();
        Map<String, Integer> map = new HashMap<>();
        Deque<String> d = new ArrayDeque<>();
        int index = 0;
        for (int i = 0; i < n; ) {
            char c = cs[i];
            if (c == '(' || c == ')') {
                d.addLast(String.valueOf(c));
                i++;
            } else {
                if (Character.isDigit(c)) {
                    //
                    int j = i;
                    while (j < n && Character.isDigit(cs[j])) j++;
                    String numStr = s.substring(i, j);
                    i = j;
                    int count = Integer.parseInt(String.valueOf(numStr));
                    //  )
                    if (!d.isEmpty() && d.peekLast().equals(")")) {
                        List<String> temp = new ArrayList<>();
                        d.pollLast(); // pop )
                        while (!d.isEmpty() && !d.peekLast().equals("(")) {
                            String current = d.pollLast();
                            map.put(current, map.getOrDefault(current, 1) * count);
                            temp.add(current);
                        }
                        d.pollLast(); // pop (
                        for (int k = temp.size() - 1; k >= 0; k--) {
                            d.addLast(temp.get(k));
                        }
                        //  )
                    } else {
                        String current = d.pollLast();
                        map.put(current, map.getOrDefault(current, 1) * count);
                        d.addLast(current);
                    }
                } else {
                    //
                    int j = i + 1;
                    while (j < n && Character.isLowerCase(cs[j])) j++;
                    String current = s.substring(i, j) + "_" + String.valueOf(index++);
                    map.put(current, map.getOrDefault(current, 0) + 1);
                    i = j;
                    d.addLast(current);
                }
            }
        }
        //
        Map<String, Node> mm = new HashMap<>();
        for (String key : map.keySet()) {
            String atom = key.split("_")[0];
            int count = map.get(key);
            Node node = null;
            if (mm.containsKey(atom)) {
                node = mm.get(atom);
            } else {
                node = new Node(atom, 0);
            }
            node.v += count;
            mm.put(atom, node);
        }
        //  Node
        PriorityQueue<Node> q = new PriorityQueue<>((a,b)->a.s.compareTo(b.s));
        for (String atom : mm.keySet()) q.add(mm.get(atom));
        StringBuilder sb = new StringBuilder();
        while (!q.isEmpty()) {
            Node poll = q.poll();
            sb.append(poll.s);
            if (poll.v > 1) sb.append(poll.v);
        }
        return sb.toString();
    }
}
```

- Return early on first HashMap hit rather than scanning full input.

---

#### #748. Shortest Completing Word

[leetcode.com/problems/shortest-completing-word/](https://leetcode.com/problems/shortest-completing-word/) · `O(n) / O(n)`

**Example:** `words = ["cba","daf","ghi"], s = "abcdefghi"` → `"ghi"`

**Algorithm:**
1. Use int[26] frequency array for O(1) char counting (lowercase letters).
2. Scan string, increment/decrement counts.
3. Check condition on frequency array (all zeros, single non-zero, etc.).
`O(n) / O(n)`

```java
class Solution {
    public String shortestCompletingWord(String licensePlate, String[] words) {
        int[] count = getCnt(licensePlate);
        String result = null;
        for (String s : words) {
            int[] current = getCnt(s);
            boolean match = true;
            for (int i = 0; i < 26 && match; i++) {
                if (count[i] > current[i]) match = false;
            }
            if (match && (result == null || result.length() > s.length())) result = s;
        }
        return result;
    }
    private int[] getCnt(String s) {
        int[] count = new int[26];
        for (char c : s.toCharArray()) {
            if (Character.isLetter(c)) count[Character.toLowerCase(c) - 'a']++;
        }
        return count;
    }
}
```

- Use char[] instead of String concatenation in loops — avoids O(n²) copies.
- int[26] char frequency array is faster than HashMap for lowercase-only strings.

---

#### #788. Rotated Digits

[leetcode.com/problems/rotated-digits/](https://leetcode.com/problems/rotated-digits/) · `O(n) / O(n)`

**Example:** `n = 10` → `1`

**Algorithm:**
1. Define dp on string indices (e.g. dp[i][j] = property of s[i..j]).
2. Base case: single chars or empty strings.
3. Fill diagonally or by length; use char equality for transitions.
4. Return dp answer.
`O(n) / O(n)`

```java
class Solution {
    public int rotatedDigits(int n) {
        int[] dp = new int[10000+1];
        int[] init = new int[]{0, 0, 1, -1, -1, 1, 1, -1, 0, 1};
        for(int i = 0; i < 10; i++) {
            dp[i] = init[i];
        }
        int count = 0;
        for(int i = 0; i <= n; i++) {
            if(dp[i / 10] == -1 || dp[i % 10] == -1) {
                dp[i] = -1;
            }
            else if(dp[i / 10] == 1 || dp[i % 10] == 1) {
                dp[i] = 1;
                count++;
            }
        }
        return count;
    }
}
Here 2 - 8
```

- Use two variables instead of array if only last 1-2 states needed — O(1) space.

#### #791. Custom Sort String

[leetcode.com/problems/custom-sort-string/](https://leetcode.com/problems/custom-sort-string/) · `O(n) / O(n)`

**Q:** Sort string s so its characters appear in the order given by string order.

**Example:** `order = "cba", s = "abcd"` → `"cbad"`

**Algorithm:**
1. Use int[26] frequency array for O(1) char counting (lowercase letters).
2. Scan string, increment/decrement counts.
3. Check condition on frequency array (all zeros, single non-zero, etc.).
`O(n) / O(n)`

```java
class Solution {
    public String customSortString(String order, String s) {
        int[] count = new int[26];
        for(char ch : s.toCharArray()) {
            count[ch - 'a']++;
        }
        StringBuilder builder = new StringBuilder();
        for(char ch : order.toCharArray()) {
            for(int i = 0; i < count[ch - 'a']; i++) {
                builder.append(ch);
            }
            count[ch - 'a'] = 0;
        }
        for(char ch = 'a'; ch <= 'z'; ch++) {
            for(int i = 0; i < count[ch - 'a']; i++) {
                builder.append(ch);
            }
        }
        return builder.toString();
    }
}
```

- Use char[] instead of String concatenation in loops — avoids O(n²) copies.
- int[26] char frequency array is faster than HashMap for lowercase-only strings.

---

#### #794. Valid Tic-Tac-Toe State

[leetcode.com/problems/valid-tictactoe-state/](https://leetcode.com/problems/valid-tictactoe-state/) · `O(n) / O(n)`

**Example:** `board = ["X..","...","..."]` → `false`

**Algorithm:**
1. Identify string operation: search, transform, validate, or parse.
2. Use appropriate data structure: char array, HashMap, stack, or two pointers.
3. Process characters with relevant conditions.
4. Build or return result.
`O(n) / O(n)`

```java
class Solution {
    public boolean validTicTacToe(String[] board) {
        int xCount = 0, oCount = 0;
        for (String row : board) {
            for (char c : row.toCharArray()) {
                xCount = (c == 'X') ? (xCount + 1) : xCount;
                oCount = (c == 'O') ? (oCount + 1) : oCount;
            }
        }
        return !((oCount != xCount && oCount != xCount - 1) ||
        (oCount != xCount - 1 && win(board, 'X')) ||
        (oCount != xCount && win(board, 'O')));
    }
    public boolean win(String[] board, char p) {
        for (int i = 0; i < 3; ++i) {
            if ((p == board[0].charAt(i) && p == board[1].charAt(i) && p == board[2].charAt(i)) ||
            (p == board[i].charAt(0) && p == board[i].charAt(1) && p == board[i].charAt(2))) {
                return true;
            }
        }
        return ((p == board[0].charAt(0) && p == board[1].charAt(1) && p == board[2].charAt(2)) ||
        (p == board[0].charAt(2) && p == board[1].charAt(1) && p == board[2].charAt(0)));
    }
}
```

- Use char[] instead of String concatenation in loops — avoids O(n²) copies.
- int[26] char frequency array is faster than HashMap for lowercase-only strings.

---

#### #796. Rotate String

[leetcode.com/problems/rotate-string/](https://leetcode.com/problems/rotate-string/) · `O(n) / O(n)`

**Q:** Check if string t is a rotation of string s.

**Example:** `s = "abcde", goal = "cdeab"` → `true`

**Algorithm:**
1. Identify string operation: search, transform, validate, or parse.
2. Use appropriate data structure: char array, HashMap, stack, or two pointers.
3. Process characters with relevant conditions.
4. Build or return result.
`O(n) / O(n)`

```java
import java.math.BigInteger;
// Rabin-Karp
class Solution {
    public boolean rotateString(String A, String B) {
        if (A.equals(B)) return true;
        int MOD = 1_000_000_007;
        int P = 113;
        int Pinv = BigInteger.valueOf(P).modInverse(BigInteger.valueOf(MOD)).intValue();
        long hb = 0, power = 1;
        for (char x: B.toCharArray()) {
            hb = (hb + power * x) % MOD;
            power = power * P % MOD;
        }
        long ha = 0; power = 1;
        char[] ca = A.toCharArray();
        for (char x: ca) {
            ha = (ha + power * x) % MOD;
            power = power * P % MOD;
        }
        for (int i = 0; i < ca.length; ++i) {
            char x = ca[i];
            ha += power * x - x;
            ha %= MOD;
            ha *= Pinv;
            ha %= MOD;
            if (ha == hb && (A.substring(i+1) + A.substring(0, i+1)).equals(B))
            return true;
        }
        return false;
    }
}
class Solution {
    // sgoals+s
    public boolean rotateString(String s, String goal) {
        return s.length() == goal.length() && (s + s).indexOf(goal) != -1;
    }
}
```

- Use char[] instead of String concatenation in loops — avoids O(n²) copies.
- int[26] char frequency array is faster than HashMap for lowercase-only strings.

#### #809. Expressive Words

[leetcode.com/problems/expressive-words/](https://leetcode.com/problems/expressive-words/) · `O(n) / O(n)`

**Q:** Determine if a query word matches a stretched version of a word in a list.

**Example:** `s = "heeellooo"` → `true`

**Algorithm:**
1. Identify string operation: search, transform, validate, or parse.
2. Use appropriate data structure: char array, HashMap, stack, or two pointers.
3. Process characters with relevant conditions.
4. Build or return result.
`O(n) / O(n)`

```java
class Solution {
    public int expressiveWords(String s, String[] words) {
        int count = 0;
        for(String word : words) {
            if(isExpressive(s, word)) {
                count++;
            }
        }
        return count;
    }
    public boolean isExpressive(String s, String word) {
        int i = 0, j = 0;
        while(i < s.length() && j < word.length()) {
            char ch1 = s.charAt(i);
            int count1 = 1;
            while(i + 1 < s.length() && s.charAt(i + 1) == ch1) {
                i++;
                count1++;
            }
            i++;
            char ch2 = word.charAt(j);
            int count2 = 1;
            while(j + 1 < word.length() && word.charAt(j + 1) == ch2) {
                j++;
                count2++;
            }
            j++;
            if(ch1 != ch2) {
                return false;
            }
            if(count1 < count2) {
                return false;
            }
            if(count1 < 3 && count1 != count2) {
                return false;
            }
        }
        return i == s.length() && j == word.length();
    }
}
```

- Use char[] instead of String concatenation in loops — avoids O(n²) copies.
- int[26] char frequency array is faster than HashMap for lowercase-only strings.

#### #824. Goat Latin

[leetcode.com/problems/goat-latin/](https://leetcode.com/problems/goat-latin/) · `O(n) / O(n)`

**Q:** Convert words to Goat Latin: append 'ma', move leading consonants to end + 'ma'.

**Example:** `sentence = "I am really happy"` → `"I am really yadppa"... no, "I am yllaer yppah"`

**Algorithm:**
1. Use StringBuilder for efficient string building.
2. Process characters left to right; apply transformation rules.
3. Append to builder; handle edge cases (spaces, special chars).
4. Return builder.toString().
`O(n) / O(n)`

```java
class Solution {
    public String toGoatLatin(String S) {
        Set<Character> vowel = new HashSet();
        for (char c: new char[]{'a', 'e', 'i', 'o', 'u', 'A', 'E', 'I', 'O', 'U'})
        vowel.add(c);
        int t = 1;
        StringBuilder result = new StringBuilder();
        for (String word: S.split(" ")) {
            char first = word.charAt(0);
            if (vowel.contains(first)) {
                result.append(word);
            } else {
                result.append(word.substring(1));
                result.append(word.substring(0, 1));
            }
            result.append("ma");
            for (int i = 0; i < t; i++)
            result.append("a");
            t++;
            result.append(" ");
        }
        result.deleteCharAt(result.length() - 1);
        return result.toString();
    }
}
```

- Use char[] instead of String concatenation in loops — avoids O(n²) copies.
- int[26] char frequency array is faster than HashMap for lowercase-only strings.

#### #833. Find And Replace in String

[leetcode.com/problems/find-and-replace-in-string/](https://leetcode.com/problems/find-and-replace-in-string/) · `O(n log n) / O(1)`

**Example:** `s = "abcde", indices = [0,2], sources = ["a","cd"], targets = ["eee","ffff"]` → `"eeebffffe"`

**Algorithm:**
1. Identify string operation: search, transform, validate, or parse.
2. Use appropriate data structure: char array, HashMap, stack, or two pointers.
3. Process characters with relevant conditions.
4. Build or return result.
`O(n log n) / O(1)`

```java
class Solution {
    public String findReplaceString(String S, int[] indexes, String[] sources, String[] targets) {
        List<int[]> list = new ArrayList<>();
        for (int i = 0; i < indexes.length; i ++) {
            list.add(new int[]{indexes[i], i});
        }
        Collections.sort(list, (a, b) -> (b[0] - a[0]));
        for (int[] l : list) {
            int index = l[0];
            int i = l[1];
            String s = sources[i];
            String t = targets[i];
            if (S.substring(index, index + s.length()).equals(s)) {
                S = S.substring(0, index) + t + S.substring(index + s.length());
            }
        }
        return S;
    }
}
```

- Use char[] instead of String concatenation in loops — avoids O(n²) copies.
- int[26] char frequency array is faster than HashMap for lowercase-only strings.

#### #859. Buddy Strings

[leetcode.com/problems/buddy-strings/](https://leetcode.com/problems/buddy-strings/) · `O(n) / O(n)`

**Q:** Check if two strings are 'buddy strings': swapping exactly one pair makes them equal.

**Example:** `s = "ab", goal = "ba"` → `true`

**Algorithm:**
1. Use int[26] frequency array for O(1) char counting (lowercase letters).
2. Scan string, increment/decrement counts.
3. Check condition on frequency array (all zeros, single non-zero, etc.).
`O(n) / O(n)`

```java
class Solution {
    //
    // false
    //
    public boolean buddyStrings(String s, String goal) {
        if (s.length() != goal.length()) {
            return false;
        }
        if (s.equals(goal)) {
            int[] count = new int[26];
            for (int i = 0; i < s.length(); i++) {
                count[s.charAt(i) - 'a']++;
                if (count[s.charAt(i) - 'a'] > 1) {
                    return true;
                }
            }
            return false;
        } else {
            int first = -1, second = -1;
            for (int i = 0; i < goal.length(); i++) {
                if (s.charAt(i) != goal.charAt(i)) {
                    if (first == -1)
                    first = i;
                    else if (second == -1)
                    second = i;
                    else
                    return false;
                }
            }
            return (second != -1 && s.charAt(first) == goal.charAt(second) &&
            s.charAt(second) == goal.charAt(first));
        }
    }
}
```

- Use char[] instead of String concatenation in loops — avoids O(n²) copies.
- int[26] char frequency array is faster than HashMap for lowercase-only strings.

#### #953. Verifying an Alien Dictionary

[leetcode.com/problems/verifying-an-alien-dictionary/](https://leetcode.com/problems/verifying-an-alien-dictionary/) · `O(n) / O(n)`

**Q:** Check if words are sorted in a given alien language's alphabetical order.

**Example:** `words = ["hello","leetcode"], order = "hlabcdefgijkmnopqrstuvwxyz"` → `true`

**Algorithm:**
1. Use int[26] frequency array for O(1) char counting (lowercase letters).
2. Scan string, increment/decrement counts.
3. Check condition on frequency array (all zeros, single non-zero, etc.).
`O(n) / O(n)`

```java
class Solution {
    public boolean isAlienSorted(String[] words, String order) {
        int[] index = new int[26];
        for(int i = 0; i < order.length(); i++) {
            index[order.charAt(i) - 'a'] = i;
        }
        for(int i = 1; i < words.length; i++) {
            if(!isAlienSorted(words[i-1], words[i], index)) {
                return false;
            }
        }
        return true;
    }
    public boolean isAlienSorted(String word1, String word2, int[] index) {
        int i, j;
        for(i = 0, j = 0; i < word1.length() && j < word2.length(); i++, j++) {
            if(index[word1.charAt(i) - 'a'] > index[word2.charAt(j) - 'a']) {
                return false;
            } else if(index[word1.charAt(i) - 'a'] < index[word2.charAt(j) - 'a']) {
                return true;
            }
        }
        return i == word1.length();
    }
}
class Solution {
    public boolean isAlienSorted(String[] words, String order) {
        int[] rank = new int[26];
        for(int i = 0; i < order.length(); i++) {
            rank[order.charAt(i) - 'a'] = i;
        }
        for(int i = 1; i < words.length; i++) {
            if(!isSorted(words[i-1], words[i], rank)) {
                return false;
            }
        }
        return true;
    }
    public boolean isSorted(String word1, String word2, int[] rank) {
        int i = 0, j = 0;
        while(i < word1.length() && j < word2.length()) {
            if(rank[word1.charAt(i) - 'a'] < rank[word2.charAt(j) - 'a']) {
                return true;
            }
            if(rank[word1.charAt(i) - 'a'] > rank[word2.charAt(j) - 'a']) {
                return false;
            }
            i++;
            j++;
        }
        return i == word1.length();
    }
}
```

- Use char[] instead of String concatenation in loops — avoids O(n²) copies.
- int[26] char frequency array is faster than HashMap for lowercase-only strings.

#### #1055. Shortest Way to Form String

[leetcode.com/problems/shortest-way-to-form-string/](https://leetcode.com/problems/shortest-way-to-form-string/) · `O(n) / O(n)`

**Algorithm:**
1. Identify string operation: search, transform, validate, or parse.
2. Use appropriate data structure: char array, HashMap, stack, or two pointers.
3. Process characters with relevant conditions.
4. Build or return result.
`O(n) / O(n)`

```java
class Solution {
    public int shortestWay(String source, String target) {
        int n = source.length();
        int j = 0 ;
        int count = 0 ;
        while( j < target.length() ){
            int previous = j ;
            for(int i = 0 ; i < n ; i++){
                // sourcetargetsourcejtargetjjsource
                // targetsource
                if( j < target.length() && source.charAt(i) == target.charAt(j) )
                j++;
            }
            if( previous == j ) //j
            return -1;
            count++;
        }
        return count ;
    }
}
```

- Use char[] instead of String concatenation in loops — avoids O(n²) copies.
- int[26] char frequency array is faster than HashMap for lowercase-only strings.
