#### #1108. Defanging an IP Address

[leetcode.com/problems/defanging-an-ip-address/](https://leetcode.com/problems/defanging-an-ip-address/) · `O(n) / O(n)`

**Q:** Defang an IP address by replacing '.' with '[.]'.

**Algorithm:**
1. Use StringBuilder for efficient string building.
2. Process characters left to right; apply transformation rules.
3. Append to builder; handle edge cases (spaces, special chars).
4. Return builder.toString().
`O(n) / O(n)`

```java
class Solution {
    public String defangIPaddr(String address) {
        if(address == null || address.length() == 0) {
            return address;
        }
        StringBuilder builder = new StringBuilder();
        for(char ch : address.toCharArray()) {
            if(ch == '.') {
                builder.append("[.]");
            } else {
                builder.append(ch);
            }
        }
        return builder.toString();
    }
}
```

- Use char[] instead of String concatenation in loops — avoids O(n²) copies.
- int[26] char frequency array is faster than HashMap for lowercase-only strings.

#### #1221. Split a String in Balanced Strings

[leetcode.com/problems/split-a-string-in-balanced-strings/](https://leetcode.com/problems/split-a-string-in-balanced-strings/) · `O(n) / O(n)`

**Q:** Find the maximum number of balanced strings ('R' count == 'L' count) to split into.

**Algorithm:**
1. Identify string operation: search, transform, validate, or parse.
2. Use appropriate data structure: char array, HashMap, stack, or two pointers.
3. Process characters with relevant conditions.
4. Build or return result.
`O(n) / O(n)`

```java
class Solution {
    //  s  s  s
    public int balancedStringSplit(String s) {
        int result = 0, d = 0;
        for (int i = 0; i < s.length(); ++i) {
            char ch = s.charAt(i);
            if (ch == 'L') {
                ++d;
            } else {
                --d;
            }
            if (d == 0) {
                ++result;
            }
        }
        return result;
    }
}
```

- Use char[] instead of String concatenation in loops — avoids O(n²) copies.
- int[26] char frequency array is faster than HashMap for lowercase-only strings.

#### #1328. Break a Palindrome

[leetcode.com/problems/break-a-palindrome/](https://leetcode.com/problems/break-a-palindrome/) · `O(n) / O(n)`

**Q:** Break a palindrome by changing one character to get the lexicographically smallest non-palindrome.

**Algorithm:**
1. Use StringBuilder for efficient string building.
2. Process characters left to right; apply transformation rules.
3. Append to builder; handle edge cases (spaces, special chars).
4. Return builder.toString().
`O(n) / O(n)`

```java
class Solution {
    // aab
    public String breakPalindrome(String palindrome) {
        if(palindrome == null || palindrome.length() == 0) {
            return palindrome;
        }
        if(palindrome.length() == 1) {
            return "";
        }
        int n = palindrome.length();
        char[] arr = palindrome.toCharArray();
        StringBuilder sb = new StringBuilder(palindrome);
        for(int i = 0;i < n / 2;i++){
            if(arr[i] != 'a'){
                arr[i] = 'a';
                return new String(arr);
            }
        }
        arr[n-1] = 'b';
        return new String(arr);
    }
}
```

- Use char[] instead of String concatenation in loops — avoids O(n²) copies.
- int[26] char frequency array is faster than HashMap for lowercase-only strings.

#### #1392. Longest Happy Prefix

[leetcode.com/problems/longest-happy-prefix/](https://leetcode.com/problems/longest-happy-prefix/) · `O(n) / O(n)`

**Q:** Find the longest happy prefix (prefix that is also a suffix).

**Algorithm:**
1. Identify string operation: search, transform, validate, or parse.
2. Use appropriate data structure: char array, HashMap, stack, or two pointers.
3. Process characters with relevant conditions.
4. Build or return result.
`O(n) / O(n)`

```java
class Solution {
    // Rabin-Karp
    //  s  t
    //  k  k base
    //  mod
    public String longestPrefix(String s) {
        int n = s.length();
        long prefix = 0, suffix = 0;
        long base = 31, mod = 1000000007, mul = 1;
        int happy = 0;
        for (int i = 1; i < n; ++i) {
            prefix = (prefix * base + (s.charAt(i - 1) - 'a')) % mod;
            suffix = (suffix + (s.charAt(n - i) - 'a') * mul) % mod;
            if (prefix == suffix) {
                happy = i;
            }
            mul = mul * base % mod;
        }
        return s.substring(0, happy);
    }
}
```

- Use char[] instead of String concatenation in loops — avoids O(n²) copies.
- int[26] char frequency array is faster than HashMap for lowercase-only strings.

#### #1446. Consecutive Characters

[leetcode.com/problems/consecutive-characters/](https://leetcode.com/problems/consecutive-characters/) · `O(n) / O(n)`

**Q:** Find the maximum number of consecutive same characters in a string.

**Algorithm:**
1. Identify string operation: search, transform, validate, or parse.
2. Use appropriate data structure: char array, HashMap, stack, or two pointers.
3. Process characters with relevant conditions.
4. Build or return result.
`O(n) / O(n)`

```java
class Solution {
    public int maxPower(String s) {
        int result = 1, count = 1;
        for (int i = 1; i < s.length(); ++i) {
            if (s.charAt(i) == s.charAt(i - 1)) {
                count++;
                result = Math.max(result, count);
            } else {
                count = 1;
            }
        }
        return result;
    }
}
class Solution {
    public int maxPower(String s) {
        int n = s.length(), result = 1;
        for (int i = 0; i < n; ) {
            int j = i;
            while (j < n && s.charAt(j) == s.charAt(i)) j++;
            result = Math.max(result, j - i);
            i = j;
        }
        return result;
    }
}
```

- Use char[] instead of String concatenation in loops — avoids O(n²) copies.
- int[26] char frequency array is faster than HashMap for lowercase-only strings.

#### #1554. Strings Differ by One Character

[leetcode.com/problems/strings-differ-by-one-character/](https://leetcode.com/problems/strings-differ-by-one-character/) · `O(n) / O(n)`

**Q:** Check if any two strings in a list differ by exactly one character.

**Algorithm:**
1. Identify string operation: search, transform, validate, or parse.
2. Use appropriate data structure: char array, HashMap, stack, or two pointers.
3. Process characters with relevant conditions.
4. Build or return result.
`O(n) / O(n)`

```java
class Solution {
    public boolean differByOne(String[] dict) {
        HashSet<String> set = new HashSet<>();
        int n = dict.length;
        for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
            for (int j = 0; j < dict[i].length(); j++) {
                String str = dict[i].substring(0, j) + "#" + dict[i].substring(j + 1);
                if (set.contains(str)) return true;
                set.add(str);
            }
        }
        return false;
    }
}
```

- Use char[] instead of String concatenation in loops — avoids O(n²) copies.
- int[26] char frequency array is faster than HashMap for lowercase-only strings.

#### #1556. Thousand Separator

[leetcode.com/problems/thousand-separator/](https://leetcode.com/problems/thousand-separator/) · `O(n) / O(n)`

**Algorithm:**
1. Use StringBuilder for efficient string building.
2. Process characters left to right; apply transformation rules.
3. Append to builder; handle edge cases (spaces, special chars).
4. Return builder.toString().
`O(n) / O(n)`

```java
class Solution {
    public String thousandSeparator(int n) {
        StringBuilder builder = new StringBuilder();
        String s = String.valueOf(n);
        int count = 0;
        for (int i = s.length() - 1; i >= 0; i--) {
            builder.append(s.charAt(i));
            count++;
            // cnt%3==0i!=0()
            if (count % 3 == 0 && i != 0) {
                builder.append(".");
            }
        }
        return builder.reverse().toString();
    }
}
```

- Use char[] instead of String concatenation in loops — avoids O(n²) copies.
- int[26] char frequency array is faster than HashMap for lowercase-only strings.

#### #1736. Latest Time by Replacing Hidden Digits

[leetcode.com/problems/latest-time-by-replacing-hidden-digits/](https://leetcode.com/problems/latest-time-by-replacing-hidden-digits/) · `O(n) / O(n)`

**Q:** Find the latest valid time by replacing '?' with appropriate digits.

**Algorithm:**
1. Identify string operation: search, transform, validate, or parse.
2. Use appropriate data structure: char array, HashMap, stack, or two pointers.
3. Process characters with relevant conditions.
4. Build or return result.
`O(n) / O(n)`

```java
class Solution {
    public String maximumTime(String time) {
        char[] arr = time.toCharArray();
        if (arr[0] == '?') {
            arr[0] = ('4' <= arr[1] && arr[1] <= '9') ? '1' : '2';
        }
        if (arr[1] == '?') {
            arr[1] = (arr[0] == '2') ? '3' : '9';
        }
        if (arr[3] == '?') {
            arr[3] = '5';
        }
        if (arr[4] == '?') {
            arr[4] = '9';
        }
        return new String(arr);
    }
}
```

- Use char[] instead of String concatenation in loops — avoids O(n²) copies.
- int[26] char frequency array is faster than HashMap for lowercase-only strings.

#### #1816. Truncate Sentence

[leetcode.com/problems/truncate-sentence/](https://leetcode.com/problems/truncate-sentence/) · `O(n) / O(n)`

**Q:** Truncate a sentence to the first k words.

**Algorithm:**
1. Identify string operation: search, transform, validate, or parse.
2. Use appropriate data structure: char array, HashMap, stack, or two pointers.
3. Process characters with relevant conditions.
4. Build or return result.
`O(n) / O(n)`

```java
class Solution {
    public String truncateSentence(String s, int k) {
        int n = s.length();
        int end = 0, count = 0;
        for (int i = 1; i <= n; i++) {
            if (i == n || s.charAt(i) == ' ') {
                count++;
                if (count == k) {
                    end = i;
                    break;
                }
            }
        }
        return s.substring(0, end);
    }
}
```

- Use char[] instead of String concatenation in loops — avoids O(n²) copies.
- int[26] char frequency array is faster than HashMap for lowercase-only strings.

---

#### #1844. Replace All Digits with Characters

[leetcode.com/problems/replace-all-digits-with-characters/](https://leetcode.com/problems/replace-all-digits-with-characters/) · `O(n) / O(n)`

**Q:** Replace each digit at odd positions with the corresponding letter.

**Algorithm:**
1. Identify string operation: search, transform, validate, or parse.
2. Use appropriate data structure: char array, HashMap, stack, or two pointers.
3. Process characters with relevant conditions.
4. Build or return result.
`O(n) / O(n)`

```java
class Solution {
    public String replaceDigits(String s) {
        if(s == null || s.length() == 0) {
            return s;
        }
        char[] arr = s.toCharArray();
        for(int i = 1; i < s.length(); i = i + 2) {
            arr[i] = (char) (arr[i-1] + (int) (arr[i] - '0'));
        }
        return new String(arr);
    }
}
```

- Use char[] instead of String concatenation in loops — avoids O(n²) copies.
- int[26] char frequency array is faster than HashMap for lowercase-only strings.

---

### Greedy (28 problems)

#### #45. Jump Game II

[leetcode.com/problems/jump-game-ii/](https://leetcode.com/problems/jump-game-ii/) · `O(n) / O(1)`

**Q:** Find the minimum number of jumps to reach the last index. Each element is max jump length.

**Example:** `nums = [2,3,1,1,4]` → `2`

**Algorithm:**
1. Track current_end (reach of current jump) and farthest (max reach so far).
2. When i == current_end: increment jumps, update current_end = farthest.
3. Return jumps when current_end >= n-1.
`O(n) / O(1)`

```java
class Solution {
    public int jump(int[] nums) {
        int length = nums.length;
        int bound = 0;
        int maxPosition = 0;
        int steps = 0;
        for (int i = 0; i < length - 1; i++) {
            //  1
            maxPosition = Math.max(maxPosition, i + nums[i]);
            if (i == bound) {
                bound = maxPosition;
                steps++;
            }
        }
        return steps;
    }
}
class Solution {
    // DFS
    // BFS
    // Union Find
    // DP
    // greedy
    public int jump(int[] nums) {
        if(nums == null || nums.length == 0) {
            return -1;
        }
        if(nums.length == 1) {
            return 0;
        }
        int end = 0;
        int bound = 0;
        int jump = 0;
        for(int i = 0; i < nums.length; i++) {
            if(i <= bound) {
                end = Math.max(end, i + nums[i]);
            } else {
                jump++;
                bound = end;
                end = Math.max(end, i + nums[i]);
                if(bound < i) {
                    return -1;
                }
            }
        }
        return jump;
    }
}
class Solution {
    // DFS
    // BFS
    // Union Find
    // DP
    // greedy
    public int jump(int[] nums) {
        if(nums == null || nums.length == 0) {
            return -1;
        }
        if(nums.length == 1) {
            return 0;
        }
        int n = nums.length;
        int[] dp = new int[n];
        Arrays.fill(dp, Integer.MAX_VALUE);
        dp[0] = 0;
        for(int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
            for(int j = 0; j < i; j++) {
                if(j + nums[j] >= i && dp[j] != Integer.MAX_VALUE) {
                    dp[i] = Math.min(dp[i], dp[j] + 1);
                }
            }
        }
        return dp[n-1];
    }
}
class Solution {
    // DFS
    // BFS
    // Union Find
    // DP
    // greedy
    public int jump(int[] nums) {
        if(nums == null || nums.length == 0) {
            return -1;
        }
        if(nums.length == 1) {
            return 0;
        }
        int n = nums.length;
        int[] dp = new int[n];
        Arrays.fill(dp, Integer.MAX_VALUE);
        dp[0] = 0;
        for(int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
            for(int j = 1; j <= nums[i]; j++) {
                if(i + j < n) {
                    dp[i+j] = Math.min(dp[i+j], dp[i] + 1);
                }
            }
        }
        return dp[n-1];
    }
}
class Solution {
    // DFS
    // BFS
    // Union Find
    // DP
    // greedy
    public int jump(int[] nums) {
        if(nums == null || nums.length == 0) {
            return -1;
        }
        if(nums.length == 1) {
            return 0;
        }
        int n = nums.length;
        boolean[] visited = new boolean[n];
        Deque<Integer> queue = new ArrayDeque<>();
        queue.offer(0);
        visited[0] = true;
        for(int distance = 0; !queue.isEmpty(); distance++) {
            for(int size = queue.size(); size > 0; size--) {
                int current = queue.poll();
                if(current == n - 1) {
                    return distance;
                }
                for(int i = 1; i <= nums[current] && current + i < n; i++) {
                    if(!visited[current + i]) {
                        visited[current + i] = true;
                        queue.offer(current + i);
                    }
                }
            }
        }
        return -1;
    }
}
```

- BFS-style greedy is O(n) and optimal; DP solution would be O(n^2) — avoid for large inputs.

#### #55. Jump Game

[leetcode.com/problems/jump-game/](https://leetcode.com/problems/jump-game/) · `O(n log n) / O(1)`

**Q:** Given array of jump lengths, determine if you can reach the last index from index 0.

**Example:** `nums = [2,3,1,1,4]` → `true`

**Algorithm:**
1. Identify greedy choice: local optimum at each step.
2. Prove exchange argument: swapping any two choices doesn't improve result.
3. Iterate and make greedy decision at each position.
4. Return accumulated result.
`O(n log n) / O(1)`

```java
class Solution {
    public boolean canJump(int[] nums) {
        int canReach = 0;
        int n = nums.length;
        for(int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
            if(i > canReach) {
                return false;
            }
            canReach = Math.max(canReach, i + nums[i]);
        }
        return true;
    }
}
```

- Prove greedy by exchange argument: any swap of two choices doesn't improve result.
- If greedy fails, check if DP is needed (overlapping subproblems).

---

#### #56. Merge Intervals

[leetcode.com/problems/merge-intervals/](https://leetcode.com/problems/merge-intervals/) · `O(n log n) / O(n)`

**Q:** Merge all overlapping intervals in a collection and return non-overlapping sorted intervals.

**Example:** `intervals = [[1,3],[2,6],[8,10],[15,18]]` → `[[1,6],[8,10],[15,18]]`

**Algorithm:**
1. Sort intervals by start time.
2. Initialize result with first interval.
3. For each subsequent interval: if start <= result.last.end, merge (update end = max); else append.
`O(n log n) / O(n)`

```java
class Solution {
    public int[][] merge(int[][] intervals) {
        if(intervals == null || intervals.length == 0 || intervals[0].length == 0) {
            return intervals;
        }
        Arrays.sort(intervals, (a, b) -> (a[0] - b[0]));
        int[] current = intervals[0];
        List<int[]> list = new ArrayList<>();
        for(int[] interval : intervals) {
            if(interval[0] <= current[1]) {
                // current[0] = Math.min(current[0], interval[0]);
                current[1] = Math.max(current[1], interval[1]);
            } else {
                list.add(current);
                current = interval;
            }
        }
        list.add(current);
        return list.toArray(new int[list.size()][]);
    }
}
```

- Sorting is O(n log n); the merge pass is O(n). Total optimal for comparison-based sorting.

---

#### #57. Insert Interval

[leetcode.com/problems/insert-interval/](https://leetcode.com/problems/insert-interval/) · `O(n log n) / O(1)`

**Q:** Insert a new interval into a sorted non-overlapping list of intervals, merging if necessary.

**Example:** `intervals = [[1,3],[6,9]], newInterval = [2,5]` → `[[1,5],[6,9]]`

**Algorithm:**
1. Identify greedy choice: local optimum at each step.
2. Prove exchange argument: swapping any two choices doesn't improve result.
3. Iterate and make greedy decision at each position.
4. Return accumulated result.
`O(n log n) / O(1)`

```java
class Solution {
    public int[][] insert(int[][] intervals, int[] newInterval) {
        List<int[]> list = new ArrayList<>();
        // check edge case
        if(intervals == null) return intervals;
        int current = 0;
        // skip all the previous ones
        while(current < intervals.length && intervals[current][1] < newInterval[0]) {
            list.add(intervals[current]);
            current++;
        }
        // process the insertion
        while(current < intervals.length && intervals[current][0] <= newInterval[1]) {
            newInterval[0] = Math.min(newInterval[0], intervals[current][0]);
            newInterval[1] = Math.max(newInterval[1], intervals[current][1]);
            current++;
        }
        list.add(newInterval);
        // skip all the ones after the insertion
        while(current < intervals.length) {
            list.add(intervals[current]);
            current++;
        }
        return list.toArray(new int[list.size()][2]);
    }
}
class Solution {
    public int[][] insert(int[][] intervals, int[] newInterval) {
        List<int[]> list = new ArrayList<>();
        for(int[] interval : intervals) {
            if(interval[1] < newInterval[0]) {
                list.add(interval);
            } else if (interval[0] > newInterval[1]) {
                list.add(newInterval);
                newInterval = interval;
            } else {
                newInterval[0] = Math.min(newInterval[0], interval[0]);
                newInterval[1] = Math.max(newInterval[1], interval[1]);
            }
        }
        list.add(newInterval);
        return list.toArray(new int[list.size()][2]);
    }
}
```

- Use 1D rolling array if dp[i] only depends on previous row — reduces space from O(mn) to O(n).

---

#### #252. Meeting Rooms

[leetcode.com/problems/meeting-rooms/](https://leetcode.com/problems/meeting-rooms/) · `O(n log n) / O(1)`

**Q:** Determine if a person can attend all meetings (no overlapping intervals).

**Example:** `intervals = [[0,30],[5,10],[15,20]]` → `false`

**Algorithm:**
1. Sort by key property (end time, value/weight ratio, start, etc.).
2. Greedily pick items: take current if it doesn't violate constraint.
3. Update running state (end pointer, remaining capacity, etc.).
4. Return count or accumulated result.
`O(n log n) / O(1)`

```java
class Solution {
    public boolean canAttendMeetings(int[][] intervals) {
        if(intervals == null || intervals.length == 0) {
            return true;
        }
        Arrays.sort(intervals, (a, b) -> (a[0] - b[0]));
        int[] current = intervals[0];
        for(int i = 1; i < intervals.length; i++) {
            int[] interval = intervals[i];
            if(current[1] > interval[0]) {
                return false;
            } else {
                current = interval;
            }
        }
        return true;
    }
}
```

- Use 1D rolling array if dp[i] only depends on previous row — reduces space from O(mn) to O(n).
- After sorting, use binary search (O(log n)) instead of linear scan for lookups.

---

#### #253. Meeting Rooms II

[leetcode.com/problems/meeting-rooms-ii/](https://leetcode.com/problems/meeting-rooms-ii/) · `O(n log n) / O(n)`

**Q:** Find the minimum number of conference rooms required to hold all meetings.

**Example:** `intervals = [[0,30],[5,10],[15,20]]` → `2`

**Algorithm:**
1. Create events: +1 at meeting start, -1 at meeting end.
2. Sort events by time.
3. Sweep through; track running count; record maximum.
`O(n log n) / O(n)`

```java
class Solution {
    public int minMeetingRooms(int[][] intervals) {
        if(intervals == null || intervals.length == 0 || intervals[0].length == 0) {
            return 0;
        }
        PriorityQueue<int[]> pq = new PriorityQueue<>((interval1, interval2) -> interval1[1] - interval2[1]);
        Arrays.sort(intervals, (interval1, interval2) -> interval1[0] - interval2[0]);
        for(int[] interval : intervals) {
            if(!pq.isEmpty() && pq.peek()[1] <= interval[0]) {
                pq.poll();
            }
            pq.offer(interval);
        }
        return pq.size();
    }
}
```

- Sorting by start time with a min-heap of end times is an alternative approach.

---

#### #435. Non-overlapping Intervals

[leetcode.com/problems/nonoverlapping-intervals/](https://leetcode.com/problems/nonoverlapping-intervals/) · `O(n log n) / O(1)`

**Q:** Find the minimum number of intervals to remove so the rest don't overlap.

**Example:** `intervals = [[1,2],[2,3],[3,4],[1,3]]` → `1`

**Algorithm:**
1. Sort by key property (end time, value/weight ratio, start, etc.).
2. Greedily pick items: take current if it doesn't violate constraint.
3. Update running state (end pointer, remaining capacity, etc.).
4. Return count or accumulated result.
`O(n log n) / O(1)`

```java
class Solution {
    //
    public int eraseOverlapIntervals(int[][] intervals) {
        if(intervals == null) {
            return 0;
        }
        Arrays.sort(intervals, (a, b) -> (a[1] - b[1]));
        int[] current = intervals[0];
        int count = 1;
        for(int i = 1; i < intervals.length; i++) {
            if(current[1] <= intervals[i][0]) {
                count++;
                current = intervals[i];
            }
        }
        return intervals.length - count;
    }
}
```

- Use 1D rolling array if dp[i] only depends on previous row — reduces space from O(mn) to O(n).
- After sorting, use binary search (O(log n)) instead of linear scan for lookups.

#### #986. Interval List Intersections

[leetcode.com/problems/interval-list-intersections/](https://leetcode.com/problems/interval-list-intersections/) · `O(n log n) / O(1)`

**Q:** Find all intersections of two lists of intervals.

**Example:** `firstList = [[0,2],[5,10],[13,23],[24,25]], secondList = [[1,5],[8,12],[15,24],[25,26]]` → `[[1,2],[5,5],[8,10],[15,23],[24,24],[25,25]]`

**Algorithm:**
1. Identify greedy choice: local optimum at each step.
2. Prove exchange argument: swapping any two choices doesn't improve result.
3. Iterate and make greedy decision at each position.
4. Return accumulated result.
`O(n log n) / O(1)`

```java
class Solution {
    //  b  [a, b]
    //  A[0] A )
    //  B  A[0]  B  B  A[0]  A[0]  B
    //  A[0]  B[0]  A[0]
    public int[][] intervalIntersection(int[][] firstList, int[][] secondList) {
        List<int[]> intersections = new ArrayList<>();
        int p1 = 0, p2 = 0;
        while(p1 < firstList.length && p2 < secondList.length) {
            int[] first = firstList[p1];
            int[] second = secondList[p2];
            int start = Math.max(first[0], second[0]);
            int end = Math.min(first[1], second[1]);
            if(start <= end) {
                intersections.add(new int[]{start, end});
            }
            if(first[1] < second[1]) {
                p1++;
            } else {
                p2++;
            }
        }
        return intersections.toArray(new int[intersections.size()][2]);
    }
}
```

- Use 1D rolling array if dp[i] only depends on previous row — reduces space from O(mn) to O(n).

---

#### #1272. Remove Interval

[leetcode.com/problems/remove-interval/](https://leetcode.com/problems/remove-interval/) · `O(n log n) / O(1)`

**Q:** Given a sorted list of non-overlapping intervals and a removal interval, return the list after removing all parts covered by the removal interval.

**Algorithm:**
1. For each interval: if it ends before removal or starts after removal, keep whole. Otherwise clip left part (if any) and clip right part (if any).
`O(n log n) / O(1)`

```java
class Solution {
    public List<List<Integer>> removeInterval(int[][] intervals, int[] toBeRemoved) {
        List<List<Integer>> result = new ArrayList<>();
        int removeStart = toBeRemoved[0], removeEnd = toBeRemoved[1];
        for (int[] interval : intervals) {
            int start = interval[0], end = interval[1];
            if (end <= removeStart || start >= removeEnd) {
                result.add(Arrays.asList(start, end));  // no overlap — keep whole
            } else {
                if (start < removeStart) result.add(Arrays.asList(start, removeStart));
                if (end > removeEnd)     result.add(Arrays.asList(removeEnd, end));
            }
        }
        return result;
    }
}
```

- No need to sort: problem guarantees intervals are already sorted and non-overlapping.
- Handle boundary cases: interval exactly touching removal edge is NOT removed.

---

#### #134. Gas Station

[leetcode.com/problems/gas-station/](https://leetcode.com/problems/gas-station/) · `O(n log n) / O(1)`

**Q:** Find the starting gas station index from which you can complete a circular route. Return -1 if impossible.

**Example:** `gas = [1,2,3,4,5], cost = [3,4,5,1,2]` → `3`

**Algorithm:**
1. Identify greedy choice: local optimum at each step.
2. Prove exchange argument: swapping any two choices doesn't improve result.
3. Iterate and make greedy decision at each position.
4. Return accumulated result.
`O(n log n) / O(1)`

```java
class Solution {
    public int canCompleteCircuit(int[] gas, int[] cost) {
        int n = gas.length;
        int i = 0;
        while (i < n) {
            int sumOfGas = 0, sumOfCost = 0;
            int count = 0;
            while (count < n) {
                int j = (i + count) % n;
                sumOfGas += gas[j];
                sumOfCost += cost[j];
                if (sumOfCost > sumOfGas) {
                    break;
                }
                count++;
            }
            if (count == n) {
                return i;
            } else {
                i = i + count + 1;
            }
        }
        return -1;
    }
}
class Solution {
    public int canCompleteCircuit(int[] gas, int[] cost) {
        // find if we can has solution;
        int overall = 0;
        for(int i = 0; i < gas.length; i++) {
            overall += gas[i] - cost[i];
        }
        if(overall < 0) {
            return -1;
        }
        // find where to start;
        int tank = 0; int start = 0;
        for(int i = 0; i < gas.length; i++) {
            tank += gas[i] - cost[i];
            if(tank < 0) {
                start = i + 1;
                tank = 0;
            }
        }
        return start;
    }
}
class Solution {
    public int canCompleteCircuit(int[] gas, int[] cost) {
        int total = 0;
        int n = gas.length;
        for(int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
            total += gas[i];
            total -= cost[i];
        }
        if(total < 0) {
            return -1;
        }
        int i = 0;
        while(i < n) {
            int gasLevel = 0;
            int j = 0;
            while(i + j < n && gasLevel >= 0) {
                gasLevel += gas[i+j];
                gasLevel -= cost[i+j];
                j++;
            }
            if(i + j == n) {
                return i;
            } else {
                i = i + j;
            }
        }
        return -1;
    }
}
```

- Prove greedy by exchange argument: any swap of two choices doesn't improve result.
- If greedy fails, check if DP is needed (overlapping subproblems).

---

#### #135. Candy

[leetcode.com/problems/candy/](https://leetcode.com/problems/candy/) · `O(n log n) / O(1)`

**Q:** Assign minimum candies to children in a line: each child ≥1 candy; child with higher rating than neighbor gets more.

**Example:** `ratings = [1,0,2]` → `5`

**Algorithm:**
1. Identify greedy choice: local optimum at each step.
2. Prove exchange argument: swapping any two choices doesn't improve result.
3. Iterate and make greedy decision at each position.
4. Return accumulated result.
`O(n log n) / O(1)`

```java
class Solution {
    //
    public int candy(int[] ratings) {
        int n = ratings.length;
        int[] left = new int[n];
        for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
            if (i > 0 && ratings[i] > ratings[i - 1]) {
                left[i] = left[i - 1] + 1;
            } else {
                left[i] = 1;
            }
        }
        int right = 0, ret = 0;
        for (int i = n - 1; i >= 0; i--) {
            if (i < n - 1 && ratings[i] > ratings[i + 1]) {
                right++;
            } else {
                right = 1;
            }
            ret += Math.max(left[i], right);
        }
        return ret;
    }
}
class Solution {
    public int candy(int[] ratings) {
        int n = ratings.length;
        int ret = 1;
        int inc = 1, dec = 0, pre = 1;
        for (int i = 1; i < n; i++) {
            //  pre+1
            if (ratings[i] >= ratings[i - 1]) {
                dec = 0;
                pre = ratings[i] == ratings[i - 1] ? 1 : pre + 1;
                ret += pre;
                inc = pre;
            } else {
                //
                dec++;
                if (dec == inc) {
                    dec++;
                }
                ret += dec;
                pre = 1;
            }
        }
        return ret;
    }
}
```

- Prove greedy by exchange argument: any swap of two choices doesn't improve result.
- If greedy fails, check if DP is needed (overlapping subproblems).

---

#### #274. H-Index

[leetcode.com/problems/hindex/](https://leetcode.com/problems/hindex/) · `O(n log n) / O(1)`

**Q:** Given citation counts, find the largest h such that h papers have ≥h citations each.

**Example:** `citations = [3,0,6,1,5]` → `3`

**Algorithm:**
1. Sort by key property (end time, value/weight ratio, start, etc.).
2. Greedily pick items: take current if it doesn't violate constraint.
3. Update running state (end pointer, remaining capacity, etc.).
4. Return count or accumulated result.
`O(n log n) / O(1)`

```java
class Solution {
    public int hIndex(int[] citations) {
        int n = citations.length;
        Arrays.sort(citations);
        int h = 0, i = n - 1;
        while(i >= 0 && citations[i] > h) {
            i--;
            h++;
        }
        return h;
    }
}
public class Solution {
    public int hIndex(int[] citations) {
        int n = citations.length, tot = 0;
        int[] counter = new int[n + 1];
        for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
            if (citations[i] >= n) {
                counter[n]++;
            } else {
                counter[citations[i]]++;
            }
        }
        for (int i = n; i >= 0; i--) {
            tot += counter[i];
            if (tot >= i) {
                return i;
            }
        }
        return 0;
    }
}
```

- After sorting, use binary search (O(log n)) instead of linear scan for lookups.

---

#### #358. Rearrange String k Distance Apart

[leetcode.com/problems/rearrange-string-k-distance-apart/](https://leetcode.com/problems/rearrange-string-k-distance-apart/) · `O(n log n) / O(1)`

**Algorithm:**
1. Identify greedy choice: local optimum at each step.
2. Prove exchange argument: swapping any two choices doesn't improve result.
3. Iterate and make greedy decision at each position.
4. Return accumulated result.
`O(n log n) / O(1)`

```java
class Solution {
    private class Node {
        public char c;
        public int fre;
        public Node(char c, int fre) {
            this.c = c;
            this.fre = fre;
        }
    }
    public String rearrangeString(String s, int k) {
        if(s == null || s.length() == 0) {
            return s;
        }
        HashMap<Character, Integer> countmap = new HashMap<>();
        PriorityQueue<Node> pq = new PriorityQueue<>((a, b) -> (b.fre - a.fre));
        for(int i = 0; i < s.length(); i++) {
            char c = s.charAt(i);
            countmap.put(c, countmap.getOrDefault(c, 0) + 1);
        }
        for(Character c: countmap.keySet()) {
            pq.offer(new Node(c, countmap.get(c)));
        }
        StringBuilder sb = new StringBuilder();
        Queue<Node> waitqueue = new LinkedList<>();
        while(!pq.isEmpty()) {
            Node node = pq.poll();
            sb.append(node.c);
            node.fre--;
            waitqueue.add(node);
            if(waitqueue.size() < k) {
                continue;
            }
            Node waitnode = waitqueue.poll();
            if(waitnode.fre > 0) {
                pq.offer(waitnode);
            }
        }
        return sb.length() == s.length() ? sb.toString() : "";
    }
}
```

- Return early on first HashMap hit rather than scanning full input.

---

#### #406. Queue Reconstruction by Height

[leetcode.com/problems/queue-reconstruction-by-height/](https://leetcode.com/problems/queue-reconstruction-by-height/) · `O(n^2) / O(n)`

**Q:** Reconstruct a queue of people given (height, k) pairs where k = taller-or-equal people in front.

**Example:** `people = [[7,0],[4,4],[7,1],[5,0],[6,1],[5,2]]` → `[[5,0],[7,0],[5,2],[6,1],[4,4],[7,1]]`

**Algorithm:**
1. Sort by height descending; for same height, sort by k ascending.
2. Insert each person at their k position in result list.
3. Taller people already placed don't invalidate shorter people's k count.
`O(n^2) / O(n)`

```java
class Solution {
    // KKK
    public int[][] reconstructQueue(int[][] people) {
        Arrays.sort(people, new Comparator<int[]>() {
            public int compare(int[] person1, int[] person2) {
                if (person1[0] != person2[0]) {
                    return person2[0] - person1[0];
                } else {
                    return person1[1] - person2[1];
                }
            }
        });
        List<int[]> result = new ArrayList<>();
        for (int[] person : people) {
            result.add(person[1], person);
        }
        return result.toArray(new int[result.size()][]);
    }
}
```

- Insertion at arbitrary index in ArrayList is O(n) per person — total O(n^2). Acceptable for interview.

---

#### #452. Minimum Number of Arrows to Burst Balloons

[leetcode.com/problems/minimum-number-of-arrows-to-burst-balloons/](https://leetcode.com/problems/minimum-number-of-arrows-to-burst-balloons/) · `O(n log n) / O(1)`

**Q:** Find the minimum number of arrows to burst all balloons (each arrow goes through all balloons at that x).

**Example:** `points = [[10,16],[2,8],[1,6],[7,12]]` → `2`

**Algorithm:**
1. Sort intervals by end coordinate.
2. Shoot arrow at intervals[0][1]; skip all intervals with start <= arrow position.
3. When interval's start > arrow: new arrow at this interval's end; count++.
`O(n log n) / O(1)`

```java
class Solution {
    //
    public int findMinArrowShots(int[][] intervals)  {
        if(intervals == null) {
            return 0;
        }
        Arrays.sort(intervals, (a, b) -> (a[1] - b[1]));
        int[] current = intervals[0];
        int count = 1;
        for(int i = 1; i < intervals.length; i++) {
            if(current[1] < intervals[i][0]) {
                count++;
                current = intervals[i];
            }
        }
        return count;
    }
}
```

- Sort by end coordinate (not start): greedy arrow at earliest end minimizes count.

---

#### #455. Assign Cookies

[leetcode.com/problems/assign-cookies/](https://leetcode.com/problems/assign-cookies/) · `O(n log n) / O(1)`

**Q:** Assign cookies greedily: each child needs minimum size, each cookie satisfies one child.

**Example:** `g = [1,2,3], s = [1,1]` → `1`

**Algorithm:**
1. Sort by key property (end time, value/weight ratio, start, etc.).
2. Greedily pick items: take current if it doesn't violate constraint.
3. Update running state (end pointer, remaining capacity, etc.).
4. Return count or accumulated result.
`O(n log n) / O(1)`

```java
class Solution {
    public int findContentChildren(int[] g, int[] s) {
        Arrays.sort(g);
        Arrays.sort(s);
        int i = 0, j = 0;
        int count = 0;
        while(i < g.length && j < s.length) {
            while(j < s.length && g[i] > s[j]) {
                j++;
            }
            if(j == s.length) {
                return count;
            }
            count++;
            i++;
            j++;
        }
        return count;
    }
}
Offer 04.
class Solution {
    public boolean findNumberIn2DArray(int[][] matrix, int target) {
        int i = matrix.length - 1, j = 0;
        while(i >= 0 && j < matrix[0].length)
        {
            if(matrix[i][j] > target) i--;
            else if(matrix[i][j] < target) j++;
            else return true;
        }
        return false;
    }
}
```

- Use 1D rolling array if dp[i] only depends on previous row — reduces space from O(mn) to O(n).
- After sorting, use binary search (O(log n)) instead of linear scan for lookups.

#### #575. Distribute Candies

[leetcode.com/problems/distribute-candies/](https://leetcode.com/problems/distribute-candies/) · `O(n log n) / O(1)`

**Q:** Distribute n candies (n/2 to eat) maximizing the number of distinct types.

**Example:** `quantity = [1,3,2,1,3,3,5]` → `false`

**Algorithm:**
1. Sort by key property (end time, value/weight ratio, start, etc.).
2. Greedily pick items: take current if it doesn't violate constraint.
3. Update running state (end pointer, remaining capacity, etc.).
4. Return count or accumulated result.
`O(n log n) / O(1)`

```java
class Solution {
    public int distributeCandies(int[] candyType) {
        Set<Integer> set = new HashSet<>();
        for (int candy : candyType) {
            set.add(candy);
        }
        return Math.min(set.size(), candyType.length / 2);
    }
}
public class Solution {
    public int distributeCandies(int[] candies) {
        Arrays.sort(candies);
        int count = 1;
        for (int i = 1; i < candies.length && count < candies.length / 2; i++)
        if (candies[i] > candies[i - 1])
        count++;
        return count;
    }
}
```

- After sorting, use binary search (O(log n)) instead of linear scan for lookups.

#### #605. Can Place Flowers

[leetcode.com/problems/can-place-flowers/](https://leetcode.com/problems/can-place-flowers/) · `O(n log n) / O(1)`

**Q:** Determine if n flowers can be planted in a flowerbed without adjacent flowers.

**Example:** `flowerbed = [1,0,0,0,1], n = 1` → `true`

**Algorithm:**
1. Identify greedy choice: local optimum at each step.
2. Prove exchange argument: swapping any two choices doesn't improve result.
3. Iterate and make greedy decision at each position.
4. Return accumulated result.
`O(n log n) / O(1)`

```java
class Solution {
    public boolean canPlaceFlowers(int[] flowerbed, int n) {
        int i = 0;
        int count = 0;
        while(i < flowerbed.length) {
            if(flowerbed[i] == 1) { //
                i = i + 2;
            } else if(i == flowerbed.length - 1 || flowerbed[i+1] == 0) {
                //
                count++;
                i = i + 2;
            } else {
                // 0 1
                i = i + 1;
            }
        }
        return count >= n;
    }
}
```

- Prove greedy by exchange argument: any swap of two choices doesn't improve result.
- If greedy fails, check if DP is needed (overlapping subproblems).

---

#### #621. Task Scheduler

[leetcode.com/problems/task-scheduler/](https://leetcode.com/problems/task-scheduler/) · `O(n) / O(1)`

**Q:** Find the minimum intervals needed to execute n tasks with cooldown k between same-type tasks.

**Example:** `tasks = ["A","A","A","B","B","B"], n = 2` → `8`

**Algorithm:**
1. Count task frequencies; find max frequency fmax and how many tasks share it.
2. Result = max(n, (fmax-1) * (n+1) + count_of_max_freq_tasks).
`O(n) / O(1)`

```java
class Solution {
    public int leastInterval(char[] tasks, int n) {
        if(tasks == null || tasks.length == 0) {
            return 0;
        }
        Map<Character, Integer> freq = new HashMap<>();
        int maxFreq = 0; //
        for(char task : tasks) {
            freq.put(task, freq.getOrDefault(task, 0) + 1);
            maxFreq = Math.max(maxFreq, freq.get(task));
        }
        int maxCount = 0; //task
        for(Character task : freq.keySet()) {
            if(freq.get(task) == maxFreq) {
                maxCount++;
            }
        }
        return Math.max((maxFreq - 1) * (n+1) + maxCount, tasks.length);
    }
}
```

- Mathematical formula avoids simulation. Key insight: max_freq determines minimum idle slots.

---

#### #630. Course Schedule III

[leetcode.com/problems/course-schedule-iii/](https://leetcode.com/problems/course-schedule-iii/) · `O(n log n) / O(1)`

**Q:** Find the maximum courses you can take given (duration, deadline) pairs.

**Example:** `courses = [[100,200],[200,1300],[1000,1250],[2000,3200]]` → `3`

**Algorithm:**
1. Sort by key property (end time, value/weight ratio, start, etc.).
2. Greedily pick items: take current if it doesn't violate constraint.
3. Update running state (end pointer, remaining capacity, etc.).
4. Return count or accumulated result.
`O(n log n) / O(1)`

```java
class Solution {
    public int scheduleCourse(int[][] courses) {
        Arrays.sort(courses, (a, b) -> a[1] - b[1]);
        PriorityQueue<Integer> q = new PriorityQueue<>((a, b) -> b - a);
        //
        int total = 0;
        //  sum + courses[i][0] <= courses[i][1]sum+courses[i][0]<=courses[i][1]
        //
        for (int[] course : courses) {
            int ti = course[0], di = course[1];
            if (total + ti <= di) {
                total += ti;
                q.offer(ti);
            } else if (!q.isEmpty() && q.peek() > ti) {
                total -= q.poll() - ti;
                q.offer(ti);
            }
        }
        return q.size();
    }
}
class Solution {
    public int scheduleCourse(int[][] courses) {
        //  sum + courses[i][0] <= courses[i][1]sum+courses[i][0]<=courses[i][1]
        //
        Arrays.sort(courses, (a,b)->a[1]-b[1]);
        PriorityQueue<Integer> q = new PriorityQueue<>((a,b)->b-a);
        int sum = 0;
        for (int[] c : courses) {
            int d = c[0], e = c[1];
            sum += d;
            q.add(d);
            if (sum > e) sum -= q.poll();
        }
        return q.size();
    }
}
```

- Use 1D rolling array if dp[i] only depends on previous row — reduces space from O(mn) to O(n).
- After sorting, use binary search (O(log n)) instead of linear scan for lookups.

#### #646. Maximum Length of Pair Chain

[leetcode.com/problems/maximum-length-of-pair-chain/](https://leetcode.com/problems/maximum-length-of-pair-chain/) · `O(n) / O(n)`

**Q:** Find the longest chain of pairs [a,b] where b < next_a.

**Example:** `pairs = [[1,2],[2,3],[3,4]]` → `2`

**Algorithm:**
1. Sort by key property (end time, value/weight ratio, start, etc.).
2. Greedily pick items: take current if it doesn't violate constraint.
3. Update running state (end pointer, remaining capacity, etc.).
4. Return count or accumulated result.
`O(n) / O(n)`

```java
class Solution {
    public int findLongestChain(int[][] pairs) {
        Arrays.sort(pairs, (a, b) -> a[0] - b[0]);
        int N = pairs.length;
        int[] dp = new int[N];
        Arrays.fill(dp, 1);
        for (int j = 1; j < N; ++j) {
            for (int i = 0; i < j; ++i) {
                if (pairs[i][1] < pairs[j][0])
                dp[j] = Math.max(dp[j], dp[i] + 1);
            }
        }
        int result = 0;
        for (int x: dp) if (x > result) result = x;
        return result;
    }
}
```

- Use 1D rolling array if dp[i] only depends on previous row — reduces space from O(mn) to O(n).
- After sorting, use binary search (O(log n)) instead of linear scan for lookups.

---

#### #763. Partition Labels

[leetcode.com/problems/partition-labels/](https://leetcode.com/problems/partition-labels/) · `O(n log n) / O(1)`

**Q:** Partition a string into as many parts as possible so each letter appears in at most one part.

**Example:** `s = "ababcbacadefegdehijhklij"` → `[9,7,8]`

**Algorithm:**
1. Sort by key property (end time, value/weight ratio, start, etc.).
2. Greedily pick items: take current if it doesn't violate constraint.
3. Update running state (end pointer, remaining capacity, etc.).
4. Return count or accumulated result.
`O(n log n) / O(1)`

```java
class Solution {
    public List<Integer> partitionLabels(String s) {
        if(s == null || s.length() == 0) {
            return null;
        }
        int n = s.length();
        int[] first = new int[26];
        Arrays.fill(first, -1);
        int[] last = new int[26];
        Arrays.fill(last, -1);
        for(int i = 0; i < s.length(); i++) {
            char ch = s.charAt(i);
            if(first[ch - 'a'] == -1) {
                first[ch - 'a'] = i;
            }
            last[ch - 'a'] = i;
        }
        return mergeIntervals(first, last);
    }
    public List<Integer> mergeIntervals(int[] first, int[] last) {
        List<int[]> intervals = new ArrayList<>();
        for(int i = 0; i < 26; i++) {
            if(first[i] != -1) {
                intervals.add(new int[]{first[i], last[i]});
            }
        }
        Collections.sort(intervals, (a, b) -> (a[0] - b[0]));
        List<int[]> mergedIntervals = new ArrayList<>();
        int[] current = intervals.get(0);
        for(int i = 1; i < intervals.size(); i++) {
            if(current[1] < intervals.get(i)[0]) {
                mergedIntervals.add(current);
                current = intervals.get(i);
            } else {
                current[1] = Math.max(current[1], intervals.get(i)[1]);
            }
        }
        mergedIntervals.add(current);
        List<Integer> result = new ArrayList<>();
        for(int[] interval : mergedIntervals) {
            result.add(interval[1] - interval[0] + 1);
        }
        return result;
    }
}
```

- Prove greedy by exchange argument: any swap of two choices doesn't improve result.
- If greedy fails, check if DP is needed (overlapping subproblems).

---

#### #881. Boats to Save People

[leetcode.com/problems/boats-to-save-people/](https://leetcode.com/problems/boats-to-save-people/) · `O(n log n) / O(1)`

**Q:** Find the minimum number of boats to rescue people given weight limit 2 per boat.

**Example:** `boats = [3,5,3,4], limit = 5` → `4`

**Algorithm:**
1. Sort array to enable two-pointer convergence.
2. Set left=0, right=n-1.
3. If sum/condition met: record result, move both pointers.
4. If too small: advance left. If too large: retreat right.
`O(n log n) / O(1)`

```java
class Solution {
    public int numRescueBoats(int[] people, int limit) {
        if(people == null || people.length == 0) {
            return 0;
        }
        Arrays.sort(people);
        int left = 0, right = people.length - 1;
        int count = 0;
        while(left < right) {
            if(people[right] + people[left] <= limit) {
                count++;
                right--;
                left++;
            } else {
                count++;
                right--;
            }
        }
        if(left == right) count++;
        return count;
    }
}
```

- Prove greedy by exchange argument: any swap of two choices doesn't improve result.
- If greedy fails, check if DP is needed (overlapping subproblems).

---

#### #1005. Maximize Sum Of Array After K Negations

[leetcode.com/problems/maximize-sum-of-array-after-k-negations/](https://leetcode.com/problems/maximize-sum-of-array-after-k-negations/) · `O(n log n) / O(1)`

**Algorithm:**
1. Identify greedy choice: local optimum at each step.
2. Prove exchange argument: swapping any two choices doesn't improve result.
3. Iterate and make greedy decision at each position.
4. Return accumulated result.
`O(n log n) / O(1)`

```java
class Solution {
    //  0
    //  0
    //  0
    public int largestSumAfterKNegations(int[] nums, int k) {
        Map<Integer, Integer> freq = new HashMap<>();
        for (int num : nums) {
            freq.put(num, freq.getOrDefault(num, 0) + 1);
        }
        int result = Arrays.stream(nums).sum();
        for (int i = -100; i < 0; ++i) {
            if (freq.containsKey(i)) {
                int ops = Math.min(k, freq.get(i));
                result += (-i) * ops * 2;
                freq.put(i, freq.get(i) - ops);
                freq.put(-i, freq.getOrDefault(-i, 0) + ops);
                k -= ops;
                if (k == 0) {
                    break;
                }
            }
        }
        if (k > 0 && k % 2 == 1 && !freq.containsKey(0)) {
            for (int i = 1; i <= 100; ++i) {
                if (freq.containsKey(i)) {
                    result -= i * 2;
                    break;
                }
            }
        }
        return result;
    }
}
```

- Return early on first HashMap hit rather than scanning full input.

#### #1094. Car Pooling

[leetcode.com/problems/car-pooling/](https://leetcode.com/problems/car-pooling/) · `O(n log n) / O(1)`

**Q:** Determine if a car pool trip is feasible given passenger pick-up and drop-off locations.

**Algorithm:**
1. Sort by key property (end time, value/weight ratio, start, etc.).
2. Greedily pick items: take current if it doesn't violate constraint.
3. Update running state (end pointer, remaining capacity, etc.).
4. Return count or accumulated result.
`O(n log n) / O(1)`

```java
class Solution {
    public boolean carPooling(int[][] trips, int capacity) {
        if(trips == null) {
            return true;
        }
        PriorityQueue<Integer> queue = new PriorityQueue<>();
        Arrays.sort(trips, (a, b) -> (a[1] - b[1]));
        for(int[] trip : trips) {
            while(!queue.isEmpty() && queue.peek() <= trip[1]){
                queue.poll();
            }
            for(int i = 0; i < trip[0]; i++) {
                queue.offer(trip[2]);
            }
            if(queue.size() > capacity) {
                return false;
            }
        }
        return true;
    }
}
class Solution {
    public boolean carPooling(int[][] trips, int capacity) {
        if(trips == null) {
            return true;
        }
        int[] diff = new int[1000 + 1];
        for(int[] trip : trips) {
            diff[trip[1]] += trip[0];
            diff[trip[2]] -= trip[0];
        }
        int current = diff[0];
        if(current > capacity) return false;
        for(int i = 1; i <= 1000; i++){
            current = current + diff[i];
            if(current > capacity) return false;
        }
        return true;
    }
}
class Solution {
    public boolean carPooling(int[][] trips, int capacity) {
        if(trips == null) {
            return true;
        }
        PriorityQueue<int[]> queue = new PriorityQueue<>((a, b) -> (a[2] - b[2]));
        int count = 0;
        Arrays.sort(trips, (a, b) -> (a[1] - b[1]));
        for(int[] trip : trips) {
            while(!queue.isEmpty() && queue.peek()[2] <= trip[1]){
                count -= queue.peek()[0];
                queue.poll();
            }
            queue.offer(trip);
            count += trip[0];
            if(count > capacity) {
                return false;
            }
        }
        return true;
    }
}
```

- Use 1D rolling array if dp[i] only depends on previous row — reduces space from O(mn) to O(n).
- After sorting, use binary search (O(log n)) instead of linear scan for lookups.

---

#### #1326. Minimum Number of Taps to Open to Water a Garden

[leetcode.com/problems/minimum-number-of-taps-to-open-to-water-a-garden/](https://leetcode.com/problems/minimum-number-of-taps-to-open-to-water-a-garden/) · `O(n log n) / O(1)`

**Q:** Find the minimum number of taps to open to water the entire garden.

**Algorithm:**
1. Sort by key property (end time, value/weight ratio, start, etc.).
2. Greedily pick items: take current if it doesn't violate constraint.
3. Update running state (end pointer, remaining capacity, etc.).
4. Return count or accumulated result.
`O(n log n) / O(1)`

```java
class Solution {
    public int minTaps(int n, int[] ranges) {
        //
        int[][] region = new int[n + 1][2];
        //
        for(int i = 0; i <= n; i++) {
            int[] temp = new int[2];
            temp[0] = Math.max(0, i - ranges[i]);
            temp[1] = Math.min(n, i + ranges[i]);
            region[i] = temp;
        }
        //
        Arrays.sort(region, (a, b) -> a[0] == b[0] ? a[1] - b[1] : a[0] - b[0]);
        //
        int result = 0, right = 0;
        //
        int current = 0;
        //
        while(current < n + 1) {
            //
            if(region[current][0] > right) break;
            //
            int rt = right;
            while(current < n + 1 && region[current][0] <= right) {
                rt = Math.max(rt, region[current][1]);
                current ++;
            }
            result ++;
            right = rt;
            //
            if(right == n) break;
        }
        return right == n ? result : -1;
    }
}
```

- Use 1D rolling array if dp[i] only depends on previous row — reduces space from O(mn) to O(n).
- After sorting, use binary search (O(log n)) instead of linear scan for lookups.

---

#### #1353. Maximum Number of Events That Can Be Attended

[leetcode.com/problems/maximum-number-of-events-that-can-be-attended/](https://leetcode.com/problems/maximum-number-of-events-that-can-be-attended/) · `O(n log n) / O(1)`

**Q:** Find the maximum number of events you can attend (one per day).

**Algorithm:**
1. Sort by key property (end time, value/weight ratio, start, etc.).
2. Greedily pick items: take current if it doesn't violate constraint.
3. Update running state (end pointer, remaining capacity, etc.).
4. Return count or accumulated result.
`O(n log n) / O(1)`

```java
class Solution {
    public int maxEvents(int[][] events) {
        //
        Arrays.sort(events, (a, b)->(a[0]-b[0]));
        //
        PriorityQueue<Integer> queue = new PriorityQueue<>();
        int result = 0;
        int start = events[0][0]; //
        int index = 0; //
        //
        while(index < events.length || !queue.isEmpty()){
            //
            while(index < events.length && events[index][0] == start){
                queue.offer(events[index][1]);
                index++;
            }
            //
            while(!queue.isEmpty() && queue.peek() < start){
                queue.poll();
            }
            //
            if(!queue.isEmpty()){
                queue.poll();
                result++;
            }
            //
            start++;
        }
        return result;
    }
}
```

- Use 1D rolling array if dp[i] only depends on previous row — reduces space from O(mn) to O(n).
- After sorting, use binary search (O(log n)) instead of linear scan for lookups.

---

#### #1488. Avoid Flood in The City

[leetcode.com/problems/avoid-flood-in-the-city/](https://leetcode.com/problems/avoid-flood-in-the-city/) · `O(n log n) / O(1)`

**Q:** Schedule drying days to prevent flooding given a list of rains.

**Algorithm:**
1. Identify greedy choice: local optimum at each step.
2. Prove exchange argument: swapping any two choices doesn't improve result.
3. Iterate and make greedy decision at each position.
4. Return accumulated result.
`O(n log n) / O(1)`

```java
class Solution {
    public int[] avoidFlood(int[] rains) {
        int n = rains.length;
        int[] result = new int[n];
        //-1
        Arrays.fill(result , -1);
        //
        //HashSet<Integer> set = new HashSet<>();
        HashMap<Integer , Integer> map = new HashMap<>();
        TreeSet<Integer> tree = new TreeSet<>();
        for(int i = 0 ; i < n ; i++){
            // 1
            if(rains[i] == 0){
                tree.add(i);
                result[i] = 1;
                continue;
            }
            //
            if(!map.containsKey(rains[i])){
                //
                map.put(rains[i] , i);
                //
                //set.add(rains[i]);
                continue;
            }
            //
            Integer t = tree.higher(map.get(rains[i]));
            //
            if(t == null) return new int[0];
            //
            map.put(rains[i] , i);
            result[t] = rains[i];
            tree.remove(t);
        }
        return result;
    }
}
class Solution {
    public int[] avoidFlood(int[] rains) {
        int len = rains.length;
        int[] result = new int[len];
        // a lake is filled on which day
        Map<Integer, Integer> full = new HashMap<>();
        // availble days to dry the lake
        TreeSet<Integer> set = new TreeSet<>();
        for (int i = 0; i < len; i++) {
            int current = rains[i];
            if (current > 0) {
                result[i] = -1;
                if (full.containsKey(current)) {
                    int fillDay = full.get(current);
                    Integer emp = set.ceiling(fillDay);
                    if (emp == null) {
                        return new int[] {};
                    } else {
                        result[emp] = current;
                        set.remove(emp);
                        full.put(current, i);//
                    }
                } else {
                    full.put(current, i);
                }
            } else {
                result[i] = 1;
                set.add(i);
            }
        }
        return result;
    }
}
10.01. Sorted Merge LCCI
class Solution {
    public void merge(int[] A, int m, int[] B, int n) {
        int i = m - 1, j = n - 1, k = A.length - 1;
        while(k >= 0) {
            if(j < 0 || i >= 0 && A[i] > B[j]) {
                A[k--] = A[i--];
            } else {
                A[k--] = B[j--];
            }
        }
    }
}
```

- Return early on first HashMap hit rather than scanning full input.

---

### Design (25 problems)

#### #146. LRU Cache

[leetcode.com/problems/lru-cache/](https://leetcode.com/problems/lru-cache/) · `O(1) per op / O(n)`

**Q:** Design an LRU (Least Recently Used) Cache with O(1) get and put operations.

**Algorithm:**
1. HashMap for O(1) key lookup.
2. Doubly linked list: most recent at head, LRU at tail.
3. get: move node to head. put: add at head; if over capacity, remove tail node.
`O(1) per op / O(n)`

```java
class LRUCache {
    class Node {
        int key;
        int val;
        Node previous;
        Node next;
        public Node(int key, int val) {
            this.key = key;
            this.val = val;
        }
    }
    Map<Integer, Node> map;
    Node head;
    Node tail;
    int capacity;
    public LRUCache(int capacity) {
        map = new HashMap<>();
        head = new Node(-1, -1);
        tail = new Node(-1, -1);
        head.next = tail;
        tail.previous = head;
        this.capacity = capacity;
    }
    public int get(int key) {
        if(map.containsKey(key)) {
            Node node = map.get(key);
            removeNode(node);
            addToHead(node);
            return node.val;
        } else {
            return -1;
        }
    }
    public void put(int key, int value) {
        if(map.containsKey(key)) {
            Node node = map.get(key);
            node.val = value;
            removeNode(node);
            addToHead(node);
        } else {
            Node node = new Node(key, value);
            map.put(key, node);
            addToHead(node);
            if(map.size() > capacity) {
                removeNode(tail.previous);
                map.remove(tail.previous.key);
            }
        }
    }
    public void removeNode(Node node) {
        node.previous.next = node.next;
        node.next.previous = node.previous;
    }
    public void addToHead(Node node) {
        node.next = head.next;
        head.next = node;
        node.previous = head;
        node.next.previous = node;
    }
}
```

- Any solution using only HashMap (without DLL) cannot achieve O(1) for all ops simultaneously.

---

#### #460. LFU Cache

[leetcode.com/problems/lfu-cache/](https://leetcode.com/problems/lfu-cache/) · `O(1) / O(capacity)`

**Q:** Design an LFU (Least Frequently Used) Cache with O(1) get and put.

**Example:** `put(1,1), put(2,2), get(1), put(3,3), get(2)` → `1,-1 (LFU ops)`

**Algorithm:**
1. HashMap<key, node> for O(1) access.
2. HashMap<freq, DoublyLinkedList> for O(1) freq-bucket operations.
3. minFreq tracks current minimum frequency.
4. On get/put: move node to freq+1 bucket; update minFreq.
`O(1) / O(capacity)`

```java
class LFUCache {
    int minfreq, capacity;
    Map<Integer, Node> key_table;
    Map<Integer, LinkedList<Node>> freq_table;
    public LFUCache(int capacity) {
        this.minfreq = 0;
        this.capacity = capacity;
        key_table = new HashMap<>();;
        freq_table = new HashMap<>();
    }
    public int get(int key) {
        if (capacity == 0) {
            return -1;
        }
        if (!key_table.containsKey(key)) {
            return -1;
        }
        Node node = key_table.get(key);
        int val = node.val, freq = node.freq;
        freq_table.get(freq).remove(node);
        // minFreq
        if (freq_table.get(freq).size() == 0) {
            freq_table.remove(freq);
            if (minfreq == freq) {
                minfreq += 1;
            }
        }
        //  freq + 1
        freq_table.computeIfAbsent(freq + 1, list -> new LinkedList<>()).offerFirst(new Node(key, val, freq + 1));
        key_table.put(key, freq_table.get(freq + 1).peekFirst());
        return val;
    }
    public void put(int key, int value) {
        if (capacity == 0) {
            return;
        }
        if (!key_table.containsKey(key)) {
            //
            if (key_table.size() == capacity) {
                //  minFreq  freq_table[minFreq]
                Node node = freq_table.get(minfreq).peekLast();
                key_table.remove(node.key);
                freq_table.get(minfreq).pollLast();
                if (freq_table.get(minfreq).size() == 0) {
                    freq_table.remove(minfreq);
                }
            }
            freq_table.computeIfAbsent(1, list -> new LinkedList<>()).offerFirst(new Node(key, value, 1));
            key_table.put(key, freq_table.get(1).peekFirst());
            minfreq = 1;
        } else {
            //  get
            Node node = key_table.get(key);
            int freq = node.freq;
            freq_table.get(freq).remove(node);
            if (freq_table.get(freq).size() == 0) {
                freq_table.remove(freq);
                if (minfreq == freq) {
                    minfreq += 1;
                }
            }
            freq_table.computeIfAbsent(freq + 1, list -> new LinkedList<>()).offerFirst(new Node(key, value, freq + 1));
            key_table.put(key, freq_table.get(freq + 1).peekFirst());
        }
    }
}
class Node {
    int key, val, freq;
    Node(int key, int val, int freq) {
        this.key = key;
        this.val = val;
        this.freq = freq;
    }
}
```

- LFU is significantly more complex than LRU. Key: minFreq must be updated on every put.

#### #170. Two Sum III - Data structure design

[leetcode.com/problems/two-sum-iii-data-structure-design/](https://leetcode.com/problems/two-sum-iii-data-structure-design/) · `O(1) per op / O(n)`

**Q:** Design a Two Sum data structure with add() and find() operations.

**Algorithm:**
1. Choose data structures based on required operation complexities.
2. HashMap provides O(1) lookup; doubly linked list provides O(1) insert/delete.
3. On get: retrieve from map, update ordering structure.
4. On put: evict if at capacity, insert new entry, update map.
`O(1) per op / O(n)`

```java
class TwoSum {
    List<Integer> list;
    public TwoSum() {
        list = new ArrayList<>();
    }
    public void add(int number) {
        list.add(number);
    }
    public boolean find(int value) {
        Set<Integer> set = new HashSet<>();
        for(int num : list) {
            if(set.contains(value - num)) {
                return true;
            }
            set.add(num);
        }
        return false;
    }
}
class TwoSum {
    Map<Integer, Integer> map;
    public TwoSum() {
        map = new HashMap<>();
    }
    public void add(int number) {
        map.put(number, map.getOrDefault(number, 0) + 1);
    }
    public boolean find(int value) {
        for (Map.Entry<Integer, Integer> entry : map.entrySet()){
            int num1 = entry.getKey();
            int num2 = value - num1;
            if (num2 == num1 && map.get(num1) > 1){
                return true;
            } else if(num2 != num1 && map.containsKey(num2)) {
                return true;
            }
        }
        return false;
    }
}
```

- Return early on first HashMap hit rather than scanning full input.

#### #173. Binary Search Tree Iterator

[leetcode.com/problems/binary-search-tree-iterator/](https://leetcode.com/problems/binary-search-tree-iterator/) · `O(1) per op / O(n)`

**Q:** Implement a Binary Search Tree iterator with hasNext() and next() using O(h) memory.

**Algorithm:**
1. Choose data structures based on required operation complexities.
2. HashMap provides O(1) lookup; doubly linked list provides O(1) insert/delete.
3. On get: retrieve from map, update ordering structure.
4. On put: evict if at capacity, insert new entry, update map.
`O(1) per op / O(n)`

```java
class BSTIterator {
    TreeNode current;
    Deque<TreeNode> stack;
    public BSTIterator(TreeNode root) {
        current = root;
        stack = new ArrayDeque<>();
    }
    public int next() {
        while(current != null) {
            stack.push(current);
            current = current.left;
        }
        current = stack.pop();
        int result = current.val;
        current = current.right;
        return result;
    }
    public boolean hasNext() {
        return current != null || !stack.isEmpty();
    }
}
```

- Use sentinel head/tail nodes in doubly linked list to eliminate null checks.
- Lazy deletion: mark nodes deleted in HashMap instead of actually removing from list.

#### #244. Shortest Word Distance II

[leetcode.com/problems/shortest-word-distance-ii/](https://leetcode.com/problems/shortest-word-distance-ii/) · `O(1) per op / O(n)`

**Q:** Design data structure for finding shortest word distance between two words in a list, supporting repeated queries.

**Algorithm:**
1. Choose data structures based on required operation complexities.
2. HashMap provides O(1) lookup; doubly linked list provides O(1) insert/delete.
3. On get: retrieve from map, update ordering structure.
4. On put: evict if at capacity, insert new entry, update map.
`O(1) per op / O(n)`

```java
class WordDistance {
    Map<String, List<Integer>> map;
    public WordDistance(String[] wordsDict) {
        int n = wordsDict.length;
        map = new HashMap<>(n);
        for(int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
            String word = wordsDict[i];
            map.computeIfAbsent(word, list -> new ArrayList<>()).add(i);
        }
    }
    public int shortest(String word1, String word2) {
        List<Integer> list1 = map.get(word1);
        List<Integer> list2 = map.get(word2);
        int i = 0, j = 0;
        int min = Integer.MAX_VALUE;
        while(i < list1.size() && j < list2.size()) {
            min = Math.min(min, Math.abs(list1.get(i) - list2.get(j)));
            if(list1.get(i) < list2.get(j)) {
                i++;
            } else {
                j++;
            }
        }
        return min;
    }
}
```

- Use sentinel head/tail nodes in doubly linked list to eliminate null checks.
- Lazy deletion: mark nodes deleted in HashMap instead of actually removing from list.

---

#### #245. Shortest Word Distance III

[leetcode.com/problems/shortest-word-distance-iii/](https://leetcode.com/problems/shortest-word-distance-iii/) · `O(1) per op / O(n)`

**Algorithm:**
1. Choose data structures based on required operation complexities.
2. HashMap provides O(1) lookup; doubly linked list provides O(1) insert/delete.
3. On get: retrieve from map, update ordering structure.
4. On put: evict if at capacity, insert new entry, update map.
`O(1) per op / O(n)`

```java
class Solution {
    public int shortestWordDistance(String[] wordsDict, String word1, String word2) {
        int p1 = -1, p2 = -1;
        if(word1.equals(word2)) {
            return shortestWordDistance(wordsDict, word1);
        }
        int shortestDistance = Integer.MAX_VALUE;
        for(int i = 0; i < wordsDict.length; i++) {
            if(word1.equals(wordsDict[i])) {
                p1 = i;
            }
            if(word2.equals(wordsDict[i])) {
                p2 = i;
            }
            if(p1 != -1 && p2 != -1) {
                shortestDistance = Math.min(shortestDistance, Math.abs(p1 - p2));
            }
            // System.out.println(p1 + " " + p2);
        }
        return shortestDistance;
    }
    public int shortestWordDistance(String[] wordsDict, String word) {
        int pre = -1;
        int current = -1;
        int shortestDistance = Integer.MAX_VALUE;
        for(int i = 0; i < wordsDict.length; i++) {
            if(wordsDict[i].equals(word)) {
                if(pre == -1) {
                    pre = i;
                    current = i;
                } else {
                    pre = current;
                    current = i;
                    shortestDistance = Math.min(shortestDistance, current - pre);
                }
            }
            // System.out.println(pre + " " + current);
        }
        return shortestDistance;
    }
}
```

- Use sentinel head/tail nodes in doubly linked list to eliminate null checks.
- Lazy deletion: mark nodes deleted in HashMap instead of actually removing from list.

#### #251. Flatten 2D Vector

[leetcode.com/problems/flatten-2d-vector/](https://leetcode.com/problems/flatten-2d-vector/) · `O(1) per op / O(n)`

**Algorithm:**
1. Choose data structures based on required operation complexities.
2. HashMap provides O(1) lookup; doubly linked list provides O(1) insert/delete.
3. On get: retrieve from map, update ordering structure.
4. On put: evict if at capacity, insert new entry, update map.
`O(1) per op / O(n)`

```java
import java.util.NoSuchElementException;
class Vector2D {
    private int[][] vector;
    private int inner = 0;
    private int outer = 0;
    public Vector2D(int[][] v) {
        // We need to store a *reference* to the input vector.
        vector = v;
    }
    // If the current outer and inner point to an integer, this method does nothing.
    // Otherwise, inner and outer are advanced until they point to an integer.
    // If there are no more integers, then outer will be equal to vector.length
    // when this method terminates.
    private void advanceToNext() {
        // While outer is still within the vector, but inner is over the
        // end of the inner list pointed to by outer, we want to move
        // forward to the start of the next inner vector.
        while (outer < vector.length && inner == vector[outer].length) {
            inner = 0;
            outer++;
        }
    }
    public int next() {
        // As per Java specs, throw an exception if there's no next.
        // This will also ensure the pointers point to an integer otherwise.
        if (!hasNext()) throw new NoSuchElementException();
        // Return current element and move inner so that is after the current
        // element.
        return vector[outer][inner++];
    }
    public boolean hasNext() {
        // Ensure the position pointers are moved such they point to an integer,
        // or put outer = vector.length.
        advanceToNext();
        // If outer = vector.length then there are no integers left, otherwise
        // we've stopped at an integer and so there's an integer left.
        return outer < vector.length;
    }
}
```

- Use 1D rolling array if dp[i] only depends on previous row — reduces space from O(mn) to O(n).

#### #281. Zigzag Iterator

[leetcode.com/problems/zigzag-iterator/](https://leetcode.com/problems/zigzag-iterator/) · `O(1) per op / O(n)`

**Algorithm:**
1. Choose data structures based on required operation complexities.
2. HashMap provides O(1) lookup; doubly linked list provides O(1) insert/delete.
3. On get: retrieve from map, update ordering structure.
4. On put: evict if at capacity, insert new entry, update map.
`O(1) per op / O(n)`

```java
public class ZigzagIterator {
    ArrayList<Integer>[] listArr;
    int i;
    int j;
    int size;
    public ZigzagIterator(List<Integer> v1, List<Integer> v2) {
        listArr = new ArrayList[2];
        listArr[0] = new ArrayList<>(v1);
        listArr[1] = new ArrayList<>(v2);
        size = Math.max(v1.size(), v2.size());
        i = v1.size() > 0 ? 0 : 1;
        j = 0;
    }
    public int next() {
        int result = listArr[i].get(j);
        moveToNext();
        return result;
    }
    public boolean hasNext() {
        return i < listArr.length && j < size;
    }
    private void moveToNext() {
        movePoint();
        if (j >= listArr[i].size()) {
            movePoint();
        }
    }
    private void movePoint() {
        i += 1;
        if (i == listArr.length) {
            i = 0;
            j += 1;
        }
    }
}
```

- Use sentinel head/tail nodes in doubly linked list to eliminate null checks.
- Lazy deletion: mark nodes deleted in HashMap instead of actually removing from list.

#### #284. 窥探迭代器

[leetcode.com/problems//](https://leetcode.com/problems//) · `O(1) per op / O(n)`

**Algorithm:**
1. Choose data structures based on required operation complexities.
2. HashMap provides O(1) lookup; doubly linked list provides O(1) insert/delete.
3. On get: retrieve from map, update ordering structure.
4. On put: evict if at capacity, insert new entry, update map.
`O(1) per op / O(n)`

```java
class PeekingIterator implements Iterator<Integer> {
    private Iterator<Integer> iterator;
    private Integer nextElement;
    public PeekingIterator(Iterator<Integer> iterator) {
        this.iterator = iterator;
        nextElement = iterator.next();
    }
    public Integer peek() {
        return nextElement;
    }
    @Override
    public Integer next() {
        Integer ret = nextElement;
        nextElement = iterator.hasNext() ? iterator.next() : null;
        return ret;
    }
    @Override
    public boolean hasNext() {
        return nextElement != null;
    }
}
```

- Use sentinel head/tail nodes in doubly linked list to eliminate null checks.
- Lazy deletion: mark nodes deleted in HashMap instead of actually removing from list.

#### #341. Flatten Nested List Iterator

[leetcode.com/problems/flatten-nested-list-iterator/](https://leetcode.com/problems/flatten-nested-list-iterator/) · `O(1) per op / O(n)`

**Q:** Implement an iterator that flattens a nested list of integers.

**Algorithm:**
1. Choose data structures based on required operation complexities.
2. HashMap provides O(1) lookup; doubly linked list provides O(1) insert/delete.
3. On get: retrieve from map, update ordering structure.
4. On put: evict if at capacity, insert new entry, update map.
`O(1) per op / O(n)`

```java
public class NestedIterator implements Iterator<Integer> {
    Deque<NestedInteger> stack = new ArrayDeque<>();
    public NestedIterator(List<NestedInteger> list) {
        for (int i = list.size() - 1; i >= 0; i--) {
            NestedInteger item = list.get(i);
            stack.push(item);
        }
    }
    @Override
    public Integer next() {
        return hasNext() ? stack.pop().getInteger() : -1;
    }
    @Override
    public boolean hasNext() {
        if (stack.isEmpty()) {
            return false;
        } else {
            NestedInteger item = stack.peek();
            if (item.isInteger()) {
                return true;
            } else {
                item = stack.pop();
                List<NestedInteger> list = item.getList();
                for (int i = list.size() - 1; i >= 0; i--) {
                    stack.push(list.get(i));
                }
                return hasNext();
            }
        }
    }
}
```

- Use sentinel head/tail nodes in doubly linked list to eliminate null checks.
- Lazy deletion: mark nodes deleted in HashMap instead of actually removing from list.

#### #346. Moving Average from Data Stream

[leetcode.com/problems/moving-average-from-data-stream/](https://leetcode.com/problems/moving-average-from-data-stream/) · `O(1) per op / O(n)`

**Q:** Compute the moving average from a stream of integers given a window size.

**Algorithm:**
1. Choose data structures based on required operation complexities.
2. HashMap provides O(1) lookup; doubly linked list provides O(1) insert/delete.
3. On get: retrieve from map, update ordering structure.
4. On put: evict if at capacity, insert new entry, update map.
`O(1) per op / O(n)`

```java
Sliding window median: two heap
Sliding window maximum/minimum:
Sliding window average:
class MovingAverage {
    int size, windowSum = 0, count = 0;
    Deque<Integer> queue;
    public MovingAverage(int _size) {
        queue = new ArrayDeque<>();
        size = _size;
    }
    public double next(int val) {
        count++;
        queue.add(val);
        int tail = count > size ? (int) queue.poll() : 0;
        windowSum = windowSum - tail + val;
        return windowSum * 1.0 / (Math.min(count, size));
    }
}
```

- Use sentinel head/tail nodes in doubly linked list to eliminate null checks.
- Lazy deletion: mark nodes deleted in HashMap instead of actually removing from list.
