#### #59. Spiral Matrix II

[leetcode.com/problems/spiral-matrix-ii/](https://leetcode.com/problems/spiral-matrix-ii/) · `O(log n) / O(1)`

**Algorithm:**
1. Identify mathematical pattern: GCD, modular arithmetic, or number theory.
2. Apply relevant formula or recurrence.
3. Handle overflow using long or modular arithmetic.
4. Return computed value.
`O(log n) / O(1)`

```java
class Solution {
    public int[][] generateMatrix(int n) {
        int startX = 0; int endX = n - 1;
        int startY = 0; int endY = n - 1;
        int[][] result = new int[n][n];
        int value = 1;
        while(startX <= endX && startY <= endY) {
            // top;
            for(int j = startY; j <= endY; j++) {
                result[startX][j] = value++;
            }
            if(++startX > endX) break;
            // right;
            for(int i = startX; i <= endX; i++) {
                result[i][endY] = value++;
            }
            if(--endY < startY) break;
            // bottom;
            for(int j = endY; j >= startY; j--) {
                result[endX][j] = value++;
            }
            if(--endX < startX) break;
            // left;
            for(int i = endX; i >= startX; i--) {
                result[i][startY] = value++;
            }
            if(++startY > endY) break;
        }
        return result;
    }
}
class Solution {
    public int[][] generateMatrix(int n) {
        int[][] result = new int[n][n];
        int x = 0, y = 0;
        int value = 1;
        result[0][0] = 1;
        while(value < n * n) {
            while(y + 1 < n && result[x][y+1] == 0) {
                result[x][++y] = ++value;
            }
            while(x + 1 < n && result[x + 1][y] == 0) {
                result[++x][y] = ++value;
            }
            while(y - 1 >= 0 && result[x][y - 1] == 0) {
                result[x][--y] = ++value;
            }
            while(x - 1 >= 0 && result[x - 1][y] == 0) {
                result[--x][y] = ++value;
            }
            System.out.println(value);
        }
        return result;
    }
}
```

- Use 1D rolling array if dp[i] only depends on previous row — reduces space from O(mn) to O(n).

#### #66. Plus One

[leetcode.com/problems/plus-one/](https://leetcode.com/problems/plus-one/) · `O(log n) / O(1)`

**Q:** Increment a large integer represented as an array of digits.

**Example:** `digits = [1,2,3]` → `[1,2,4]`

**Algorithm:**
1. Identify mathematical pattern: GCD, modular arithmetic, or number theory.
2. Apply relevant formula or recurrence.
3. Handle overflow using long or modular arithmetic.
4. Return computed value.
`O(log n) / O(1)`

```java
class Solution {
    public int[] plusOne(int[] digits) {
        int n = digits.length;
        int carry = 1;
        for(int i = n - 1; i >= 0; i--) {
            digits[i] += carry;
            carry = digits[i] / 10;
            digits[i] = digits[i] % 10;
        }
        if(carry == 1) {
            int[] result = new int[n+1];
            result[0] = 1;
            return result;
        }
        return digits;
    }
}
```

- Use long arithmetic and take modulo after each multiplication to prevent overflow.
- Cache GCD computations when called repeatedly on overlapping pairs.

#### #118. Pascal's Triangle

[leetcode.com/problems/pascals-triangle/](https://leetcode.com/problems/pascals-triangle/) · `O(log n) / O(1)`

**Q:** Generate Pascal's triangle up to numRows rows.

**Example:** `numRows = 5` → `[[1],[1,1],[1,2,1],[1,3,3,1],[1,4,6,4,1]]`

**Algorithm:**
1. Identify mathematical pattern: GCD, modular arithmetic, or number theory.
2. Apply relevant formula or recurrence.
3. Handle overflow using long or modular arithmetic.
4. Return computed value.
`O(log n) / O(1)`

```java
class Solution {
    public List<List<Integer>> generate(int numRows) {
        List<List<Integer>> ret = new ArrayList<>();
        for (int i = 0; i < numRows; ++i) {
            List<Integer> row = new ArrayList<>();
            for (int j = 0; j <= i; ++j) {
                if (j == 0 || j == i) {
                    row.add(1);
                } else {
                    row.add(ret.get(i - 1).get(j - 1) + ret.get(i - 1).get(j));
                }
            }
            ret.add(row);
        }
        return ret;
    }
}
```

- Use long arithmetic and take modulo after each multiplication to prevent overflow.
- Cache GCD computations when called repeatedly on overlapping pairs.

#### #119. Pascal's Triangle II

[leetcode.com/problems/pascals-triangle-ii/](https://leetcode.com/problems/pascals-triangle-ii/) · `O(log n) / O(1)`

**Q:** Return the kth row of Pascal's triangle using O(k) space.

**Example:** `rowIndex = 3` → `[1,3,3,1]`

**Algorithm:**
1. Identify mathematical pattern: GCD, modular arithmetic, or number theory.
2. Apply relevant formula or recurrence.
3. Handle overflow using long or modular arithmetic.
4. Return computed value.
`O(log n) / O(1)`

```java
class Solution {
    public List<Integer> getRow(int rowIndex) {
        List<List<Integer>> ret = new ArrayList<>();
        for (int i = 0; i <= rowIndex; ++i) {
            List<Integer> row = new ArrayList<>();
            for (int j = 0; j <= i; ++j) {
                if (j == 0 || j == i) {
                    row.add(1);
                } else {
                    row.add(ret.get(i - 1).get(j - 1) + ret.get(i - 1).get(j));
                }
            }
            ret.add(row);
        }
        return ret.get(ret.size() - 1);
    }
}
```

- Use long arithmetic and take modulo after each multiplication to prevent overflow.
- Cache GCD computations when called repeatedly on overlapping pairs.

#### #149. Max Points on a Line

[leetcode.com/problems/max-points-on-a-line/](https://leetcode.com/problems/max-points-on-a-line/) · `O(log n) / O(1)`

**Q:** Find the maximum number of points that lie on the same straight line.

**Algorithm:**
1. Identify mathematical pattern: GCD, modular arithmetic, or number theory.
2. Apply relevant formula or recurrence.
3. Handle overflow using long or modular arithmetic.
4. Return computed value.
`O(log n) / O(1)`

```java
class Solution {
    public int maxPoints(int[][] points) {
        int n = points.length;
        // Set<String> lines = new HashSet<>();
        Map<String, Set<String>> map = new HashMap<>();
        int max = 1;
        for(int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
            for(int j = i + 1; j < n; j++) {
                int[] point1 = points[i];
                int[] point2 = points[j];
                double a = ((double) (point1[1] - point2[1])) / (point1[0] - point2[0]);
                double b = (double) (point1[1] + point2[1] - a * (point1[0] + point2[0])) / 2;
                String s = hash(a,b);
                map.computeIfAbsent(s, set -> new HashSet<>());
                map.get(s).add(hash(point1));
                map.get(s).add(hash(point2));
                max = Math.max(max, map.get(s).size());
            }
        }
        return max;
    }
    private String hash(double a, double b) {
        return a + "#" + b;
    }
    private String hash(int[] point) {
        return point[0] + "#" + point[1];
    }
}
class Solution {
    public int maxPoints(int[][] points) {
        int n = points.length;
        if (n <= 2) {
            return n;
        }
        int ret = 0;
        for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
            if (ret >= n - i || ret > n / 2) {
                break;
            }
            Map<Integer, Integer> map = new HashMap<>();
            for (int j = i + 1; j < n; j++) {
                int x = points[i][0] - points[j][0];
                int y = points[i][1] - points[j][1];
                if (x == 0) {
                    y = 1;
                } else if (y == 0) {
                    x = 1;
                } else {
                    if (y < 0) {
                        x = -x;
                        y = -y;
                    }
                    int gcdXY = gcd(Math.abs(x), Math.abs(y));
                    x /= gcdXY;
                    y /= gcdXY;
                }
                int key = y + x * 20001;
                map.put(key, map.getOrDefault(key, 0) + 1);
            }
            int maxn = 0;
            for (Map.Entry<Integer, Integer> entry: map.entrySet()) {
                int num = entry.getValue();
                maxn = Math.max(maxn, num + 1);
            }
            ret = Math.max(ret, maxn);
        }
        return ret;
    }
    public int gcd(int a, int b) {
        return b != 0 ? gcd(b, a % b) : a;
    }
}
```

- Use 1D rolling array if dp[i] only depends on previous row — reduces space from O(mn) to O(n).
- Return early on first HashMap hit rather than scanning full input.

2. Apply relevant formula or recurrence.
3. Handle overflow using long or modular arithmetic.
4. Return computed value.
`O(log n) / O(1)`

```java
class Solution {
    public int trailingZeroes(int n) {
        //  5
        // 2   5
        int count = 0;
        while (n > 0) {
            count += n / 5;
            n = n / 5;
        }
        return count;
    }
}
```

#### #202. Happy Number

[leetcode.com/problems/happy-number/](https://leetcode.com/problems/happy-number/) · `O(log n) / O(1)`

**Q:** Determine if a number eventually reaches 1 by repeatedly replacing it with the sum of squares of its digits.

**Example:** `n = 19` → `true`

**Algorithm:**
1. Identify mathematical pattern: GCD, modular arithmetic, or number theory.
2. Apply relevant formula or recurrence.
3. Handle overflow using long or modular arithmetic.
4. Return computed value.
`O(log n) / O(1)`

```java
class Solution {
    public int getNext(int n) {
        int totalSum = 0;
        while (n > 0) {
            int d = n % 10;
            n = n / 10;
            totalSum += d * d;
        }
        return totalSum;
    }
    public boolean isHappy(int n) {
        int slow = n;
        int fast = getNext(n);
        while (slow != fast) {
            slow = getNext(slow);
            fast = getNext(getNext(fast));
        }
        return slow == 1;
    }
}
```

- Use long arithmetic and take modulo after each multiplication to prevent overflow.
- Cache GCD computations when called repeatedly on overlapping pairs.

#### #204. Count Primes

[leetcode.com/problems/count-primes/](https://leetcode.com/problems/count-primes/) · `O(log n) / O(1)`

**Q:** Count prime numbers less than a non-negative integer n.

**Example:** `n = 10` → `4`

**Algorithm:**
1. Sieve of Eratosthenes: boolean array, mark multiples of each prime.
2. Start from 2; skip already-composite numbers.
3. Count or collect remaining primes.
`O(log n) / O(1)`

```java
class Solution {
    public int countPrimes(int n) {
        int[] isPrime = new int[n];
        Arrays.fill(isPrime, 1);
        int result = 0;
        for (int i = 2; i < n; ++i) {
            if (isPrime[i] == 1) {
                result += 1;
                if ((long) i * i < n) {
                    for (int j = i * i; j < n; j += i) {
                        isPrime[j] = 0;
                    }
                }
            }
        }
        return result;
    }
}
```

- Use long arithmetic and take modulo after each multiplication to prevent overflow.
- Cache GCD computations when called repeatedly on overlapping pairs.

#### #223. Rectangle Area

[leetcode.com/problems/rectangle-area/](https://leetcode.com/problems/rectangle-area/) · `O(log n) / O(1)`

**Algorithm:**
1. Identify mathematical pattern: GCD, modular arithmetic, or number theory.
2. Apply relevant formula or recurrence.
3. Handle overflow using long or modular arithmetic.
4. Return computed value.
`O(log n) / O(1)`

```java
class Solution {
    public int computeArea(int ax1, int ay1, int ax2, int ay2, int bx1, int by1, int bx2, int by2) {
        int area1 = (ax2 - ax1) * (ay2 - ay1), area2 = (bx2 - bx1) * (by2 - by1);
        int overlapWidth = Math.min(ax2, bx2) - Math.max(ax1, bx1);
        int overlapHeight = Math.min(ay2, by2) - Math.max(ay1, by1);
        int overlapArea = Math.max(overlapWidth, 0) * Math.max(overlapHeight, 0);
        return area1 + area2 - overlapArea;
    }
}
```

- Use long arithmetic and take modulo after each multiplication to prevent overflow.
- Cache GCD computations when called repeatedly on overlapping pairs.

2. Square base and halve exponent each iteration.
3. Apply modulo at each step if needed.

```java
        return n == 1;
    }

}
```

#### #233. Number of Digit One

[leetcode.com/problems/number-of-digit-one/](https://leetcode.com/problems/number-of-digit-one/) · `O(log n) / O(1)`

**Q:** Count total occurrences of digit 1 in all integers from 1 to n.

**Example:** `n = 13` → `6`

**Algorithm:**
1. Identify mathematical pattern: GCD, modular arithmetic, or number theory.
2. Apply relevant formula or recurrence.
3. Handle overflow using long or modular arithmetic.
4. Return computed value.
`O(log n) / O(1)`

```java
class Solution {
    //  1
    public int countDigitOne(int n) {
        // mulk  10^k
        //  k  10^k
        //  k
        long mulk = 1;
        int result = 0;
        for (int k = 0; n >= mulk; ++k) {
            result += (n / (mulk * 10)) * mulk + Math.min(Math.max(n % (mulk * 10) - mulk + 1, 0), mulk);
            mulk *= 10;
        }
        return result;
    }
}
```

- Use long arithmetic and take modulo after each multiplication to prevent overflow.
- Cache GCD computations when called repeatedly on overlapping pairs.

#### #243. Shortest Word Distance

[leetcode.com/problems/shortest-word-distance/](https://leetcode.com/problems/shortest-word-distance/) · `O(log n) / O(1)`

**Algorithm:**
1. Identify mathematical pattern: GCD, modular arithmetic, or number theory.
2. Apply relevant formula or recurrence.
3. Handle overflow using long or modular arithmetic.
4. Return computed value.
`O(log n) / O(1)`

```java
class Solution {
    public int shortestDistance(String[] wordsDict, String word1, String word2) {
        int ptr1 = -1;
        int ptr2 = -1;
        int min = wordsDict.length;
        for(int i = 0; i < wordsDict.length; i++) {
            String current = wordsDict[i];
            if(current.equals(word1)) {
                if(ptr2 != -1) {
                    min = Math.min(min, Math.abs(ptr2 - i));
                }
                ptr1 = i;
            }
            if(current.equals(word2)) {
                if(ptr1 != -1) {
                    min = Math.min(min, Math.abs(ptr1 - i));
                }
                ptr2 = i;
            }
        }
        return min;
    }
}
```

- Use long arithmetic and take modulo after each multiplication to prevent overflow.
- Cache GCD computations when called repeatedly on overlapping pairs.

1. Identify mathematical pattern: GCD, modular arithmetic, or number theory.
2. Apply relevant formula or recurrence.
3. Handle overflow using long or modular arithmetic.
4. Return computed value.
`O(log n) / O(1)`

```java
class Solution {
    public int addDigits(int num) {
        //  9  1 - 9
        // n  0  0
        // n  9  n  9  n mod 9
        // n  9  9
        return (num - 1) % 9 + 1;
    }
}
```

- Use long arithmetic and take modulo after each multiplication to prevent overflow.
- Cache GCD computations when called repeatedly on overlapping pairs.

#### #263. Ugly Number

[leetcode.com/problems/ugly-number/](https://leetcode.com/problems/ugly-number/) · `O(log n) / O(1)`

**Q:** Check if a number is an ugly number (prime factors only 2, 3, 5).

**Example:** `n = 6` → `true`

**Algorithm:**
1. Identify mathematical pattern: GCD, modular arithmetic, or number theory.
2. Apply relevant formula or recurrence.
3. Handle overflow using long or modular arithmetic.
4. Return computed value.
`O(log n) / O(1)`

```java
class Solution {
    public boolean isUgly(int n) {
        if(n <= 0) {
            return false;
        }
        while(n != 1) {
            if(n % 2 != 0 && n % 3 != 0 && n % 5 != 0) {
                return false;
            } else if(n % 2 == 0) {
                n /= 2;
            } else if(n % 3 == 0) {
                n /= 3;
            } else {
                n /= 5;
            }
        }
        return true;
    }
}
class Solution {
    public boolean isUgly(int n) {
        if (n <= 0) {
            return false;
        }
        int[] factors = {2, 3, 5};
        for (int factor : factors) {
            while (n % factor == 0) {
                n /= factor;
            }
        }
        return n == 1;
    }
}
```

- Use long arithmetic and take modulo after each multiplication to prevent overflow.
- Cache GCD computations when called repeatedly on overlapping pairs.

#### #1201. Ugly Number III

[leetcode.com/problems/ugly-number-iii/](https://leetcode.com/problems/ugly-number-iii/) · `O(log n) / O(1)`

**Algorithm:**
1. Identify mathematical pattern: GCD, modular arithmetic, or number theory.
2. Apply relevant formula or recurrence.
3. Handle overflow using long or modular arithmetic.
4. Return computed value.
`O(log n) / O(1)`

```java
class Solution {
    public int nthUglyNumber(int n, int a, int b, int c) {
        PriorityQueue<int[]> pq = new PriorityQueue<>((a1,b1)->a1[0]-b1[0]);
        pq.add(new int[]{a,a});
        if(b != a) pq.add(new int[]{b,b});
        if(c != a && c != b) pq.add(new int[]{c,c});
        if(pq.size() == 1) return n*pq.poll()[1];
        int flag = 0;
        while(n>0){
            int[] i = pq.poll();
            if(i[0] > flag) n--;
            int j = pq.peek()[0];
            int m = (j-i[0])/i[1];
            if(n <= m) return n*i[1]+i[0];
            flag = i[0]+ m*i[1];
            i[0] += (m+1)*i[1];
            n -= m;
            pq.add(i);
        }
        return flag;
    }
}
```

- Use long arithmetic and take modulo after each multiplication to prevent overflow.
- Cache GCD computations when called repeatedly on overlapping pairs.

#### #292. Nim Game

[leetcode.com/problems/nim-game/](https://leetcode.com/problems/nim-game/) · `O(log n) / O(1)`

**Q:** In Nim game, you and opponent alternate removing 1–3 stones; player who takes last stone wins. Can you win?

**Example:** `n = 4` → `false`

**Algorithm:**

3. Handle overflow using long or modular arithmetic.
4. Return computed value.
`O(log n) / O(1)`

```java
class Solution {
    public boolean canWinNim(int n) {
        return n % 4 != 0;
    }
}
```

#### #357. Count Numbers with Unique Digits

[leetcode.com/problems/count-numbers-with-unique-digits/](https://leetcode.com/problems/count-numbers-with-unique-digits/) · `O(n) / O(n)`

**Algorithm:**
1. Identify mathematical pattern: GCD, modular arithmetic, or number theory.
2. Apply relevant formula or recurrence.
3. Handle overflow using long or modular arithmetic.
4. Return computed value.
`O(n) / O(n)`

```java
class Solution {
    public int countNumbersWithUniqueDigits(int n) {
        if(n==0)
        return 1;
        int result =10;    //10
        int dp[] = new int[n];
        dp[0] = 9;      // 0
        for (int i=1; i<n; i++) {
            dp[i] = dp[i-1]*(10-i);
            result +=dp[i];
        }
        return result;
    }
}
```

- Use two variables instead of array if only last 1-2 states needed — O(1) space.

#### #365. Water and Jug Problem

[leetcode.com/problems/water-and-jug-problem/](https://leetcode.com/problems/water-and-jug-problem/) · `O(log n) / O(1)`

**Algorithm:**
1. Identify mathematical pattern: GCD, modular arithmetic, or number theory.
2. Apply relevant formula or recurrence.
3. Handle overflow using long or modular arithmetic.
4. Return computed value.
`O(log n) / O(1)`

```java
class Solution {
    public boolean canMeasureWater(int x, int y, int z) {
        Deque<int[]> stack = new LinkedList<>();
        stack.push(new int[]{0, 0});
        Set<Long> seen = new HashSet<>();
        while (!stack.isEmpty()) {
            if (seen.contains(hash(stack.peek()))) {
                stack.pop();
                continue;
            }
            seen.add(hash(stack.peek()));
            int[] state = stack.pop();
            int remain_x = state[0], remain_y = state[1];
            if (remain_x == z || remain_y == z || remain_x + remain_y == z) {
                return true;
            }
            //  X
            stack.push(new int[]{x, remain_y});
            //  Y
            stack.push(new int[]{remain_x, y});
            //  X
            stack.push(new int[]{0, remain_y});
            //  Y
            stack.push(new int[]{remain_x, 0});
            //  X  Y
            stack.push(new int[]{remain_x - Math.min(remain_x, y - remain_y), remain_y + Math.min(remain_x, y - remain_y)});
            //  Y  X
            stack.push(new int[]{remain_x + Math.min(remain_y, x - remain_x), remain_y - Math.min(remain_y, x - remain_x)});
        }
        return false;
    }
    public long hash(int[] state) {
        return (long) state[0] * 1000001 + state[1];
    }
}
class Solution {
    public boolean canMeasureWater(int jug1Capacity, int jug2Capacity, int targetCapacity) {
        if(targetCapacity > jug1Capacity + jug2Capacity) {
            return false;
        }
        return targetCapacity % gcd(jug1Capacity, jug2Capacity) == 0;
    }
    private int gcd(int a, int b) {
        return b == 0 ? a : gcd(b, a % b);
    }
}
```

- Use long arithmetic and take modulo after each multiplication to prevent overflow.
- Cache GCD computations when called repeatedly on overlapping pairs.

---

#### #367. Valid Perfect Square

[leetcode.com/problems/valid-perfect-square/](https://leetcode.com/problems/valid-perfect-square/) · `O(log n) / O(1)`

**Algorithm:**
1. Set left=0, right=n-1 (or on answer space).
2. Compute mid = left + (right-left)/2 to avoid overflow.
3. If nums[mid] satisfies condition: lock bound (shrink toward answer).
4. Else: eliminate that half.
5. Return left (or right) after loop.
`O(log n) / O(1)`

```java
class Solution {
    public boolean isPerfectSquare(int num) {
        int left = 0, right = num;
        while(left <= right) {
            int mid = left + (right - left) / 2;
            long square = (long) mid * mid;
            if(square == num) {
                return true;
            } else if(square > num) {
                right = mid - 1;
            } else {
                left = mid + 1;
            }
        }
        return false;
    }
}
```

- Use long arithmetic and take modulo after each multiplication to prevent overflow.
- Cache GCD computations when called repeatedly on overlapping pairs.

#### #598. Range Addition II

[leetcode.com/problems/range-addition-ii/](https://leetcode.com/problems/range-addition-ii/) · `O(log n) / O(1)`

**Q:** Count cells in the intersection of all rectangles defined by range addition operations.

**Example:** `ops = [[2,2],[3,3]], m = 3, n = 3` → `4`

**Algorithm:**
1. Identify mathematical pattern: GCD, modular arithmetic, or number theory.
2. Apply relevant formula or recurrence.
3. Handle overflow using long or modular arithmetic.
4. Return computed value.
`O(log n) / O(1)`

```java
class Solution {
    //  [0, 0]
    //
    public int maxCount(int m, int n, int[][] ops) {
        int mina = m, minb = n;
        for (int[] op : ops) {
            mina = Math.min(mina, op[0]);
            minb = Math.min(minb, op[1]);
        }
        return mina * minb;
    }
}
```

- Use 1D rolling array if dp[i] only depends on previous row — reduces space from O(mn) to O(n).

#### #372. Super Pow

[leetcode.com/problems/super-pow/](https://leetcode.com/problems/super-pow/) · `O(log n) / O(1)`

**Algorithm:**
1. Fast exponentiation: if exp odd → multiply result by base.
2. Square base and halve exponent each iteration.
3. Apply modulo at each step if needed.
`O(log n) / O(1)`

```java
class Solution {
    static final int MOD = 1337;
    public int superPow(int a, int[] b) {
        int result = 1;
        for (int i = b.length - 1; i >= 0; --i) {
            result = (int) ((long) result * pow(a, b[i]) % MOD);
            a = pow(a, 10);
        }
        return result;
    }
    public int pow(int x, int n) {
        int result = 1;
        while (n != 0) {
            if (n % 2 != 0) {
                result = (int) ((long) result * x % MOD);
            }
            x = (int) ((long) x * x % MOD);
            n /= 2;
        }
        return result;
    }
}
```

- Use long arithmetic and take modulo after each multiplication to prevent overflow.
- Cache GCD computations when called repeatedly on overlapping pairs.

#### #397. Integer Replacement

[leetcode.com/problems/integer-replacement/](https://leetcode.com/problems/integer-replacement/) · `O(log n) / O(1)`

**Q:** Find the minimum steps to reduce a number to 1 (even: n/2, odd: n+1 or n-1).

**Example:** `n = 8` → `3`

**Algorithm:**
1. Identify mathematical pattern: GCD, modular arithmetic, or number theory.
2. Apply relevant formula or recurrence.
3. Handle overflow using long or modular arithmetic.
4. Return computed value.
`O(log n) / O(1)`

```java
class Solution {
    public int integerReplacement(int n) {
        if (n == 1) {
            return 0;
        }
        if (n % 2 == 0) {
            return 1 + integerReplacement(n / 2);
        }
        return 2 + Math.min(integerReplacement(n / 2), integerReplacement(n / 2 + 1));
    }
}
class Solution {
    Map<Integer, Integer> memo = new HashMap<>();
    public int integerReplacement(int n) {
        if (n == 1) {
            return 0;
        }
        if (!memo.containsKey(n)) {
            if (n % 2 == 0) {
                memo.put(n, 1 + integerReplacement(n / 2));
            } else {
                memo.put(n, 2 + Math.min(integerReplacement(n / 2), integerReplacement(n / 2 + 1)));
            }
        }
        return memo.get(n);
    }
}
class Solution {
    public int integerReplacement(int n) {
        if (n == 1) return 0;
        Map<Long, Integer> map = new HashMap<>();
        Deque<Long> d = new ArrayDeque<>();
        d.addLast(n * 1L);
        map.put(n * 1L, 0);
        while (!d.isEmpty()) {
            long t = d.pollFirst();
            int step = map.get(t);
            long[] ns = t % 2 == 0 ? new long[]{t / 2} : new long[]{t + 1, t - 1};
            for (long x : ns) {
                if (x == 1) return step + 1;
                if (!map.containsKey(x)) {
                    map.put(x, step + 1);
                    d.addLast(x);
                }
            }
        }
        return -1;
    }
}
class Solution {
    public int integerReplacement(int _n) {
        long n = _n;
        int result = 0;
        while (n != 1) {
            if (n % 2 == 0) {
                n >>= 1;
            } else {
                if (n != 3 && ((n >> 1) & 1) == 1) n++;
                else n--;
            }
            result++;
        }
        return result;
    }
}
```

- Return early on first HashMap hit rather than scanning full input.

#### #398. Random Pick Index

[leetcode.com/problems/random-pick-index/](https://leetcode.com/problems/random-pick-index/) · `O(log n) / O(1)`

**Q:** Pick a random index from those matching a target value (unknown total size).

**Algorithm:**
1. Identify mathematical pattern: GCD, modular arithmetic, or number theory.
2. Apply relevant formula or recurrence.
3. Handle overflow using long or modular arithmetic.
4. Return computed value.
`O(log n) / O(1)`

```java
class Solution {
    int[] nums;
    Random r;
    public Solution(int[] nums) {
        this.nums = nums;
        r = new Random();
    }
    public int pick(int target) {
        int index = -1;
        int count = 0;
        for(int i = 0; i < nums.length; i++) {
            if(nums[i] == target) {
                count++;
                if(r.nextInt(count) == 0) {
                    index = i;
                }
            }
        }
        return index;
    }
}
```

- Use long arithmetic and take modulo after each multiplication to prevent overflow.
- Cache GCD computations when called repeatedly on overlapping pairs.

#### #400. Nth Digit

[leetcode.com/problems/nth-digit/](https://leetcode.com/problems/nth-digit/) · `O(log n) / O(1)`

**Example:** `n = 11` → `0`

**Algorithm:**
1. Identify mathematical pattern: GCD, modular arithmetic, or number theory.
2. Apply relevant formula or recurrence.
3. Handle overflow using long or modular arithmetic.
4. Return computed value.
`O(log n) / O(1)`

```java
???
class Solution {
    public int findNthDigit(int n) {
        int d = 1, count = 9;
        while (n > (long) d * count) {
            n -= d * count;
            d++;
            count *= 10;
        }
        int index = n - 1;
        int start = (int) Math.pow(10, d - 1);
        int num = start + index / d;
        int digitIndex = index % d;
        int digit = (num / (int)(Math.pow(10, d - digitIndex - 1))) % 10;
        return digit;
    }
}
```

- Use long arithmetic and take modulo after each multiplication to prevent overflow.
- Cache GCD computations when called repeatedly on overlapping pairs.

#### #412. Fizz Buzz

[leetcode.com/problems/fizz-buzz/](https://leetcode.com/problems/fizz-buzz/) · `O(log n) / O(1)`

**Q:** Print numbers 1–n, replacing multiples of 3 with 'Fizz', 5 with 'Buzz', both with 'FizzBuzz'.

**Example:** `n = 15` → `["1","2","Fizz","4","Buzz","Fizz","7","8","Fizz","Buzz","11","Fizz","13","14","FizzBuzz"]`

**Algorithm:**
1. Identify mathematical pattern: GCD, modular arithmetic, or number theory.
2. Apply relevant formula or recurrence.
3. Handle overflow using long or modular arithmetic.
4. Return computed value.
`O(log n) / O(1)`

```java
class Solution {
    public List<String> fizzBuzz(int n) {
        List<String> answer = new ArrayList<>();
        for (int i = 1; i <= n; i++) {
            StringBuffer sb = new StringBuffer();
            if (i % 3 == 0) {
                sb.append("Fizz");
            }
            if (i % 5 == 0) {
                sb.append("Buzz");
            }
            if (sb.length() == 0) {
                sb.append(i);
            }
            answer.add(sb.toString());
        }
        return answer;
    }
}
```

- Use long arithmetic and take modulo after each multiplication to prevent overflow.
- Cache GCD computations when called repeatedly on overlapping pairs.

#### #441. Arranging Coins

[leetcode.com/problems/arranging-coins/](https://leetcode.com/problems/arranging-coins/) · `O(log n) / O(1)`

**Example:** `n = 5` → `2`

**Algorithm:**
1. Set left=0, right=n-1 (or on answer space).
2. Compute mid = left + (right-left)/2 to avoid overflow.
3. If nums[mid] satisfies condition: lock bound (shrink toward answer).
4. Else: eliminate that half.
5. Return left (or right) after loop.
`O(log n) / O(1)`

```java
// cumulative count, cumulative sum
//
class Solution {
    public int arrangeCoins(int n) {
        int count = 0;
        for(int row = 1; n > 0; row++) {
            if(n >= row) {
                count++;
                n -= row;
            } else {
                return count;
            }
        }
        return count;
    }
}
class Solution {
    public int arrangeCoins(int n) {
        int left = 1, right = n;
        while (left < right) {
            //
            int mid = (right - left + 1) / 2 + left;
            if ((long) mid * (mid + 1) <= (long) 2 * n) {
                left = mid;
            } else {
                right = mid - 1;
            }
        }
        return left;
    }
}
class Solution {
    public int arrangeCoins(int n) {
        //
        return (int)((Math.sqrt(1 + 8.0 * n) - 1) / 2);
    }
}
```

- Use long arithmetic and take modulo after each multiplication to prevent overflow.
- Cache GCD computations when called repeatedly on overlapping pairs.

#### #447. 回旋镖的数量

[leetcode.com/problems//](https://leetcode.com/problems//) · `O(log n) / O(1)`

**Example:** `n = 4, pairs = [[1,2],[2,3],[3,4],[4,1],[1,3],[2,4]]` → `8`

**Algorithm:**
1. Identify mathematical pattern: GCD, modular arithmetic, or number theory.
2. Apply relevant formula or recurrence.
3. Handle overflow using long or modular arithmetic.
4. Return computed value.
`O(log n) / O(1)`

```java
class Solution {
    public int numberOfBoomerangs(int[][] points) {
        int result = 0;
        for (int[] p : points) {
            Map<Integer, Integer> count = new HashMap<>();
            for (int[] q : points) {
                int dis = (p[0] - q[0]) * (p[0] - q[0]) + (p[1] - q[1]) * (p[1] - q[1]);
                count.put(dis, count.getOrDefault(dis, 0) + 1);
            }
            for (Map.Entry<Integer, Integer> entry : count.entrySet()) {
                int m = entry.getValue();
                result += m * (m - 1);
            }
        }
        return result;
    }
}
```

- Use 1D rolling array if dp[i] only depends on previous row — reduces space from O(mn) to O(n).
- Return early on first HashMap hit rather than scanning full input.

#### #453. Minimum Moves to Equal Array Elements

**Algorithm:**
1. Identify mathematical pattern: GCD, modular arithmetic, or number theory.
2. Apply relevant formula or recurrence.
3. Handle overflow using long or modular arithmetic.
4. Return computed value.
`O(log n) / O(1)`

```java
class Solution {
    //
    public int minMoves(int[] nums) {
        int minNum = Arrays.stream(nums).min().getAsInt();
        int result = 0;
        for (int num : nums) {
            result += num - minNum;
        }
        return result;
    }
}
```

#### #458. Poor Pigs

[leetcode.com/problems/poor-pigs/](https://leetcode.com/problems/poor-pigs/) · `O(log n) / O(1)`

**Algorithm:**
1. Identify mathematical pattern: GCD, modular arithmetic, or number theory.
2. Apply relevant formula or recurrence.
3. Handle overflow using long or modular arithmetic.
4. Return computed value.
`O(log n) / O(1)`

```java
class Solution {
    public int poorPigs(int buckets, int minutesToDie, int minutesToTest) {
        // pow(base, ans) ≥ bucketsans ≥ log(buckets) / log(base) ans  ans = ceil(log(buckets) / log(base))
        int times = minutesToTest / minutesToDie;
        int base = times + 1;
        // base ^ ans >= buckets
        // ans >= log(buckets) / log(base)
        double temp = Math.log(buckets) / Math.log(base);
        int result = (int)Math.ceil(temp);
        return result;
    }
}
```

- Use long arithmetic and take modulo after each multiplication to prevent overflow.
- Cache GCD computations when called repeatedly on overlapping pairs.

#### #470. Implement Rand10() Using Rand7()

[leetcode.com/problems/implement-rand10-using-rand7/](https://leetcode.com/problems/implement-rand10-using-rand7/) · `O(log n) / O(1)`

**Q:** Implement rand10() using only rand7().

**Algorithm:**
1. Identify mathematical pattern: GCD, modular arithmetic, or number theory.
2. Apply relevant formula or recurrence.
3. Handle overflow using long or modular arithmetic.
4. Return computed value.
`O(log n) / O(1)`

```java
class Solution extends SolBase {
    public int rand10() {
        int row, col, index;
        do {
            row = rand7();
            col = rand7();
            index = col + (row - 1) * 7;
        } while (index > 40);
        return 1 + (index - 1) % 10;
    }
}
```

- Use long arithmetic and take modulo after each multiplication to prevent overflow.

#### #492. Construct the Rectangle

[leetcode.com/problems/construct-the-rectangle/](https://leetcode.com/problems/construct-the-rectangle/) · `O(log n) / O(1)`

**Example:** `area = 4` → `[1,4]`

**Algorithm:**
1. Identify mathematical pattern: GCD, modular arithmetic, or number theory.
2. Apply relevant formula or recurrence.
3. Handle overflow using long or modular arithmetic.
4. Return computed value.
`O(log n) / O(1)`

```java
class Solution {
    public int[] constructRectangle(int area) {
        int w = 1, l = area;
        int[] result = new int[2];
        int min = Integer.MAX_VALUE;
        while(w <= l) {
            int diff = l - w;
            if(l * w == area) {
                if(diff < min) {
                    min = diff;
                    result[0] = w;
                    result[1] = l;
                }
                l--;
                w++;
            } else if(l * w > area) {
                l--;
            } else {
                w++;
            }
        }
        return result;
    }
}
class Solution {
    public int[] constructRectangle(int area) {
        int w = (int) Math.sqrt(area);
        while (area % w != 0) {
            --w;
        }
        return new int[]{area / w, w};
    }
}
```

- Use long arithmetic and take modulo after each multiplication to prevent overflow.
- Cache GCD computations when called repeatedly on overlapping pairs.

#### #495. Teemo Attacking

[leetcode.com/problems/teemo-attacking/](https://leetcode.com/problems/teemo-attacking/) · `O(log n) / O(1)`

**Example:** `timeSeries = [1,4], duration = 2` → `4`

**Algorithm:**
1. Identify mathematical pattern: GCD, modular arithmetic, or number theory.
2. Apply relevant formula or recurrence.
3. Handle overflow using long or modular arithmetic.
4. Return computed value.
`O(log n) / O(1)`

```java
class Solution {
    public int findPoisonedDuration(int[] timeSeries, int duration) {
        int count = 0;
        int end = 0;
        for(int i = 0; i < timeSeries.length; i++) {
            if(timeSeries[i] >= end) {
                count += duration;
            } else {
                count += duration - (end - timeSeries[i]);
            }
            end = timeSeries[i] + duration;
        }
        return count;
    }
}
```

- Use long arithmetic and take modulo after each multiplication to prevent overflow.
- Cache GCD computations when called repeatedly on overlapping pairs.

#### #528. Random Pick with Weight

[leetcode.com/problems/random-pick-with-weight/](https://leetcode.com/problems/random-pick-with-weight/) · `O(log n) / O(1)`

**Q:** Randomly pick an index proportional to its weight.

**Algorithm:**
1. Set left=0, right=n-1 (or on answer space).
2. Compute mid = left + (right-left)/2 to avoid overflow.
3. If nums[mid] satisfies condition: lock bound (shrink toward answer).
4. Else: eliminate that half.
5. Return left (or right) after loop.
`O(log n) / O(1)`

```java
class Solution {
    int[] prefix;
    int total = 0;
    Random random = new Random();
    public Solution(int[] w) {
        prefix = new int[w.length];
        prefix[0] = w[0];
        for(int i = 1; i < w.length; i++) {
            prefix[i] += prefix[i-1] + w[i];
        }
        total = prefix[w.length - 1];
    }
    public int pickIndex() {
        return binarySearch(random.nextInt(total) + 1);
    }
    public int binarySearch(int k) {
        int left = 0, right = prefix.length -1;
        int result = right;
        while(left <= right) {
            int mid = left + (right - left) / 2;
            if(k <= prefix[mid]) {
                result = mid;
                right = mid - 1;
            } else {
                left = mid + 1;
            }
        }
        return result;
    }
}
*/398. Random Pick Index
class Solution {
    int[] nums;
    Random random = new Random();
    public Solution(int[] nums) {
        this.nums = nums;
    }
    public int pick(int target) {
        int pick = -1;
        int count = 0;
        for(int i = 0; i < nums.length; i++) {
            if(nums[i] == target) {
                count++;
                if(random.nextInt(count) == 0) {
                    pick = i;
                }
            }
        }
        return pick;
    }
}
```

- Use long arithmetic and take modulo after each multiplication to prevent overflow.

#### #593. Valid Square

[leetcode.com/problems/valid-square/](https://leetcode.com/problems/valid-square/) · `O(log n) / O(1)`

**Example:** `p1 = [0,0], p2 = [1,1], p3 = [1,0], p4 = [0,1]` → `true`

**Algorithm:**
1. Identify mathematical pattern: GCD, modular arithmetic, or number theory.
2. Apply relevant formula or recurrence.
3. Handle overflow using long or modular arithmetic.
4. Return computed value.
`O(log n) / O(1)`

```java
public class Solution {
    public double dist(int[] p1, int[] p2) {
        return (p2[1] - p1[1]) * (p2[1] - p1[1]) + (p2[0] - p1[0]) * (p2[0] - p1[0]);
    }
    public boolean check(int[] p1, int[] p2, int[] p3, int[] p4) {
        return dist(p1,p2) > 0 && dist(p1, p2) == dist(p2, p3) && dist(p2, p3) == dist(p3, p4) && dist(p3, p4) == dist(p4, p1) && dist(p1, p3) == dist(p2, p4);
    }
    public boolean validSquare(int[] p1, int[] p2, int[] p3, int[] p4) {
        int[][] p = {p1,p2,p3,p4};
        return checkAllPermutations(p, 0);
    }
    private boolean checkAllPermutations(int[][] p, int l) {
        if (l == 4) {
            return check(p[0], p[1], p[2], p[3]);
        } else {
            boolean result = false;
            for (int i = l; i < 4; i++) {
                swap(p, l, i);
                result |= checkAllPermutations(p, l + 1);
                swap(p, l, i);
            }
            return result;
        }
    }
    public void swap(int[][] p, int x, int y) {
        int[] temp = p[x];
        p[x] = p[y];
        p[y] = temp;
    }
}
```

- Use 1D rolling array if dp[i] only depends on previous row — reduces space from O(mn) to O(n).

#### #633. Sum of Square Numbers

[leetcode.com/problems/sum-of-square-numbers/](https://leetcode.com/problems/sum-of-square-numbers/) · `O(log n) / O(1)`

**Example:** `c = 5` → `true`

**Algorithm:**
1. Identify pointer strategy: opposite ends or same direction.
2. Move pointers based on comparison with target condition.
3. Track result/count when condition satisfied.
4. Handle duplicates by skipping equal adjacent elements.
`O(log n) / O(1)`

```java
class Solution {
    public boolean judgeSquareSum(int c) {
        long left = 0;
        long right = (long) Math.sqrt(c);
        while (left <= right) {
            long sum = left * left + right * right;
            if (sum == c) {
                return true;
            } else if (sum > c) {
                right--;
            } else {
                left++;
            }
        }
        return false;
    }
}
```

- Use long arithmetic and take modulo after each multiplication to prevent overflow.
- Cache GCD computations when called repeatedly on overlapping pairs.

---

#### #656. Coin Path

[leetcode.com/problems/coin-path/](https://leetcode.com/problems/coin-path/) · `O(n) / O(n)`

**Algorithm:**
1. Identify mathematical pattern: GCD, modular arithmetic, or number theory.
2. Apply relevant formula or recurrence.
3. Handle overflow using long or modular arithmetic.
4. Return computed value.
`O(n) / O(n)`

```java
class Solution {
    private void enQueue(Deque<int[]> queue, int[] val) {
        while(!queue.isEmpty() && queue.getLast()[0] > val[0]) {
            queue.removeLast();
        }
        queue.addLast(val);
    }
    private void deQueue(Deque<int[]> queue, int[] val) {
        if(!queue.isEmpty() && queue.getFirst()[0] == val[0]) {
            queue.removeFirst();
        }
    }
    public List<Integer> cheapestJump(int[] coins, int maxJump) {
        int n = coins.length;
        Deque<int[]> queue = new ArrayDeque<>();
        int[] dp = new int[n];
        Arrays.fill(dp, Integer.MAX_VALUE);
        dp[0] = coins[0];
        int[] path = new int[n];
        enQueue(queue, new int[]{dp[0], 0});
        for(int i = 1; i < n; i++) {
            int[] top = queue.peek();
            if(top == null) {
                continue;
            }
            dp[i] = coins[i] == -1 ? top[0] : top[0] + coins[i];
            if(coins[i] != -1) enQueue(queue, new int[]{dp[i], i});
            path[i] = top[1];
            if(i - maxJump >= 0) {
                deQueue(queue, new int[]{dp[i - maxJump], i - maxJump});
            }
        }
        if(dp[n-1] == Integer.MAX_VALUE) {
            return new ArrayList<>();
        }
        List<Integer> result = new ArrayList<>();
        int current = n - 1;
        while(current != 0) {
            result.add(current + 1);
            current = path[current];
        }
        result.add(1);
        Collections.reverse(result);
        return result;
    }
}
```

- Use two variables instead of array if only last 1-2 states needed — O(1) space.
- Bidirectional BFS from both ends reduces complexity from O(b^d) to O(b^(d/2)).

#### #781. Rabbits in Forest

[leetcode.com/problems/rabbits-in-forest/](https://leetcode.com/problems/rabbits-in-forest/) · `O(log n) / O(1)`

**Example:** `answers = [1,1,2]` → `5`

**Algorithm:**
1. Identify mathematical pattern: GCD, modular arithmetic, or number theory.
2. Apply relevant formula or recurrence.
3. Handle overflow using long or modular arithmetic.
4. Return computed value.
`O(log n) / O(1)`

```java
class Solution {
    public int numRabbits(int[] answers) {
        //
        if(answers == null || answers.length == 0) {
            return 0;
        }
        Map<Integer, Integer> map = new HashMap<>();
        for(int answer : answers) {
            map.put(answer, map.getOrDefault(answer, 0) + 1);
        }
        int result = 0;
        for (Map.Entry<Integer, Integer> entry : map.entrySet()) {
            //  x  y
            int y = entry.getKey(), x = entry.getValue();
            result += (x + y) / (y + 1) * (y + 1);
        }
        return result;
    }
}
```

- Return early on first HashMap hit rather than scanning full input.

#### #789. Escape The Ghosts

[leetcode.com/problems/escape-the-ghosts/](https://leetcode.com/problems/escape-the-ghosts/) · `O(log n) / O(1)`

**Example:** `sequence = "RGBRGBERGBR", pattern = "RGB"` → `0`

**Algorithm:**
1. Identify mathematical pattern: GCD, modular arithmetic, or number theory.
2. Apply relevant formula or recurrence.
3. Handle overflow using long or modular arithmetic.
4. Return computed value.
`O(log n) / O(1)`

```java
class Solution {
    public boolean escapeGhosts(int[][] ghosts, int[] target) {
        //
        int[] source = new int[] {0, 0};
        int playerDistance = distance(source, target);
        for (int[] ghost : ghosts) {
            if (distance(ghost, target) <= playerDistance) {
                return false;
            }
        }
        return true;
    }
    private int distance(int[] p1, int[] p2) {
        return Math.abs(p1[0] - p2[0]) + Math.abs(p1[1] - p2[1]);
    }
}
```

- Use 1D rolling array if dp[i] only depends on previous row — reduces space from O(mn) to O(n).

#### #792. Number of Matching Subsequences

[leetcode.com/problems/number-of-matching-subsequences/](https://leetcode.com/problems/number-of-matching-subsequences/) · `O(log n) / O(1)`

**Example:** `words = ["amazon","apple","facebook","google","leetcode"], searchWord = "e"` → `6`

**Algorithm:**
1. Identify mathematical pattern: GCD, modular arithmetic, or number theory.
2. Apply relevant formula or recurrence.
3. Handle overflow using long or modular arithmetic.
4. Return computed value.
`O(log n) / O(1)`

```java
class Solution {
    class Node{
        String word;
        int index;
        public Node(String word, int index) {
            this.word = word;
            this.index = index;
        }
    }
    public int numMatchingSubseq(String s, String[] words) {
        int result = 0;
        //
        //  26  heads  S  1
        List<Node>[] buckets = new List[26];
        for(int i = 0; i < 26; i++) {
            buckets[i] = new ArrayList<>();
        }
        for(String word : words) {
            buckets[word.charAt(0) - 'a'].add(new Node(word, 0));
        }
        for(char ch : s.toCharArray()) {
            List<Node> oldBucket = buckets[ch - 'a'];
            buckets[ch - 'a'] = new ArrayList<>();
            for(Node node : oldBucket) {
                node.index++;
                if(node.index == node.word.length()) {
                    result++;
                } else {
                    buckets[node.word.charAt(node.index) - 'a'].add(node);
                }
            }
        }
        return result;
    }
}
```

- Use long arithmetic and take modulo after each multiplication to prevent overflow.
- Cache GCD computations when called repeatedly on overlapping pairs.

#### #829. Consecutive Numbers Sum

[leetcode.com/problems/consecutive-numbers-sum/](https://leetcode.com/problems/consecutive-numbers-sum/) · `O(log n) / O(1)`

**Q:** Find the number of ways to write n as a sum of consecutive positive integers.

**Example:** `n = 5` → `[[1,2,3,4,5],[4,5,6],[7,8,9,10],[2,3,4,5],[5,6,7],[9,10,11],[1,5]]... (subsets)`

**Algorithm:**
1. Identify mathematical pattern: GCD, modular arithmetic, or number theory.
2. Apply relevant formula or recurrence.
3. Handle overflow using long or modular arithmetic.
4. Return computed value.
`O(log n) / O(1)`

```java
// (first + last) * (first - last + 1) / 2 = n
// first * first + first - last * last + last = 2 * n
class Solution {
    public int consecutiveNumbersSum(int N) {
        // 2N = k(2x + k + 1)
        int result = 0;
        for (int k = 1; k <= Math.sqrt(2 * N); ++k)
        if (2 * N % k == 0) {
            int y = 2 * N / k - k - 1;
            if (y % 2 == 0 && y >= 0)
            result++;
        }
        return result;
    }
}
```

- Use long arithmetic and take modulo after each multiplication to prevent overflow.
- Cache GCD computations when called repeatedly on overlapping pairs.

#### #836. Rectangle Overlap

[leetcode.com/problems/rectangle-overlap/](https://leetcode.com/problems/rectangle-overlap/) · `O(log n) / O(1)`

**Q:** Check if two axis-aligned rectangles overlap.

**Example:** `rec1 = [0,0,2,2], rec2 = [1,1,3,3]` → `true`

**Algorithm:**
1. Identify mathematical pattern: GCD, modular arithmetic, or number theory.
2. Apply relevant formula or recurrence.
3. Handle overflow using long or modular arithmetic.
4. Return computed value.
`O(log n) / O(1)`

```java
class Solution {
    public boolean isRectangleOverlap(int[] rec1, int[] rec2) {
        int overlapWidth = Math.min(rec1[2], rec2[2]) - Math.max(rec1[0], rec2[0]);
        int overlapHeight = Math.min(rec1[3], rec2[3]) - Math.max(rec1[1], rec2[1]);
        int overlapArea = Math.max(overlapWidth, 0) * Math.max(overlapHeight, 0);
        return overlapArea > 0;
    }
}
```

- Use long arithmetic and take modulo after each multiplication to prevent overflow.
- Cache GCD computations when called repeatedly on overlapping pairs.

#### #858. Mirror Reflection

[leetcode.com/problems/mirror-reflection/](https://leetcode.com/problems/mirror-reflection/) · `O(log n) / O(1)`

**Q:** Given clock hands at h:m, find the minimum angle between them.

**Example:** `mat = [[0,1,0],[0,0,0],[0,0,1]], N = 7` → `[[0,0,0],[0,0,0],[0,0,0]]`

**Algorithm:**
1. Identify mathematical pattern: GCD, modular arithmetic, or number theory.
2. Apply relevant formula or recurrence.
3. Handle overflow using long or modular arithmetic.
4. Return computed value.
`O(log n) / O(1)`

```java
class Solution {
    public int mirrorReflection(int p, int q) {
        int g = gcd(p, q);
        p /= g; p %= 2;
        q /= g; q %= 2;
        if (p == 1 && q == 1) return 1;
        return p == 1 ? 0 : 2;
    }
    public int gcd(int a, int b) {
        if (a == 0) return b;
        return gcd(b % a, a);
    }
}
```

- Use long arithmetic and take modulo after each multiplication to prevent overflow.
- Cache GCD computations when called repeatedly on overlapping pairs.

#### #869. Reordered Power of 2

[leetcode.com/problems/reordered-power-of-2/](https://leetcode.com/problems/reordered-power-of-2/) · `O(2ⁿ) / O(n)`

**Q:** Check if any permutation of n's digits is a power of 2.

**Example:** `n = 10` → `4`

**Algorithm:**
1. Fast exponentiation: if exp odd → multiply result by base.
2. Square base and halve exponent each iteration.
3. Apply modulo at each step if needed.
`O(2ⁿ) / O(n)`

```java
class Solution {
    boolean[] vis;
    public boolean reorderedPowerOf2(int n) {
        char[] nums = Integer.toString(n).toCharArray();
        Arrays.sort(nums);
        vis = new boolean[nums.length];
        return backtrack(nums, 0, 0);
    }
    public boolean backtrack(char[] nums, int index, int num) {
        if (index == nums.length) {
            return isPowerOfTwo(num);
        }
        for (int i = 0; i < nums.length; ++i) {
            //
            if ((num == 0 && nums[i] == '0') || vis[i] || (i > 0 && !vis[i - 1] && nums[i] == nums[i - 1])) {
                continue;
            }
            vis[i] = true;
            if (backtrack(nums, index + 1, num * 10 + nums[i] - '0')) {
                return true;
            }
            vis[i] = false;
        }
        return false;
    }
    public boolean isPowerOfTwo(int n) {
        //  1  0
        return (n & (n - 1)) == 0;
    }
}
```

- Sort input first to enable early termination: break when remaining sum impossible.
- Prune: if candidates[i] > remaining target, break (array is sorted).
- After sorting, use binary search (O(log n)) instead of linear scan for lookups.

#### #939. Minimum Area Rectangle

[leetcode.com/problems/minimum-area-rectangle/](https://leetcode.com/problems/minimum-area-rectangle/) · `O(log n) / O(1)`

**Q:** Find the minimum area rectangle from a set of points in an axis-aligned grid.

**Example:** `points = [[1,2],[2,2],[3,2],[2,3]]` → `1`

**Algorithm:**
1. Identify mathematical pattern: GCD, modular arithmetic, or number theory.
2. Apply relevant formula or recurrence.
3. Handle overflow using long or modular arithmetic.
4. Return computed value.
`O(log n) / O(1)`

```java
class Solution {
    public int minAreaRect(int[][] points) {
        if(points == null || points.length == 0 || points[0].length == 0) {
            return 0;
        }
        // x -> set of y
        HashMap<Integer, HashSet<Integer>> hashmap = new HashMap<>();
        for(int[] point: points) {
            int x = point[0];
            int y = point[1];
            hashmap.putIfAbsent(x, new HashSet<>());
            hashmap.get(x).add(y);
        }
        int result = Integer.MAX_VALUE;
        for(int i = 0; i < points.length; i++) {
            for(int j = i + 1; j < points.length; j++) {
                int x1 = points[i][0];
                int y1 = points[i][1];
                int x2 = points[j][0];
                int y2 = points[j][1];
                // area0
                if(x1 == x2 || y1 == y2) {
                    continue;
                }
                if(hashmap.get(x1).contains(y2) && hashmap.get(x2).contains(y1)) {
                    result = Math.min(result, Math.abs(x2 - x1) * Math.abs(y2 - y1));
                }
            }
        }
        return result == Integer.MAX_VALUE ? 0 : result;
    }
}
```

- Use 1D rolling array if dp[i] only depends on previous row — reduces space from O(mn) to O(n).

---

#### #963. Minimum Area Rectangle II

[leetcode.com/problems/minimum-area-rectangle-ii/](https://leetcode.com/problems/minimum-area-rectangle-ii/) · `O(log n) / O(1)`

**Q:** Find the minimum area rectangle aligned to any angle from a set of points.

**Example:** `points = [[0,2],[2,1],[2,3],[4,3]]` → `0.56569`

**Algorithm:**
1. Identify mathematical pattern: GCD, modular arithmetic, or number theory.
2. Apply relevant formula or recurrence.
3. Handle overflow using long or modular arithmetic.
4. Return computed value.
`O(log n) / O(1)`

```java
class Solution {
    //
    // hash in[]
    public double minAreaFreeRect(int[][] points) {
        if (points.length < 4) {
            return 0;
        }
        Set<String> set = new HashSet<>();
        for (int i = 0; i < points.length; i++) {
            set.add(hash().apply(points[i]));
        }
        double area = 0;
        for (int i = 0; i < points.length; i++) {
            for (int j = i + 1; j < points.length; j++) {
                int d2ij = distance2(points[i], points[j]);
                for (int k = j + 1; k < points.length; k++) {
                    int d2ik = distance2(points[i], points[k]);
                    int d2jk = distance2(points[j], points[k]);
                    if (d2ij + d2ik != d2jk) {
                        continue;
                    }
                    int x = points[j][0] + points[k][0] - points[i][0];
                    int y = points[j][1] + points[k][1] - points[i][1];
                    int[] pointXY = new int[]{x, y};
                    if (!set.contains(hash().apply(pointXY))) {
                        continue;
                    }
                    double tmpArea = Math.sqrt(d2ij) * Math.sqrt(d2ik);
                    if (area == 0 || area > tmpArea) {
                        area = tmpArea;
                    }
                }
            }
        }
        return area;
    }
    private Function<int[], String> hash() {
        return point -> String.format("%d_%d", point[0], point[1]);
    }
    private int distance2(int[] a, int[] b) {
        return (b[0] - a[0]) * (b[0] - a[0]) + (b[1] - a[1]) * (b[1] - a[1]);
    }
}
```

- Use 1D rolling array if dp[i] only depends on previous row — reduces space from O(mn) to O(n).

---

#### #1041. Robot Bounded In Circle

[leetcode.com/problems/robot-bounded-in-circle/](https://leetcode.com/problems/robot-bounded-in-circle/) · `O(log n) / O(1)`

**Q:** Determine if a robot following instructions stays within a bounded circle.

**Algorithm:**
1. Identify mathematical pattern: GCD, modular arithmetic, or number theory.
2. Apply relevant formula or recurrence.
3. Handle overflow using long or modular arithmetic.
4. Return computed value.
`O(log n) / O(1)`

```java
class Solution {
    public boolean isRobotBounded(String instructions) {
        // (x,y)
        int[][] directions = new int[][]{{0, 1}, {1, 0}, {0, -1}, {-1, 0}};
        int[] pos = new int[]{0, 0};
        int direction = 0;;
        for(char ch : instructions.toCharArray()) {
            if(ch == 'G') {
                pos[0] += directions[direction][0];
                pos[1] += directions[direction][1];
            } else if(ch == 'L') {
                direction = direction - 1 < 0 ? 3 : direction -1;
            } else if(ch == 'R') {
                direction = direction + 1 >= 4 ? 0 : direction + 1;
            }
        }
        return (pos[0] == 0 && pos[1] == 0) || direction != 0;
    }
}
```

- Use 1D rolling array if dp[i] only depends on previous row — reduces space from O(mn) to O(n).

#### #1160. Find Words That Can Be Formed by Characters

[leetcode.com/problems/find-words-that-can-be-formed-by-characters/](https://leetcode.com/problems/find-words-that-can-be-formed-by-characters/) · `O(log n) / O(1)`

**Algorithm:**
1. Identify mathematical pattern: GCD, modular arithmetic, or number theory.
2. Apply relevant formula or recurrence.
3. Handle overflow using long or modular arithmetic.
4. Return computed value.
`O(log n) / O(1)`

```java
class Solution {
    public int countCharacters(String[] words, String chars) {
        if(words == null || chars == null) {
            return 0;
        }
        int count = 0;
        for(String word : words) {
            if(valid(word, chars)) {
                count += word.length();
            }
        }
        return count;
    }
    public boolean valid(String word, String chars) {
        int[] count = new int[26];
        for(char ch : chars.toCharArray()) {
            count[ch - 'a']++;
        }
        for(char ch : word.toCharArray()) {
            count[ch -'a']--;
            if(count[ch -'a'] < 0) {
                return false;
            }
        }
        return true;
    }
}
```

- Use long arithmetic and take modulo after each multiplication to prevent overflow.
- Cache GCD computations when called repeatedly on overlapping pairs.

#### #1304. Find N Unique Integers Sum up to Zero

[leetcode.com/problems/find-n-unique-integers-sum-up-to-zero/](https://leetcode.com/problems/find-n-unique-integers-sum-up-to-zero/) · `O(log n) / O(1)`

**Q:** Find n unique integers that sum to zero.

**Algorithm:**
1. Identify mathematical pattern: GCD, modular arithmetic, or number theory.
2. Apply relevant formula or recurrence.
3. Handle overflow using long or modular arithmetic.
4. Return computed value.
`O(log n) / O(1)`

```java
class Solution {
    // -sum
    public int[] sumZero(int n) {
        int[] result = new int[n];
        int sum = 0;
        for(int i = 0; i < n - 1; i++) {
            result[i] = i + 1;
            sum += i + 1;
        }
        result[n - 1] = -sum;
        return result;
    }
}
```

- Use long arithmetic and take modulo after each multiplication to prevent overflow.
- Cache GCD computations when called repeatedly on overlapping pairs.

#### #1344. Angle Between Hands of a Clock

[leetcode.com/problems/angle-between-hands-of-a-clock/](https://leetcode.com/problems/angle-between-hands-of-a-clock/) · `O(log n) / O(1)`

**Q:** Calculate the angle between clock hands at a given time.

**Algorithm:**
1. Identify mathematical pattern: GCD, modular arithmetic, or number theory.
2. Apply relevant formula or recurrence.
3. Handle overflow using long or modular arithmetic.
4. Return computed value.
`O(log n) / O(1)`

```java
class Solution {
    public double angleClock(int hour, int minutes) {
        int oneMinAngle = 6;
        int oneHourAngle = 30;
        double minutesAngle = oneMinAngle * minutes;
        double hoursAngle = (hour % 12 + minutes / 60.0) * oneHourAngle;
        double diff = Math.abs(hoursAngle - minutesAngle);
        return Math.min(diff, 360 - diff);
    }
}
```

- Use long arithmetic and take modulo after each multiplication to prevent overflow.
- Cache GCD computations when called repeatedly on overlapping pairs.

#### #1583. Count Unhappy Friends

[leetcode.com/problems/count-unhappy-friends/](https://leetcode.com/problems/count-unhappy-friends/) · `O(log n) / O(1)`

**Q:** Count the number of unhappy friends after pairing.

**Algorithm:**
1. Identify mathematical pattern: GCD, modular arithmetic, or number theory.
2. Apply relevant formula or recurrence.
3. Handle overflow using long or modular arithmetic.
4. Return computed value.
`O(log n) / O(1)`

```java
class Solution {
    public int unhappyFriends(int n, int[][] preferences, int[][] pairs) {
        // order[i][j]  j  i
        int[][] order = new int[n][n];
        for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
            for (int j = 0; j < n - 1; j++) {
                order[i][preferences[i][j]] = j;
            }
        }
        //  x, y match[x] = y; match[y] = x;
        int[] match = new int[n];
        for (int[] pair : pairs) {
            int person0 = pair[0], person1 = pair[1];
            match[person0] = person1;
            match[person1] = person0;
        }
        int unhappyCount = 0;
        for (int x = 0; x < n; x++) {
            int y = match[x];
            int index = order[x][y];
            for (int i = 0; i < index; i++) {
                // x prefers u over y
                int u = preferences[x][i];
                int v = match[u];
                // u prefers x over v
                if (order[u][x] < order[u][v]) {
                    unhappyCount++;
                    break;
                }
            }
        }
        return unhappyCount;
    }
}
```

- Use 1D rolling array if dp[i] only depends on previous row — reduces space from O(mn) to O(n).

---

#### #1588. Sum of All Odd Length Subarrays

[leetcode.com/problems/sum-of-all-odd-length-subarrays/](https://leetcode.com/problems/sum-of-all-odd-length-subarrays/) · `O(log n) / O(1)`

**Q:** Compute the sum of all odd-length subarray sums.

**Algorithm:**
1. Identify mathematical pattern: GCD, modular arithmetic, or number theory.
2. Apply relevant formula or recurrence.
3. Handle overflow using long or modular arithmetic.
4. Return computed value.
`O(log n) / O(1)`

```java
class Solution {
    public int sumOddLengthSubarrays(int[] arr) {
        int n = arr.length;
        int[] prefixSums = new int[n + 1];
        for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
            prefixSums[i + 1] = prefixSums[i] + arr[i];
        }
        int sum = 0;
        for (int start = 0; start < n; start++) {
            for (int length = 1; start + length <= n; length += 2) {
                int end = start + length - 1;
                sum += prefixSums[end + 1] - prefixSums[start];
            }
        }
        return sum;
    }
}
class Solution {
    //
    public int sumOddLengthSubarrays(int[] arr) {
        int sum = 0;
        int n = arr.length;
        for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
            int leftCount = i, rightCount = n - i - 1;
            int leftOdd = (leftCount + 1) / 2;
            int rightOdd = (rightCount + 1) / 2;
            int leftEven = leftCount / 2 + 1;
            int rightEven = rightCount / 2 + 1;
            sum += arr[i] * (leftOdd * rightOdd + leftEven * rightEven);
        }
        return sum;
    }
}
```

- Use long arithmetic and take modulo after each multiplication to prevent overflow.
- Cache GCD computations when called repeatedly on overlapping pairs.

#### #1646. Get Maximum in Generated Array

[leetcode.com/problems/get-maximum-in-generated-array/](https://leetcode.com/problems/get-maximum-in-generated-array/) · `O(log n) / O(1)`

**Q:** Generate an array from a given sequence and return the max element.

**Algorithm:**
1. Identify mathematical pattern: GCD, modular arithmetic, or number theory.
2. Apply relevant formula or recurrence.
3. Handle overflow using long or modular arithmetic.
4. Return computed value.
`O(log n) / O(1)`

```java
class Solution {
    public int getMaximumGenerated(int n) {
        if (n == 0) return 0;
        int[] nums = new int[n + 1];
        nums[0] = 0;
        nums[1] = 1;
        for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
            if (2 * i <= n) nums[2 * i] = nums[i];
            if (2 * i + 1 <= n) nums[2 * i + 1] = nums[i] + nums[i + 1];
        }
        int result = 0;
        return Arrays.stream(nums).max().getAsInt();
    }
}
class Solution {
    public int getMaximumGenerated(int n) {
        if (n == 0) {
            return 0;
        }
        int[] nums = new int[n + 1];
        nums[1] = 1;
        for (int i = 2; i <= n; ++i) {
            nums[i] = nums[i / 2] + i % 2 * nums[i / 2 + 1];
        }
        return Arrays.stream(nums).max().getAsInt();
    }
}
```

- Use long arithmetic and take modulo after each multiplication to prevent overflow.
- Cache GCD computations when called repeatedly on overlapping pairs.

#### #1823. Find the Winner of the Circular Game

[leetcode.com/problems/find-the-winner-of-the-circular-game/](https://leetcode.com/problems/find-the-winner-of-the-circular-game/) · `O(log n) / O(1)`

**Algorithm:**
1. Identify mathematical pattern: GCD, modular arithmetic, or number theory.
2. Apply relevant formula or recurrence.
3. Handle overflow using long or modular arithmetic.
4. Return computed value.
`O(log n) / O(1)`

```java
class Solution {
    //
    public int findTheWinner(int n, int k) {
        if(n == 1) return 1;
        // 1k+12k+2,N-kN
        // k
        //knf(n-1, k)kN
        // NN(0)
        // f(n, k)ansf(n-1, k) + kansNNN1N
        int result = findTheWinner(n - 1, k) + k;
        return result % n == 0? n: (result % n);
    }
}
```

- Use long arithmetic and take modulo after each multiplication to prevent overflow.
- Cache GCD computations when called repeatedly on overlapping pairs.

#### #1969. Minimum Non-Zero Product of the Array Elements

[leetcode.com/problems/minimum-nonzero-product-of-the-array-elements/](https://leetcode.com/problems/minimum-nonzero-product-of-the-array-elements/) · `O(log n) / O(1)`

**Q:** Find the minimum non-zero product of array elements after swaps.

**Algorithm:**
1. Identify mathematical pattern: GCD, modular arithmetic, or number theory.
2. Apply relevant formula or recurrence.
3. Handle overflow using long or modular arithmetic.
4. Return computed value.
`O(log n) / O(1)`

```java
class Solution {
    static final long MOD = 1000000007;
    // xyxyx+y
    // x1
    public int minNonZeroProduct(int p) {
        long a = (1L << p) - 1L;
        long b = a - 1L;
        long c = b / 2L;
        long result = ((a % MOD) * pow(b % MOD, c)) % MOD;
        return (int) result;
    }
    public long pow (long b, long c) {
        long result = 1;
        while (c > 0) {
            if ((c & 1) == 1) {
                result = (result * b) % MOD;
            }
            c >>= 1;
            b = (b * b) % MOD;
        }
        return result;
    }
}
```

- Use long arithmetic and take modulo after each multiplication to prevent overflow.
- Cache GCD computations when called repeatedly on overlapping pairs.

---

### Concurrency (2 problems)

#### #1114. Print in Order

[leetcode.com/problems/print-in-order/](https://leetcode.com/problems/print-in-order/) · `O(1) / O(n)`

**Algorithm:**
1. Use Semaphore(0) to enforce ordering: acquire blocks until another thread releases.
2. Thread A: do work, then sem.release(). Thread B: sem.acquire(), then do work.
3. For mutual exclusion: use ReentrantLock with Condition.await()/signalAll().
`O(1) / O(n)`

```java
class Foo {
    private AtomicInteger firstJobDone = new AtomicInteger(0);
    private AtomicInteger secondJobDone = new AtomicInteger(0);
    public Foo() {}
    public void first(Runnable printFirst) throws InterruptedException {
        // printFirst.run() outputs "first".
        printFirst.run();
        // mark the first job as done, by increasing its count.
        firstJobDone.incrementAndGet();
    }
    public void second(Runnable printSecond) throws InterruptedException {
        while (firstJobDone.get() != 1) {
            // waiting for the first job to be done.
        }
        // printSecond.run() outputs "second".
        printSecond.run();
        // mark the second as done, by increasing its count.
        secondJobDone.incrementAndGet();
    }
    public void third(Runnable printThird) throws InterruptedException {
        while (secondJobDone.get() != 1) {
            // waiting for the second job to be done.
        }
        // printThird.run() outputs "third".
        printThird.run();
    }
}
class Foo {
    private Semaphore two = new Semaphore(0);
    private Semaphore three = new Semaphore(0);
    public Foo() {
    }
    public void first(Runnable printFirst) throws InterruptedException {
        // printFirst.run() outputs "first". Do not change or remove this line.
        printFirst.run();
        two.release();
    }
    public void second(Runnable printSecond) throws InterruptedException {
        two.acquire();
        // printSecond.run() outputs "second". Do not change or remove this line.
        printSecond.run();
        three.release();
    }
    public void third(Runnable printThird) throws InterruptedException {
        three.acquire();
        // printThird.run() outputs "third". Do not change or remove this line.
        printThird.run();
    }
}
```

- Consider edge cases: empty input, single element, all duplicates.
- Profile bottleneck: is it the sort, the inner loop, or the data structure operations?

---

#### #1115. Print FooBar Alternately

[leetcode.com/problems/print-foobar-alternately/](https://leetcode.com/problems/print-foobar-alternately/) · `O(1) / O(n)`

**Algorithm:**
1. Use Semaphore(0) to enforce ordering: acquire blocks until another thread releases.
2. Thread A: do work, then sem.release(). Thread B: sem.acquire(), then do work.
3. For mutual exclusion: use ReentrantLock with Condition.await()/signalAll().
`O(1) / O(n)`

```java
class FooBar {
    private int n;
    private Semaphore foo;
    private Semaphore bar;
    public FooBar(int n) {
        foo = new Semaphore(1);
        bar = new Semaphore(0);
        this.n = n;
    }
    public void foo(Runnable printFoo) throws InterruptedException {
        for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
            foo.acquire();
            // printFoo.run() outputs "foo". Do not change or remove this line.
            printFoo.run();
            bar.release();
        }
    }
    public void bar(Runnable printBar) throws InterruptedException {
        for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
            bar.acquire();
            // printBar.run() outputs "bar". Do not change or remove this line.
            printBar.run();
            foo.release();
        }
    }
}
class ZeroEvenOdd {
    private int n;
    Semaphore s1 = new Semaphore(1), s2 = new Semaphore(0), s3 = new Semaphore(0);
    public ZeroEvenOdd(int n) {
        this.n = n;
    }
    // printNumber.accept(x) outputs "x", where x is an integer.
    public void zero(IntConsumer printNumber) throws InterruptedException {
        for (int i = 0; i < n; ++i) {
            s1.acquire();
            printNumber.accept(0);
            if ((i & 1) == 0) s2.release();
            else s3.release();
        }
    }
    public void odd(IntConsumer printNumber) throws InterruptedException {
        for (int i = 1; i <= n; i += 2) {
            s2.acquire();
            printNumber.accept(i);
            s1.release();
        }
    }
    public void even(IntConsumer printNumber) throws InterruptedException {
        for (int i = 2; i <= n; i += 2) {
            s3.acquire();
            printNumber.accept(i);
            s1.release();
        }
    }
}
Offer 46.
class Solution {
    public int translateNum(int num) {
        String src = String.valueOf(num);
        int p = 0, q = 0, r = 1;
        for (int i = 0; i < src.length(); ++i) {
            p = q;
            q = r;
            r = 0;
            r += q;
            if (i == 0) {
                continue;
            }
            String pre = src.substring(i - 1, i + 1);
            if (pre.compareTo("25") <= 0 && pre.compareTo("10") >= 0) {
                r += p;
            }
        }
        return r;
    }
}
```

- Consider edge cases: empty input, single element, all duplicates.
- Profile bottleneck: is it the sort, the inner loop, or the data structure operations?
